ACMB10H3:  Equity and Diversity in the Arts<p>Course Description</p><p>Equity and diversity in the arts promotes diversity of all kinds, including those of race, gender, socio-economic status, sexual orientation or identity, age, ability or disability, religion, and aesthetics, tradition or practice. This course examines issues of equity and diversity and how they apply across all disciplines of arts, culture and media through critical readings and analysis of cultural policy.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.0 credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (VPAB07H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enroled in Specialist and Major Programs offered by the Department of Arts, Culture &amp; Media. Other students will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ACMB10H3">

ACMD91H3:  Supervised Readings<p>Course Description</p><p>Independent study of an advanced and intensive kind, under the direction of a faculty member. The material studied should bear some significant relation to the student's previous work, and should differ significantly in content and/or concentration from topics offered in other courses.<br>Students are advised that they must obtain consent from the supervising instructor<em> before</em> registering for these courses. The student should submit to the instructor a statement of objectives and proposed content for the course; this should be done by 15 April for 'F' courses and by 1 December for 'S' courses. If the proposal is approved, two faculty members from relevant disciplines will supervise and evaluate the work.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 3.0 credits at the B-level in the Department of Arts, Culture and Media.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (HUMD91H3)</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ACMD91H3">

AFSA01H3:  Africa in the World: An Introduction<p>Course Description</p><p>An interdisciplinary introduction to the history and development of Africa with Africa's place in the wider world a key theme. Students critically engage with African and diasporic histories, cultures, social structures, economies, and belief systems. Course material is drawn from Archaeology, History, Geography, Literature, Film Studies, and Women's Studies.</p> <p>Same as (HISA08H3)</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (HISA08H3), AFR150Y1 (NEW150Y1)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSA01H3">

AFSB50H3:  Africa in the Era of the Slave Trade<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the history of Sub-Saharan Africa, from the era of the slave trade to the colonial conquests. Throughout, the capacity of Africans to overcome major problems will be stressed. Themes include slavery and the slave trade; pre-colonial states and societies; economic and labour systems; and religious change. <br>Same as <a href="/course/HISB50H3">HISB50H3</a></p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISB50H3">HISB50H3</a>, (HISC50H3), HIS295H, HIS396H, (HIS396Y)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: 0.5 credit in a AFS or ANT or CLA or IDS or GEO or GAS or HIS or POL or SOC or WST course<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSB50H3">
AFSB51H3:  Africa from the Colonial Conquests to Independence<p>Course Description</p><p>Modern Sub-Saharan Africa, from the colonial conquests to the end of the colonial era. The emphasis is on both structure and agency in a hostile world. Themes include conquest and resistance; colonial economies; peasants and labour; gender and ethnicity; religious and political movements; development and underdevelopment; Pan-Africanism, nationalism and independence.<br>Same as <a href="/course/HISB51H3">HISB51H3</a></p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISB51H3">HISB51H3and (HISC51H3) and HIS396H and (HIS396Y)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: 0.5 credit in a AFS or ANT or CLA or IDS or GEO or GAS or HIS or POL or SOC or WST course. <a href="/course/AFSB50H3">AFSB50H3</a>/<a href="/course/HISB50H3">HISB50H3strongly recommended<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSB51H3">
AFSB54H3:  Africa in the Postcolonial Era<p>Course Description</p><p>Africa from the 1960s to the present. After independence, Africans experienced great optimism and then the disappointments of unmet expectations, development crises, conflict and AIDS. Yet the continent’s strength is its youth. Topics include African socialism and capitalism; structural adjustment and resource economies; dictatorship and democratization; migration and urbanization; social movements.<br>Same as <a href="/course/HISB54H3">HISB54H3</a></p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISB54H3">HISB54H3</a>, NEW250Y1</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: 0.5 credit in a AFS or ANT or CLA or IDS or GEO or GAS or HIS or POL or SOC or WST course<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSB54H3">
AFSC03H3:  Contemporary Africa: State, Society, and Politics<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is intended as an advanced critical introduction to contemporary African politics. It seeks to examine the nature of power and politics, state and society, war and violence, epistemology and ethics, identity and subjectivities, history and the present from a comparative and historical perspective. It asks what the main drivers of African politics are, and how we account for political organization and change on the continent from a comparative and historical perspective.</p> <p>Same as <a href="/course/IDSC03H3">IDSC03H3</a>.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/IDSA01H3">IDSA01H3or <a href="/course/AFSA01H3">AFSA01H3</a>] or by instructor’s permission<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/IDSC03H3">IDSC03H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSC03H3">
AFSC19H3:  Community-Driven Development: Cooperatives, Social Enterprises and the Black Social Economy<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to alternative business institutions (including cooperatives, credit unions, worker-owned firms, mutual aid, and social enterprises) to challenge development. It investigates the history and theories of the solidarity economy as well as its potential contributions to local, regional and international socio-economic development. There will be strong experiential education aspects in the course to debate issues. Students analyze case studies with attention paid to Africa and its diaspora to combat exclusion through cooperative structures.</p> <p>Same as <a href="/course/IDSC19H3">IDSC19H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/AFSA01H3">AFSA01H3or <a href="/course/IDSA01H3">IDSA01H3or <a href="/course/POLB90H3">POLB90H3or permission of the instructor<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/IDSC19H3">IDSC19H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSC19H3">
AFSC52H3:  Ethiopia: Seeing History<p>Course Description</p><p>This course uses a focus on material history and visual culture to explore Ethiopia from the fourth through the nineteenth century, with particular emphasis on the Christian Church, the monarchy, links with both the Mediterranean world and the Indian subcontinent, and the relationship of individuals to their social, economic, artistic and geographic environments.<br>Same as <a href="/course/HISC52H3">HISC52H3and <a href="/course/VPHC52H3">VPHC52H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [1.0 credit in History] or [<a href="/course/VPHA46H3">VPHA46H3and an additional 1.0 credit in VPH courses]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISC52H3">HISC52H3</a>, <a href="/course/VPHC52H3">VPHC52H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSC52H3">
AFSC55H3:  War and Society in Modern Africa<p>Course Description</p><p>Conflict and social change in Africa from the slave trade to contemporary times. Topics include the politics of resistance, women and war, repressive and weak states, the Cold War, guerrilla movements, resource predation. Case studies of anti-colonial rebellions, liberation wars, and civil conflicts will be chosen from various regions.<br>Same as <a href="/course/HISC55H3">HISC55H3</a><br></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.0 credits, including: <a href="/course/AFSB50H3">AFSB50H3</a>/<a href="/course/HISB50H3">HISB50H3or <a href="/course/AFSB51H3">AFSB51H3</a>/<a href="/course/HISB51H3">HISB51H3or (HISC50H3) or (HISC51H3)<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISC55H3">HISC55H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSC55H3">
AFSC70H3:  The Caribbean Diaspora<p>Course Description</p><p>The migration of Caribbean peoples to the United States, Canada, and Europe from the late 19th century to the present. The course considers how shifting economic circumstances and labour demands, the World Wards, evolving imperial relationships, pan-Africanism and international unionism, decolonization, natural disasters, and globalization shaped this migration.<br> Same as <a href="/course/HISC70H3">HISC70H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.0 credits, including 0.5 credit at the A- or B-level in HIS courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: NEW428H,<a href="/course/HISC70H3">HISC70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSC70H3">
AFSD07H3:  Extractive Industries in Africa<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines resource extraction in African history. We examine global trade networks in precolonial Africa, and the transformations brought by colonial extractive economies. Case studies, from diamonds to uranium, demonstrate how the resource curse has affected states and economies, especially in the postcolonial period.<br><br>Same as <a href="/course/IDSD07H3">IDSD07H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [10.0 credits including [<a href="/course/AFSA01H3">AFSA01H3or <a href="/course/IDSA01H3">IDSA01H3or <a href="/course/POLB90H3">POLB90H3</a>]] or permission of instructor<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/IDSD07H3">IDSD07H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSD07H3">
AFSD16H3:  Africana Political Economy in Comparative Perspective<p>Course Description</p><p>This course analyzes racial capitalism among persons of African descent in the Global South and Global North with a focus on diaspora communities. Students learn about models for self-determination, solidarity economies and cooperativism as well as Black political economy theory.<br><br>Same as <a href="/course/IDSD16H3">IDSD16H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [10.0 credits including [<a href="/course/AFSA01H3">AFSA01H3or <a href="/course/IDSA01H3">IDSA01H3or <a href="/course/POLB90H3">POLB90H3</a>]] or permission of instructor<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/IDSD16H3">IDSD16H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSD16H3">
AFSD20H3:  Thinking Conflict, Security, and Development<p>Course Description</p><p>This course offers an advanced critical introduction to the security-development nexus and the political economy of conflict, security, and development. It explores the major issues in contemporary conflicts, the securitization of development, the transformation of the security and development landscapes, and the broader implications they have for peace and development in the Global South.</p> <p>Same as <a href="/course/IDSD20H3">IDSD20H3</a>.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [12.0 including (<a href="/course/IDSA01H3">IDSA01H3or <a href="/course/AFSA01H3">AFSA01H3or <a href="/course/POLC09H3">POLC09H3</a>)] or by instructor’s permission<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <div><a href="/course/IDSD20H3">IDSD20H3</a></div><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSD20H3">
AFSD51H3:  Southern Africa: Colonial Rule, Apartheid and Liberation<p>Course Description</p><p>A seminar study of southern African history from 1900 to the present. Students will consider industrialization in South Africa, segregation, apartheid, colonial rule, liberation movements, and the impact of the Cold War. Historiography and questions of race, class and gender will be important. Extensive reading and student presentations are required.<br>Same as <a href="/course/HISD51H3">HISD51H3</a><br>Africa and Asia Area</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits including <a href="/course/HISB50H3">HISB50H3</a>/<a href="/course/AFSB50H3">AFSB50H3or <a href="/course/HISB51H3">HISB51H3</a>/<a href="/course/AFSB51H3">AFSB51H3or <a href="/course/HISB54H3">HISB54H3</a>/<a href="/course/AFSB54H3">AFSB54H3or <a href="/course/HISC55H3">HISC55H3</a>/<a href="/course/AFSC55H3">AFSC55H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISD51H3">HISD51H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSD51H3">
AFSD52H3:  East African Societies in Transition<p>Course Description</p><p>A seminar study of East African peoples from late pre-colonial times to the 1990's, emphasizing their rapid although uneven adaptation to integration of the region into the wider world. Transitions associated with migrations, commercialization, religious change, colonial conquest, nationalism, economic development and conflict, will be investigated. Student presentations are required.<br> Same as <a href="/course/HISD52H3">HISD52H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits including <a href="/course/HISB50H3">HISB50H3</a>/<a href="/course/AFSB50H3">AFSB50H3or <a href="/course/HISB51H3">HISB51H3</a>/<a href="/course/AFSB51H3">AFSB51H3or <a href="/course/HISB54H3">HISB54H3</a>/<a href="/course/AFSB54H3">AFSB54H3or <a href="/course/HISC55H3">HISC55H3</a>/<a href="/course/AFSC55H3">AFSC55H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISD52H3">HISD52H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSD52H3">
AFSD53H3:  Africa and Asia in the First World War<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar course examines the First World War in its imperial and colonial context in Africa and Asia. Topics include forgotten fronts in Africa, the Middle East, Asia and the Pacific, colonial armies and civilians, imperial economies and resources, the collapse of empires and the remaking of the colonial world.</p> <p>Same as <a href="/course/GASD53H3">GASD53H3and <a href="/course/HISD53H3">HISD53H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits, including: [1.0 credit in AFS, GAS, or Africa and Asia area HIS courses]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/GASD53H3">GASD53H3</a>, <a href="/course/HISD53H3">HISD53H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/AFSD53H3">
ANTA01H3:  Introduction to Anthropology: Becoming Human<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to Biological Anthropology and Archaeology. ANTA02H3:  Introduction to Anthropology: Society, Culture and Language<p>Course Description</p><p>How does an anthropological perspective enable us to understand cultural difference in an interconnected world? In this course, students will learn about the key concepts of culture, society, and language. Drawing upon illustrations of family, economic, political, and religious systems from a variety of the world's cultures, this course will introduce students to the anthropological approach to studying and understanding human ways of life.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: ANT100Y, ANT102H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTA02H3">
ANTB01H3:  Political Ecology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines human-environmental relations from an anthropological perspective. Throughout the semester, we explore how peoples from different parts of the globe situate themselves within culturally constructed landscapes. Topics covered include ethnoecology, conservation, green consumerism, the concept of 'wilderness', and what happens when competing and differentially empowered views of the non-human world collide.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ANTA02H3">ANTA02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB01H3">

ANTB09H3:  Culture through Film and Media<p>Course Description</p><p>How is culture represented through visual media, from ethnographic and documentary film, to feature films, television, and new media? How do various communities re-vision themselves through mass, independent, or new media? This course investigates media and its role in the contemporary world from a socio-cultural anthropological perspective.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ANTA02H3">ANTA02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB09H3">
ANTB11H3:  World Prehistory<p>Course Description</p><p>This introduction to archaeology focuses on how societies around the world have changed through time from the earliest humans to the emergence of state-level societies. This course uses a global perspective to address key issues such as evidence of the earliest art, development of agriculture, and the origins of social inequality and warfare. </p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB11H3">
ANTB12H3:  Anthropology of Science Fiction<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is about science fiction as a form of cultural and political critique. The course will explore themes that are central to both ethnography and science fiction, including topics such as colonialism, gender, and the climate crisis, while reflecting on the power of writing and myth-making to produce meaning and the future. </p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB12H3">
ANTB14H3:  Evolutionary Anthropology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores the synthetic theory of evolution, its principles, processes, evidence and application as it relates to the evolution of human and nonhuman primates. Lecture topics and laboratory projects include: evolutionary theory, human variation, human adaptability, primate biology, and behaviour, taxonomy and classification, paleontological principles and human origins and evolution.</p> <p>Science credit</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ANT203Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB14H3">
ANTB15H3:  Human Biological Variation and Evolution <p>Course Description</p><p>This course will explore biological variation in the genus Homo from evolutionary and anthropological perspectives. Topics such as human adaptability, genetic variation and evolution, the non-existence of biological race, and the ecogeographic patterning of human phenotypic variation will be covered. <br>Science credit<br>Same as <a href="/course/HLTB20H3">HLTB20H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3or [<a href="/course/HLTA02H3">HLTA02H3and <a href="/course/HLTA03H3">HLTA03H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ANT203Y, <a href="/course/HLTB20H3">HLTB20H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB15H3">
ANTB16H3:  Canadian Cultural Identities<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores the creation or invention of a Canadian national identity in literature, myth and symbolism, mass media, and political culture. Ethnographic accounts that consider First Nations, regional, and immigrant identities are used to complicate the dominant story of national unity.<br> Area course</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB16H3">
ANTB18H3:  Development, Inequality and Social Change in Latin America<p>Course Description</p><p>This course addresses Latin American systems of inequality in relation to national and transnational political economy, from colonialism to neoliberalism; how ideas of race, culture, and nation intersect with development thinking and modernization agendas; and how the poor and marginalized have accommodated, resisted, and transformed cultural and political domination.<br>Area course</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (ANTC08H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB18H3">
ANTB19H3:  Ethnography and the Comparative Study of Human Societies<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to the theory and practice of ethnography, the intensive study of people's lives as shaped by social relations, cultural beliefs, and historical forces. Various topics, including religion, economics, politics, and kinship introduce students to key anthropological concepts and theoretical developments in the field.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTA02H3" target="_blank">ANTA02H3or [any 2.0 credits in ANT, HLT, IDS, CIT, GGR, POL, SOC or HCS courses]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ANT204Y, ANT207H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB19H3">
ANTB20H3:  Ethnography and the Global Contemporary <p>Course Description</p><p>How has the global flow of goods, persons, technologies, and capital reproduced forms of inequality? Using ethnography and other media, students examine globalization through topics like migration, race and citizenship, environmental degradation, and increasing violence while also discussing older anthropological concerns (e.g., kinship, religious practices, and authority). This course enhances students’ understanding of ethnography, as a method for studying how actors engage and rework the global forces shaping their lives.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTA02H3" target="_blank">ANTA02H3or [any 2.0 credits in ANT, HLT, IDS, CIT, GGR, POL, SOC or HCS courses]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ANT204Y, ANT204H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB20H3">
ANTB22H3:  Primate Behaviour<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will provide students with a general introduction to the behaviour and ecology of non-human primates (prosimians, Old and New World monkeys, and apes), with a particular emphasis on social behaviour. The course will consist of lectures reinforced by course readings; topics covered will include dominance, affiliation, social and mating systems, communication, and reproduction.<br>Science credit</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB22H3">
ANTB26H3:  The Middle East and North Africa: Past and Present<p>Course Description</p><p>What makes the Middle East and North Africa unique as a world region? This course considers the enduring impact of the past colonial encounter with the North Atlantic, as well as religious movements, nationalist histories, the impact of new communication technologies, and regional conflicts. Examples are drawn from a variety of contexts.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (ANTC89H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB26H3">
ANTB33H3:  The Future of Work<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores a pressing issue facing contemporary life: “the future of work.” It examines how work has been and continues to be transformed by automation, digital technologies, climate change, pandemics, the retrenchment of the welfare state, deindustrialization, global supply chains, and imperial and colonial rule. All kinds of media (e.g., academic texts, corporate publications, policy reports, activist literature, cinema) will be utilized to demonstrate how these transformations are not limited to work or labour but reverberate across social, political, and economic life.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ANTA02H3">ANTA02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB33H3">
ANTB35H3:  Kids These Days: Youth, Language and Media<p>Course Description</p><p>Around the world, youth is understood as the liminal phase in our lives. This course examines how language and new media technologies mark the lives of youth today. We consider social media, smartphones, images, romance, youth activism and the question of technological determinism. ANTB36H3:  Anthropology of the End of the World<p>Course Description</p><p>A cultural and comparative study of apocalyptic thought, practice, and representation around the world. It explores the conditions that inspire end times thinking and the uses it serves. Cases may include: millenarian movements, Revelation, colonialism, epidemics, infertility, deindustrialization, dystopian science fiction, nuclear war, climate change, and zombies.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA02H3">ANTA02H3or [any 2.0 credits in ANT, HLT, IDS, CIT, GGR, POL, SOC or HCS courses]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB36H3">
ANTB42H3:  Culture and Society in Contemporary South Asia<p>Course Description</p><p>This course surveys central issues in the ethnographic study of contemporary South Asia (Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, the Maldives, Nepal, Pakistan and Sri Lanka). Students will engage with classical and recent ethnographies to critically examine key thematic fault lines within national imaginations, especially along the lines of religion, caste, gender, ethnicity, and language. Not only does the course demonstrate how these fault lines continually shape the nature of nationalism, state institutions, development, social movements, violence, and militarism across the colonial and post-colonial periods but also, demonstrates how anthropological knowledge and ethnography provide us with a critical lens for exploring the most pressing issues facing South Asia in the world today.</p> <p>Same as <a href="/course/GASB42H3">GASB42H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA02H3">ANTA02H3or [any 4.0 credits in ANT, HLT, IDS, CIT, GGR, POL, SOC, GAS, HCS or Africa and Asia Area HIS courses]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ANTC12H3), <a href="/course/GASB42H3">GASB42H3</a>, (GASC12H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB42H3">
ANTB64H3:  Are You What You Eat?: The Anthropology of Food<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the social significance of food and foodways from the perspective of cultural anthropology. We explore how the global production, distribution, and consumption of food, shapes or reveals, social identities, political processes, and cultural relations. Lectures are supplemented by hands-on tutorials in the Culinaria Kitchen Laboratory.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA02H3">ANTA02H3or any 2.0 credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ANTC64H3), ANT346H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB64H3">
ANTB65H3:  An Introduction to Pacific Island Societies<p>Course Description</p><p>The ethnography of Pacific Island societies has been central to the development of the discipline of anthropology, particularly in the study of exchange systems, ritual, and gender. This course examines the unique contributions that Pacific scholarship has made to the development of anthropological theory in these areas. As well, the course focuses on Pacific Islander perspectives on contemporary issues such as: cultural change and continuity in the face of globalization; identity and representation; rapid urbanization; place-making and indigeneity; and climate change and the environment. <br>Area course</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (ANTC65H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB65H3">
ANTB66H3:  Spiritual Paths: A Comparative Anthropology of Pilgrimage<p>Course Description</p><p>A comparison of pilgrimage in different religious traditions, including Christian, Buddhist, Muslim, Hindu and those of indigenous communities (such as the Huichol of Mexico) will introduce students to the anthropology of religion. We will consider the aspirations and experiences of various pilgrims, while being mindful of cultural similarities and differences.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: RLG215H</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ANTA02H3">ANTA02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB66H3">
ANTB80H3:  Introduction to Archaeology: Methods, Theories, and Practices<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to the methods, theories, and practices used in Archaeology. Building on the course material presented in <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3</a>, there will be a focus on important themes in Archaeology as a subfield of Anthropology, including: artefact analysis, dating methods, theories of the origins of social development/complexity, and careers in archaeology today. This course will include lectures and complimentary readings that will expose students to the important ideas within the field. There will also be an experiential component in the form of four hands-on workshops where students will get to interact with artefacts and gain experience using some of the methods discussed in class. There will be an extra workshop for students to get help with their essay outline.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ANT200Y1, ANT200Y5, ANT200H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTB80H3">

ANTC07H3:  Material Worlds<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores the intersection of the social and the material by examining the role of objects in making worlds. ANTC09H3:  Sex, Love, and Intimacy: Anthropological Approaches to Kinship and Marriage<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores Anthropological approaches to kinship and family arrangements. In addition to examining the range of forms that family arrangements can take cross-culturally, we also examine how kinship configurations have changed within our own society in recent years. Topics to be covered include trans-national adoption, "mail-order-brides", new reproductive technologies and internet dating.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/ANTB19H3">ANTB19H3and <a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>] or [1.5 credits in ANT]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC09H3">
ANTC10H3:  Anthropological Perspectives on Development<p>Course Description</p><p>A critical probe of the origins, concepts, and practices of regional and international development in cultural perspective. Attention is paid to how forces of global capitalism intersect with local systems of knowledge and practice.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/ANTB19H3">ANTB19H3and <a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>] or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC10H3">
ANTC14H3:  Feminism and Anthropology<p>Course Description</p><p>Examines why, when, and how gender inequality became an anthropological concern by tracing the development of feminist thought in a comparative ethnographic framework.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/ANTB19H3">ANTB19H3and <a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>] or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC14H3">
ANTC15H3:  Genders and Sexualities<p>Course Description</p><p>Explores cultural constructions of male and female in a range of societies and institutions. Also examines non-binary gender configurations.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>]or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC15H3">
ANTC16H3:  Human Innovations<p>Course Description</p><p>In this course, students examine theoretical and methodological approaches to understanding significant concepts and events in the evolution of modern humans and our recent fossil ancestors. The goal of this course is to provide students with a current and detailed understanding of the evolutionary events that ultimately led to the biological, behavioural, and cultural evolution of modern humans.</p> <p>Science credit</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ANT332Y)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC16H3">
ANTC17H3:  Human Origins: New Discoveries<p>Course Description</p><p>In this course, students explore how new discoveries in human origins research influence our current understandings of hominin evolution over the past 7 million years. In this lab-based course, students develop their practical skills in identifying, describing, and interpreting primate skeletal and dental anatomy as a foundation for understanding the hominin fossil record. Next, students evaluate and interpret new research on the biology, diversity, dispersals, and evolutionary relationships of fossil hominins. We examine how new research is progressing the field of paleoanthropology and attempting to clarify the origins of modern human biology and behaviour.</p> <p>Science credit</p> <br><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ANT332Y)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC17H3">
ANTC18H3:  Urban Worlds<p>Course Description</p><p>The planet today is more urbanized than at any other moment in its history. What are the tools we need to examine urbanization in this contemporary moment? This course explores how urbanization has altered everyday life for individuals and communities across the globe. Students will trace urbanization as transformative of environmental conditions, economic activities, social relations, and political life. Students will thus engage with work on urbanization to examine how urban spaces and environments come to be differentiated along the lines of race, class, and gender. Not only does this course demonstrate how such fault lines play themselves out across contexts, but also provides the critical lenses necessary to tackle the most pressing issues related to urbanization today. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>]or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC18H3">
ANTC19H3:  Producing People and Things: Economics and Social Life<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines economic arrangements from an anthropological perspective. A key insight to be examined concerns the idea that by engaging in specific acts of production, people produce themselves as particular kinds of human beings. Topics covered include gifts and commodities, consumption, global capitalism and the importance of objects as cultural mediators in colonial and post-colonial encounters.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>] </a>or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC19H3">
ANTC20H3:  Gifts, Money and Morality<p>Course Description</p><p>What limits exist or can be set to commoditized relations? To what extent can money be transformed into virtue, private goods into the public "Good"? We examine the anthropological literature on gift-giving, systems of exchange and value, and sacrifice. Students may conduct a short ethnographic project on money in our own society, an object at once obvious and mysterious.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>]or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC20H3">
ANTC21H3:  Locked Up: Anthropology of Imprisonment and Abolition<p>Course Description</p><p>Do we need prisons? Do all societies have them? Are there different forms of incarceration around the world? What are the alternatives? This course situates the current expansion of punitive imprisonment in an examination of the diverse ways societies around the world and through time have sought to address the breach of law and sanction intolerable behaviour. It assesses how the growth of prisons intersects with capitalism, racial terror, and social inequality. Its anthropological approach brings the experiences of incarcerated people and communities to the fore and engages local community responses from prison abolition groups.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/ANTB19H3">ANTB19H3and <a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>] or any 4.0 credits&nbsp;</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ANTA02H3">ANTA02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC21H3">
ANTC22H3:  Education, Power, and Potential: Anthropological Perspectives and Ethnographic Insights <p>Course Description</p><p><span><span>What does it mean to get an education? What are the consequences of getting (or not getting) a “good education”? For whom? Who decides? Why does it matter? </span><span>How are different kinds of education oriented toward different visions of the future? What might we learn about a particular cultural context if we explore education and learning as social </span><span>processes and</span><span> cultural products linked to specific cultural values, beliefs, and power dynamics? These are just some of the questions we will explore in this course. Overall, students will gain a familiarity with the anthropology of education through an exploration of ethnographic case studies from a variety of historical and cultural contexts. </span></span><span> </span></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>]</a>&nbsp;or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/ANTC88H3">ANTC88H3if taken in Fall 2021<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in any of the following Combined Degree Programs:  Evolutionary Anthropology (Specialist), Honours Bachelor of Science/ Master of Teaching  Evolutionary Anthropology (Major), Honours Bachelor of Science/ Master of Teaching  Socio-Cultural Anthropology (Specialist), Honours Bachelor of Arts/ Master of Teaching  Socio-Cultural Anthropology (Major), Honours Bachelor of Arts/ Master of Teaching</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC22H3">
ANTC24H3:  Culture, Mental Illness, and Psychiatry<p>Course Description</p><p>Does schizophrenia exist all over the world? Does depression look different in China than it does in Canada? By examining how local understandings of mental illness come into contact with Western psychiatric models, this course considers the role of culture in the experience, expression, definition, and treatment of mental illness and questions the universality of Western psychiatric categories. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>]</a>&nbsp;or [any 4.0 credits]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ANTC61H3">ANTC61H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC24H3">
ANTC25H3:  Anthropology and Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>How are we to understand the relationship between psychological universals and diverse cultural and social forms in the constitution of human experience? Anthropology's dialogue with Freud; cultural construction and expression of emotions, personhood, and self.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>]</a>&nbsp;or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC25H3">
ANTC27H3:  Primate Sociality<p>Course Description</p><p><span><span>Primates are an intensely social order of animals showing wide variation in group size, </span><span>organization</span><span> and structure. Using an evolutionary perspective, this course will focus on why primates form groups and how their relationships with different individuals are </span><span>maintained</span><span>, with reference to other orders of animals. </span><span>In particular, this</span><span> course examines how different forms of cooperation evolve when natural selection is often thought to </span><span>maintain</span><span> only selfish behaviours. The form and function of different social systems, mating systems, and behaviours will be examined.</span></span><span> </span></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTB22H3">ANTB22H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC27H3">
ANTC28H3:  Living through the Anthropocene<p>Course Description</p><p>We are living through the Anthropocene, a time in which humans have broadly transformed Earth’s composition and environmental processes. Did this period begin in 1950? Applying Indigenous and archaeological knowledges, we will contribute to debates concerning the deeper histories of a multi-sited, patchy Anthropocene. Past societies have lived through their own “Anthropocenes”, and their diverse stories are needed to shape contemporary environmental conservation initiatives. Critical plot points come from Indigenous Knowledge, archaeological materials, and paleoenvironmental evidence recorded in sediment cores. We will learn to integrate these lines of evidence through investigations of Indigenous environmental stewardship and lake sediment cores in Ontario.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3</a>, or <a href="/course/ANTB80H3">ANTB80H3</a>, or <a href="/course/EESA01H3">EESA01H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: Physical Geography, or Earth Sciences, or Environmental Sciences at Secondary or Post-Secondary level are beneficial but not required.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC28H3">
ANTC29H3:  Archaeologies of North America <p>Course Description</p><p>This course engages with the diverse histories of First Nations societies in North America, from time immemorial, through over 14 thousand years of archaeology, to the period approaching European arrivals. We tack across the Arctic, Plains, Northwest Coast, Woodlands, and East Coast to chart the major cultural periods and societal advancements told by First Nations histories and the archaeological record. Along with foundational discussions of ancestral peoples, societal development, and human paleoecology, we also engage with core topical debates in North American archaeology, such as the ethics of ancient DNA, peopling processes, environmental change, response, and conservation, inequalities, decolonization, and progress in Indigenous archaeologies.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC29H3">

Topic will change year to year.</span></span><span> </span></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ANTB11H3">ANTB11H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC30H3">

ANTC32H3:  Political Anthropology<p>Course Description</p><p>Can ethnographic research help us make sense of various political situations and conflicts around the world?
