ANTC34H3:  The Anthropology of Transnationalism<p>Course Description</p><p>This course considers dimensions of transnationalism as a mode of human sociality and site for cultural production. Topics covered include transnational labour migration and labour circuits, return migration, the transnational dissemination of electronic imagery, the emergence of transnational consumer publics, and the transnational movements of refugees, kinship networks, informal traders and religions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>]</a>&nbsp;or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC34H3">
ANTC42H3:  Human Growth and Development <p>Course Description</p><p><span>This course examines variability in biological growth and development in the genus Homo from both evolutionary and non-evolutionary perspectives. Emphasis is placed on exploring the adaptive and cultural contributors to variability in human growth patterns. Case studies from the Evolutionary Anthropology, Bioarchaeology, and Human Biology literature are used.</span><span> </span></p> <p><span>Science credit</span><span> </span></p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC42H3">
ANTC44H3:  Amazonian Anthropology<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar explores anthropological insights and historical/archeological debates emerging from Amazonia, a hotspot of social and biodiversity currently under grave threat. We will look at current trends in the region, the cultural logic behind deforestation and land-grabbing, and the cultural and intellectual production of indigenous, ribeirinho, and quilombola inhabitants of the region.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>] </a>or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC44H3">
ANTC47H3:  Human Skeletal Anatomy and Biology <p>Course Description</p><p>A "hands-on" laboratory course which introduces students to human skeletal anatomy and biology. The course will cover the gross anatomy of the skeleton and dentition, as well as basic histology and the composition and microstructure of bone and teeth.</p> <p>Science credit</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3or <a href="/course/BIOB33H3">BIOB33H3or <a href="/course/HLTB33H3">HLTB33H3or <a href="/course/PMDB33H3">PMDB33H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ANT334H, ANT334Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC47H3">
ANTC48H3:  Advanced Topics In Human Osteology<p>Course Description</p><p>A "hands-on" laboratory course which introduces students to the methods of analyzing human skeletal remains. Topics and analytic methods include: (1) the recovery and treatment of skeletal remains from archaeological sites; (2) odontological description, including dental pathology; (3) osteometric description; (4) nonmetric trait description; (5) methods of estimating age at death and sex; (6) quantitative analysis of metric and nonmetric data; and (7) paleopathology.<br> Science credit</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTC47H3">ANTC47H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ANT334H, ANT334Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC48H3">
ANTC49H3:  From Animal to Human Cognition: Senses, Sentience and Smarts<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will survey and assess the evolution of different animal sensory systems and their ecological underpinnings. The concept of sentience, and the question of when an animal can be said to be sentient, will be critically examined. The cognitive abilities of our extant primate relatives and other animals will be discussed to examine how human cognition is, or is not, unique. The evolutionary forces that may have selected for human cognitive abilities will be considered.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ANTB22H3">ANTB22H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC49H3">
ANTC52H3:  Global Politics of Language<p>Course Description</p><p>Language and ways of speaking are foundational to political cultures. This course covers the politics of language in the age of globalization, including multiculturalism and immigration, citizenship, race and ethnicity, post-colonialism, and indigeneity. Ethnographic examples are drawn from a variety of contexts, including Canadian official bilingualism and First Nations.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>]</a>&nbsp;or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC52H3">
ANTC53H3:  Journalism Around the World<p>Course Description</p><p>How does journalism engage and feed into broader public debates? And how does journalism from around the world impact such debates differently? This course considers the topic of journalism and public sphere theory, and discusses the relationship between the press and politics, government, and democracy. The course takes a comparative lens to journalism, and will also draw on ethnographic readings and approaches. <br>Same as <a href="/course/MDSC36H3">MDSC36H3</a>/(MDSC53H3)</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/ANTB19H3">ANTB19H3and <a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>] or [any 4.0 credits] or [2.0 credits at the B level in MDS courses] or [2.0 credits at the B level in JOU courses] or [4.5 credits from the Major (Joint) program in New Media Studies Group I and Group II courses]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/MDSC36H3">MDSC36H3</a>/(MDSC53H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC53H3">
ANTC55H3:  People and the Environment: Approaches to a More-than-Human World<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to anthropological perspectives on what is usually referred to as the environment. We will explore ethnographic descriptions of different ways that humans and non-humans live together. Topics include: how do hunting-based collectives see their relation to animals and the forest? What is environmental racism and what does it reveal about race and segregation? What is the relation between Indigeneity and conservation? What is the relation between the human mind and ecology?</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA02H3">ANTA02H3or 1.0 credit of courses in social sciences and humanities&nbsp;</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ANTA02H3">ANTA02H3</a>, <a href="/course/ANTB19H3">ANTB19H3</a>, <a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3and <a href="/course/ANTB01H3">ANTB01H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC55H3">
ANTC58H3:  Constructing the Other:  Orientalism through Time and Place<p>Course Description</p><p>This course reflects on the concept of <em>Orientalism</em> and how it informs the fields of Classical Studies and Anthropology. Topics to be discussed include the Orientalization of the past and the origin, role, and significance of ancient representations of the "Other" in contemporary discourses.<br> Same as <a href="/course/CLAC68H3">CLAC68H3and <a href="/course/HISC68H3">HISC68H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.0 credits, including 0.5 credit at the A- or B-level in ANT, HIS or CLA courses.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/CLAC68H3">CLAC68H3</a>, <a href="/course/HISC68H3">HISC68H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC58H3">
ANTC59H3:  Anthropology of Language and Media<p>Course Description</p><p>Anthropology studies language and media in ways that show the impact of cultural context. This course introduces this approach and also considers the role of language and media with respect to intersecting themes: ritual, religion, gender, race/ethnicity, power, nationalism, and globalization. Class assignments deal with lecturers, readings, and students' examples.<br> Same as <a href="/course/MDSC21H3">MDSC21H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTB19H3">ANTB19H3and <a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3or any 2.0 credits in MDS<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ANTB21H3), (MDSB02H3), <a href="/course/MDSC21H3">MDSC21H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC59H3">
ANTC61H3:  Medical Anthropology: Illness and Healing in Cultural Perspective<p>Course Description</p><p>Social and symbolic aspects of the body, the life-cycle, the representation and popular explanation of illness, the logic of traditional healing systems, the culture of North American illness and biomedicine, mental illness, social roots of disease, innovations in health care delivery systems.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB20H3" target="_blank">ANTB20H3</a>] or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC61H3">
ANTC62H3:  Medical Anthropology: Biological and Population Perspectives<p>Course Description</p><p><span><span>This course examines health and disease in the genus Homo from biological and population perspectives. Emphasis is placed on exploring the variability of populations in disease susceptibility, resistance, and outcomes. In addition, this course will introduce students to the basic concepts, principles and methods of medical anthropology and epidemiology.</span></span><span> </span><br>Science credit</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC62H3">
ANTC65H3:  Anthropology of Science, Medicine, and Technology <p>Course Description</p><p>This course is an enquiry into the social construction of science and scientific expertise, with a particular focus on medicine and health. The interdisciplinary field of Science and Technology Studies (STS) opens up a very different perspective from what gets taught in biology classes about how medical knowledge is created, disseminated, becomes authoritative (or not), and is taken up by different groups of people. In our current era of increasing anti-science attitudes and “alternative facts,” this course will offer students an important new awareness of the politics of knowledge production.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>]</a>&nbsp;or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <span><span>Students who enrolled in <a href="/course/ANTC69H3">ANTC69H3in Fall 2023 may not take this course for credit.</span></span><span> </span><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC65H3">
ANTC66H3:  Anthropology of Tourism<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores the global cultural phenomenon of tourism. Using case studies and historical perspectives, we investigate the complex motivations and consequences of travel, the dimensions of tourism as development, the ways tourism commodifies daily life, the politics of tourism representation, and the intersection of travel, authenticity and modernity.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>]or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC66H3">
ANTC67H3:  Foundations in Epidemiology<p>Course Description</p><p>Epidemiology is the study of disease and its determinants in populations. It is grounded in the biomedical paradigm, statistical reasoning, and that risk is context specific. This course will examine such issues as: methods of sampling, types of controls, analysis of data, and the investigation of epidemics.<br>Science credit</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <span><span>Any statistics course</span></span><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC67H3">
ANTC68H3:  Deconstructing Epidemics<p>Course Description</p><p>Colonization, globalization and socio-ecological factors play an important role in origin, maintenance and emergence of old and new infectious diseases in human populations such as yellow fever, cholera, influenza, SARS. Issues of co-morbidity, the epidemiological transition, syndemics and the impact of global warming on the emergence of new diseases are discussed.<br>Science credit</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: Any statistics course<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC68H3">
ANTC69H3:  Ideas That Matter: Key Themes and Thinkers in Anthropology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores key themes, theories, and thinkers that have shaped anthropological thought, past and present. In any given year we will focus on the work of a particular important thinker or a school of thought. As we examine trends and approaches that have been influential to the field, we consider the debates these have generated, the ethnographic innovations they have inspired, and their relevance for core debates in anthropology. Topics and readings will be chosen annually by the instructor.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>]</a>&nbsp;or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the specialist program in Anthropology. Additional students will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC69H3">
ANTC70H3:  Ethnographic Methods in Anthropology: Past, Present, and Future<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is an exploration of the ongoing significance of the ethnographic method to the practice of research in socio-cultural anthropology. How and why have ethnographic methods become so central to anthropology, and what can we continue to learn with them? Students complement readings and lectures on theories and practices of ethnographic methods, both historical and contemporary, with exercises and assignments designed to provide first-hand experience in carrying out various techniques of ethnographic research. We also consider the unique ethical challenges of ethnographic methods and what it means to conduct ethically sound research.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTB19H3">ANTB19H3and <a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3and [at least 0.5 credit at the C-level in socio-cultural anthropology courses].<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ANTC60H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist in Anthropology, followed by students in the Major in Anthropology, followed by students in the Specialist programs in International Development Studies.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC70H3">
ANTC71H3:  Climate, Palaeoecology, and Policy: Archaeology of Humans in the Environment<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the evolution of human-environment systems over deep time as well as the present implications of these relationships. We will examine the archaeological methods used in reconstructing human palaeoecology and engage with evolutionary and ecological theory as it has been applied to the archaeological record in order to understand how humans have altered ecosystems and adapted to changing climates through time and space. Building upon the perspective of humans as a long-term part of ecological systems, each student will choose a current environmental policy issue and progressively build a proposal for a remediation strategy or research program to address gaps in knowledge.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [0.5 credit from the following: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3</a>, <a href="/course/ANTB80H3">ANTB80H3</a>, <a href="/course/EESA01H3">EESA01H3or <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3</a>] and [1.0 credit of additional B- or C- level courses in ANT, BIO, or EES]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: A knowledge of evolutionary anthropology, archaeology, or relevant courses in ecology.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC71H3">
ANTC80H3:  Race and Racism:  Anthropological Insights<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores ideas of race and racist practice, both past and present. Socio-cultural perspectives on race and racism must address a central contradiction: although biological evidence suggests that racial categories are not scientifically valid, race and racism are real social phenomena with real consequences. In order to address this contradiction, the course will examine the myriad ways that race is produced and reproduced, as well as how racism is perpetuated and sustained.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>]</a>&nbsp;or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC80H3">
ANTC88H3:  Special Topics<p>Course Description</p><p>The topics presented in this course will represent a range of contemporary issues in Anthropology. Topics will vary by instructor and term.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">[ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB20H3'&gt;ANTB20H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3</a>]</a>&nbsp;or [any 4.0 credits]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC88H3">
ANTC90H3:  Cultural Heritage in Action<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will introduce students to field-based methods used in archaeological fieldwork. This includes all stages of work from archival research to collection, cleaning, and report writing. The focus is on understanding the process of archaeological field research and developing an understanding of archaeological sites in cultural, environmental, and community contexts, not simply locations for artifact retrieval. The course will include training in field safety, archival research, ground survey, remote sensing, geological coring, excavation, and preliminary interpretation of archaeological data.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3</a>, <a href="/course/ANTB80H3">ANTB80H3and 1.0 credit of additional C-level courses in any field</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: [<a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/ANTB80H3">ANTB80H3or <a href="/course/ANTB11H3">ANTB11H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC90H3">
ANTC99H3:  Primate Evolution<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines 65 million years of evolutionary history for non-human primates. The primary emphasis will be on the fossil record. Topics covered may include the reconstruction of behaviour from fossil remains, the evolution of modern primate groups, and the origins of the Order.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <span><span><a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3or <a href="/course/BIOB33H3">BIOB33H3or <a href="/course/HLTB33H3">HLTB33H3or <a href="/course/PMDB33H3">PMDB33H3</a></span></span><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTC99H3">
ANTD04H3:  The Anthropology of Violence and Suffering<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the social life of violence, its cultural production and political effects in a global perspective. It asks how social worlds are made and unmade through, against, and after violent events, how violence is remembered and narrated, and how ethnography might respond to experiences of suffering, trauma, and victimhood. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTB19H3">ANTB19H3and <a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3and [at least 0.5 credit at the C-level in Socio-Cultural Anthropology].<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD04H3">
ANTD05H3:  Advanced Fieldwork Methods in Social and Cultural Anthropology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides students with experience in carrying out ethnographic research in the Greater Toronto Area. Working with the Center for Ethnography, students define and execute individual research projects of their own design. The course provides students with the opportunity to present and discuss their unfolding research, as well as to present the findings of their research. This course is completed over two terms, and culminates in an original research paper.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/ANTB19H3">ANTB19H3and <a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3and [(ANTC60H3) or <a href="/course/ANTC70H3">ANTC70H3</a>]] and [an additional 1.0 credit at the C-level in socio-cultural anthropology] and [a cumulative GPA of 2.7, or permission of the instructor].&nbsp;<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ANTD05Y3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Preference will be given to Specialists and Majors in Anthropology, in that order.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD05H3">
ANTD06H3:  Reading Ethnography<p>Course Description</p><p>This course considers the reading and writing of ethnography - the classic genre of socio-cultural anthropology. We examine what differentiates ethnography from other forms of research and how to distinguish ethnographic works of high quality. Also considered are the politics of representation, including how ethnographic writing may reflect unequal relationships of power.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTB19H3">ANTB19H3and <a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3and [at least 1.0 credit at the C-level in Socio-Cultural Anthropology.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD06H3">
ANTD07H3:  Advanced Regional Seminar<p>Course Description</p><p>This course allows students to examine particular culture areas at an advanced level. Specific case studies from the region will be used to highlight theoretical and ethnographic issues. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB20H3" target="_blank">ANTB20H3and [at least 0.5 credit at the C-level in Socio-Cultural Anthropology]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD07H3">
ANTD10H3:  The Anthropology of 'Life' Itself<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will examine cultural understandings of ‘life’ – What is life? What is a life? How do humans value (or alternatively not value) life in different social and cultural settings? What constitutes a ‘good life’? ANTD17H3:  Medical Osteology: Public Health Perspectives on Human Skeletal Health<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar course will examine the clinical, epidemiological and public health literature on osteoporosis and other conditions impacting skeletal health. The course will also explore the potential economic impacts of osteoporosis on Canada's health care system given emerging demographic changes.<br>Science credit</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTC47H3'&gt;ANTC47H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><span><span><a href="/course/ANTC47H3">ANTC47H3</a></span></span></a><span><span> or <a href="/course/BIOB33H3">BIOB33H3or <a href="/course/HLTB33H3">HLTB33H3or <a href="/course/PMDB33H3">PMDB33H3</span></span><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD17H3">
ANTD18H3:  Palaeolithic Archaeology <p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar style course provides a foundation in the anthropology and archaeology of small-scale societies, particularly hunter-gatherers. The seminar’s temporal remit is broad, spanning ~2.5 million years of human evolution from the earliest tool-making hominins to living human societies. A selection of critical topics will therefore be covered. These include theoretical aspects of and evolutionary trends in forager subsistence strategies; technologies; mobility and use of space; sociopolitical organization; cognition; symbolism, ritual and religion; and transitions to food production. Topics will be illustrated using diverse case studies drawn from throughout the Paleolithic. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD18H3">
ANTD19H3:  Primate Conservation<p>Course Description</p><p>A large percentage of nonhuman primate species are at risk of extinction due mostly to human-induced processes. Relying on theory from Conservation Biology, this course will consider the intrinsic and extrinsic factors that lead to some primate species being threatened, while others are able to deal with anthropogenic influences. Students will critically examine conservation tactics and the uniqueness of each situation will be highlighted. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/ANTB22H3'&gt;ANTB22H3&lt;/a&gt;" target="_blank"><span><span><a href="/course/ANTB22H3">ANTB22H3</a></span></span></a><span><span> or <a href="/course/BIOA02H3">BIOA02H3</a></span></span><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD19H3">
ANTD20H3:  Culture and Community<p>Course Description</p><p>A field-based research seminar exploring the cultural dimensions of community and sense of place. Partnering with community-based organizations in Scarborough and the GTA, students will investigate topical issues in the immediate urban environment from an anthropological perspective. Yearly foci may include food, heritage, diaspora, and family.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB19H3" target="_blank">ANTB19H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/ANTB20H3" target="_blank">ANTB20H3and [at least 0.5 credit at the C-level in Socio-Cultural Anthropology]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ANTC60H3">ANTC60H3or <a href="/course/ANTC70H3">ANTC70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD20H3">
ANTD22H3:  Theory and Methodology in Primatology<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar course will examine contemporary theory and questions in primatology and carefully examine the types of data that researchers collect to answer their research questions.<br> Science credit</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTB22H3">ANTB22H3or <a href="/course/BIOC54H3">BIOC54H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD22H3">
ANTD25H3:  Medical Primatology: Public Health Perspectives on Zoonotic Diseases<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will examine the social and cultural contexts of animal-to-human disease transmission globally, and the public risks associated zoonoses present here in Canada. The course will incorporate both anthropological and epidemiological perspectives.<br>Science credit</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTB14H3">ANTB14H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD25H3">
ANTD26H3:  Caveman, Farmer, Herder, Trader: Evolution of Diet in Society<p>Course Description</p><p>Beginning with archaic <em>Homo sapiens</em> and ending with a discussion of how diet exists in a modern globalized cash economy, this course engages an archaeological perspective on changes in human diet and corresponding societal shifts. We will explore paradigmatic discourse around topics such as big game hunting, diet breadth, niche construction, and the Agricultural Revolution, while examining the archaeological record to clarify what "cavemen" really ate, inquire whether agriculture was as "revolutionary" as it has been presented, and delve into evidence of how colonialism, capitalism, and globalization have shaped our modern diet. Discussions will aim to interrogate current theories and contextualize why scientists (and the public) think the way they do about diet in the past and present.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3and <a href="/course/ANTB80H3">ANTB80H3and 1.0 credit from any course at the C-level] or [<a href="/course/FSTA01H3">FSTA01H3and 1.0 credit from any course at the C-level and permission of the instructor] or [<a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3and <a href="/course/ANTB11H3">ANTB11H3and 1.0 credit at the C-level]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: Some courses in human evolution and archaeology are highly recommended, knowledge of and interest in food system and the human past are acceptable.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD26H3">

ANTD33H3:  Geoarchaeological Perspectives of Human-Environment Interactions<p>Course Description</p><p>This course investigates global diversity in human-environment dialogues from a geoarchaeological perspective. We will emphasize the place of geoarchaeology in evolutionary anthropology, specifically addressing topics such as the role of fire in human evolution, human-ecosystem coevolution, societal resilience and collapse, and the developing Anthropocene. Through “hands-on” authentic research, the class will engage with the collection and interpretation of chronological, geochemical, biomolecular, micromorphological, and micro-sedimentary data for site formation processes, paleoenvironments, and human behaviors. We will collaborate on developing new geoarchaeological perspectives of the human-environment interactions unfolding along the eastern branch of Yat-qui-i-be-no-nick (Highland Creek) coursing through UTSC. How did Highland Creek shape cultures and societies through time? How did people shape the Creek’s environs?</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <p style="background: white;">One of <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3</a>, or <a href="/course/EESA01H3">EESA01H3</a>, or <a href="/course/ESTB01H3">ESTB01H3</a></p></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <span><span>Physical Geography and/or Earth Sciences at Secondary or Post-Secondary level (</span><span>beneficial</span><span> but not </span><span>required</span><span>).</span></span><span> </span><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD33H3">
ANTD35H3:  Bioarchaeology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will focus on a new direction in anthropology, exploring the potential of skeletal remains in reconstructing past lifeways. This seminar style class will build upon concepts introduced in Human Osteology courses. Additionally, more advanced methods of reconstructing patterns of subsistence, diet, disease, demography and physical activity.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTC47H3">ANTC47H3or <a href="/course/BIOB33H3">BIOB33H3or <a href="/course/HLTB33H3">HLTB33H3or <a href="/course/PMDB33H3">PMDB33H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ANT434H, ANT441H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD35H3">

ANTD50H3:  Contemporary Debates in the Study of Human Evolution<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar course will allow students to engage with the contemporary literature on topics of controversy in Human Evolution. Topics to be covered may include the evolutionary relationships among hominin species, the problem of naming and identifying parts of the evolving human lineage, inferences on the behavior of past hominins (e.g., burial of the dead), and the ethical issues that surround the study of our ancestors.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ANTC16H3">ANTC16H3and/or <a href="/course/ANTC17H3">ANTC17H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD50H3">
ANTD60H3:  Advanced Archaeological Laboratory Methods<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides specialized hands-on training in archaeological laboratory methods. Students will develop their own research project, undertaking analysis of archaeological materials, analyzing the resulting data, and writing a report on their findings. The methodological focus may vary from year to year. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3and <a href="/course/ANTB80H3">ANTB80H3and [1.0 credits at the C-level in any field] and permission of the instructor<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD60H3">
ANTD70H3:  Archaeological Field Methods<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides specialized hands-on experience with field-based archaeology, including planning, survey, testing, and/or excavation, as well as an overview of various archaeological excavation methods and practices. Students may enroll in this course to gain credit for participation in approved off-campus field work. In this case, they will coordinate with the instructor to develop a series of appropriate assignments relevant to their coursework and learning goals. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3and <a href="/course/ANTB80H3">ANTB80H3and [1.0 credits of additional C-level courses in any field] and permission of instructor<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD70H3">
ANTD71H3:  Community Engaged Fieldwork with Food<p>Course Description</p><p>This research seminar uses our immediate community of Scarborough to explore continuity and change within diasporic foodways. Students will develop and practise ethnographic and other qualitative research skills to better understand the many intersections of food, culture, and community. This course culminates with a major project based on original research.<br>Same as <a href="/course/HISD71H3">HISD71H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/HISB14H3">HISB14H3</a>/(HISC14H3) or <a href="/course/HISC04H3">HISC04H3or [2.0 credits in ANT courses of which 1.0 credit must be at the C-level] or permission of the instructor<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISD71H3">HISD71H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ANTB64H3">ANTB64H3</a>, <a href="/course/ANTC70H3">ANTC70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ANTD71H3">

ASTA01H3:  Introduction to Astronomy and Astrophysics I: The Sun and Planets<p>Course Description</p><p>The solar neighbourhood provides examples of astronomical bodies that can be studied by both ground-based and space vehicle based-observational instruments. The astronomical bodies studied range from cold and rocky planets and asteroids to extremely hot and massive bodies, as represented by the sun. This course considers astronomical bodies and their evolution, as well as basic parts of physics, chemistry, etc., required to observe them and understand their structure. The course is suitable for both science and non-science students.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: AST101H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ASTA01H3">
ASTA02H3:  Introduction to Astronomy and Astrophysics II: Beyond the Sun and Planets<p>Course Description</p><p>The structure and evolution of stars and galaxies is considered, with our own galaxy, the Milky Way, providing the opportunity for detailed study of a well-observed system. Even this system challenges us with many unanswered questions, and the number of questions increases with further study of the universe and its large-scale character. Current models and methods of study of the universe will be considered. The course is suitable for both science and non-science students.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: AST121H, AST201H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ASTA02H3">
ASTB03H3:  Great Moments in Astronomy<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of the people, the background and the events associated with some major advances in astronomy.<br>Emphasis is given to the role of a few key individuals and to how their ideas have revolutionized our understanding of nature and the Universe. The perspective gained is used to assess current astronomical research and its impact on society.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 4.0 full credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: AST210H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ASTB03H3">
ASTB23H3:  Astrophysics of Stars, Galaxies and the Universe<p>Course Description</p><p>Overview of astrophysics (except planetary astrophysics). Appropriate level for science students. Structure and evolution of stars, white dwarfs, neutron stars. Structure of Milky Way. Classification of galaxies. Potential theory, rotation curves, orbits, dark matter. Spiral patterns. Galaxy clusters. Mergers. Black holes in active galactic nuclei. Expansion of universe, dark energy.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3or <a href="/course/MATA31H3">MATA31H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/MATA36H3">MATA36H3or <a href="/course/MATA37H3">MATA37H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PHYA21H3">PHYA21H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MATB41H3">MATB41H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ASTB21H3), (ASTC22H3), [AST221H and AST222H]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ASTB23H3">
ASTC02H3:  Practical Astronomy: Instrumentation and Data Analysis<p>Course Description</p><p>A hands-on introduction to astronomical observing using the UTSC telescope. Lectures cover topics of astronomical instrumentation and data reduction. Observations of Solar System planets, moons, planetary nebula, globular clusters and galaxies will be made. Students will present their results in the style of a scientific paper and a talk.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ASTB23H3">ASTB23H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: AST325H, AST326Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ASTC02H3">

BIOB10H3:  Cell Biology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is designed to introduce theory and experimental techniques in cell biology. The course examines the structure and function of major animal and plant organelles and integrates this into a discussion of protein biosynthesis, signal-based sorting and intracellular trafficking using the cytoskeleton. Cell motility and cell interactions with the environment will also be examined to provide a solid foundation on the basic unit of life.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOA01H3">BIOA01H3and <a href="/course/BIOA02H3">BIOA02H3and <a href="/course/CHMA10H3">CHMA10H3and <a href="/course/CHMA11H3">CHMA11H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOB10H3">
BIOB11H3:  Molecular Aspects of Cellular and Genetic Processes<p>Course Description</p><p>A course focusing on the central dogma of genetics and how molecular techniques are used to investigate cellular processes. Topics include structure and function of the nucleus, DNA replication and cell cycle control, transcription and translation, gene regulation and signal transduction.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOA01H3">BIOA01H3and <a href="/course/BIOA02H3">BIOA02H3and <a href="/course/CHMA10H3">CHMA10H3and <a href="/course/CHMA11H3">CHMA11H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: BIO230H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOB11H3">
BIOB12H3:  Cell and Molecular Biology Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>A practical introduction to experimentation in cell and molecular biology. Lab modules will introduce students to concepts and techniques in the general preparation of solutions and buffers, microbiology, molecular biology, biochemistry, microscopy, data analysis, and science communication. This core laboratory course is the gateway for Molecular Biology &amp; Biotechnology Specialists to upper level laboratory offerings.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CHMA10H3">CHMA10H3and <a href="/course/CHMA11H3">CHMA11H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist programs in Molecular Biology and Biotechnology (Co-op and non-Co-op), Medicinal and Biological Chemistry, Neuroscience (Stage 1, Co-op only), Neuroscience (Cellular/Molecular Stream), and the Major program in Biochemistry. Additional students will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOB12H3">
BIOB20H3:  Introduction to Computational Biology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explains the fundamental methods of quantitative reasoning, with applications in medicine, natural sciences, ecology and evolutionary biology. It covers the major aspects of statistics by working through concrete biological problems. The course will help students develop an understanding of key concepts through computer simulations, problem solving and interactive data visualisation using the R programming language (no prior skills with R or specialized math concepts are required).</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOA01H3">BIOA01H3and <a href="/course/BIOA02H3">BIOA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: BIO259H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOB20H3">
BIOB32H3:  Animal Physiology Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines physiological mechanisms that control and co-ordinate the function of various systems within the body. The laboratory exercises examine properties of digestive enzymes, characteristics of blood, kidney function, metabolic rate and energetics, nerve function and action potentials, synaptic transmission, skeletal muscle function and mechanoreception.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-corequisite"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-corequisite">Corequisite: (BIOB30H3) or <a href="/course/BIOB34H3">BIOB34H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: BIO252Y, BIO270H, BIO271H, (ZOO252Y)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOB32H3">
BIOB33H3:  Human Development and Anatomy<p>Course Description</p><p>A lecture based course with online learning modules which deals with the functional morphology of the human organism. The subject matter extends from early embryo-genesis through puberty to late adult life.<br></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/BIOA01H3">BIOA01H3and <a href="/course/BIOA02H3">BIOA02H3</a>] or [<a href="/course/HLTA03H3">HLTA03H3and <a href="/course/HLTA20H3">HLTA20H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ANA300Y, ANA301H, <a href="/course/HLTB33H3">HLTB33H3</a>, <a href="/course/PMDB33H3">PMDB33H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Human Biology programs. BIOB34H3:  Animal Physiology<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the principles of animal physiology rooted in energy usage and cellular physiology. A comparative approach is taken, which identifies both the universal and unique mechanisms present across the animal kingdom. Metabolism, thermoregulation, digestion, respiration, water regulation, nitrogen excretion, and neural circuits are the areas of principal focus.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOA01H3">BIOA01H3and <a href="/course/BIOA02H3">BIOA02H3and <a href="/course/CHMA11H3">CHMA11H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: BIO270H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOB34H3">
BIOB35H3:  Essentials of Human Physiology<p>Course Description</p><p>An exploration of the normal physiology of the human body. Emphasis will be placed on organ systems associated with head and neck, especially nervous, respiratory, muscular, digestive, cardiovascular, and endocrine. Particular emphasis will be placed on speech, audition, and swallowing. The interrelationship among organ systems and how they serve to maintain homeostasis and human health will also be discussed.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOA01H3">BIOA01H3or <a href="/course/BIOA11H3">BIOA11H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/BIOC32H3">BIOC32H3</a>, <a href="/course/BIOC34H3">BIOC34H3</a>, BIO210Y5, PSL201Y1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist Program in Psycholinguistics (Co-op and Non co-op). BIOB38H3:  Plants and Society<p>Course Description</p><p>How do plants feed the world and which plants have the highest impact on human lives? What is the origin of agriculture and how did it change over time? The human population will climb to 10 billion in 2050 and this will tax our planet’s ability to sustain life. Environmentally sustainable food production will become even more integral.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOA01H3">BIOA01H3and <a href="/course/BIOA02H3">BIOA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (BIOC38H3), EEB202H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOB38H3">
BIOB50H3:  Ecology<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the main principles of ecology; the science of the interactions of organisms with each other and with their environment. Topics include physiological, behavioural, population, community, and applied aspects of ecology (e.g. disease ecology, climate change impacts, and approaches to conservation). Emphasis is given to understanding the connections between ecology and other biological subdisciplines. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOA01H3">BIOA01H3and <a href="/course/BIOA02H3">BIOA02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOB50H3">
BIOB51H3:  Evolutionary Biology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is an introduction to the main principles of evolution; the study of the diversity, relationships, and change over time in organisms at all scales of organization (from individuals to populations to higher taxonomic groups). The theory and principles of evolutionary biology give critical insight into a wide range of fields, including conservation, genetics, medicine, pathogenesis, community ecology, and development.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOA01H3">BIOA01H3and <a href="/course/BIOA02H3">BIOA02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOB51H3">
BIOB52H3:  Ecology and Evolutionary Biology Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to field, lab and computational approaches to ecology and evolution. Laboratories will explore a variety of topics, ranging from population genetics to community ecology and biodiversity. Some lab exercises will involve outdoor field work.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOA01H3">BIOA01H3and <a href="/course/BIOA02H3">BIOA02H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3or <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOB52H3">
BIOB90H3:  Integrative Research Poster Project<p>Course Description</p><p>In this course, students will develop scientific communication skills by working collaboratively with peers to create an informative scientific poster that will be presented in a poster session modelled on those held at most major scientific conferences. Successful posters will engage the interest of the audience in the topic, clearly and concisely outline understanding gained from the primary literature, and discuss how understanding is enhanced by integrating knowledge.</p> <p>Note: Students in all Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major programs in Biological Sciences are required to complete <a href="/course/BIOB90H3">BIOB90H3prior to graduation. <br></p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOB90H3">
BIOB97H3:  Bio-CURE: Course-based Undergraduate Research in Biological Sciences<p>Course Description</p><p>This course-based undergraduate research experience (CURE) in biological sciences will introduce students to the process of scientific inquiry as they engage in a hypothesis-driven research project with an emphasis on student-driven discovery, critical thinking, and collaboration. Students will learn to effectively access, interpret, and reference scientific literature as they formulate their research question and create an experimental design. Students will gain hands-on experience in research techniques and apply concepts in research ethics, reproducibility, and quantitative analyses to collect and interpret data.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-corequisite"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-corequisite">Corequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3and at least one of <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3</a>, <a href="/course/BIOB34H3">BIOB34H3</a>, <a href="/course/BIOB38H3">BIOB38H3</a>,<a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3</a>, <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: None<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Have completed no more than 11 credits towards a degree program at the time of enrolment.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOB97H3">
