BIOC10H3:  Cell Biology: Proteins from Life to Death<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar course builds on fundamental cell biology concepts using primary literature. This course will examine specific organelles and their functions in protein biogenesis, modification, trafficking, and quality control within eukaryotic cells. The experimental basis of knowledge will be emphasized and students will be introduced to hypothesis-driven research in cell biology.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: BIO315H, CSB428H</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/BIOC12H3">BIOC12H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC10H3">
BIOC12H3:  Biochemistry I: Proteins and Enzymes<p>Course Description</p><p>A lecture course describing factors involved in determining protein structures and the relationship between protein structure and function. Topics include: amino acids; the primary, secondary, tertiary and quaternary structures of proteins; protein motifs and protein domains; glycoproteins; membrane proteins; classical enzyme kinetics and allosteric enzymes; mechanisms of enzyme action.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3and <a href="/course/CHMB41H3">CHMB41H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/CHMB62H3">CHMB62H3</a>, BCH210H, BCH242Y</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CHMB42H3">CHMB42H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC12H3">
BIOC13H3:  Biochemistry II: Bioenergetics and Metabolism<p>Course Description</p><p>A lecture course that introduces how cells or organisms extract energy from their environment. The major metabolic pathways to extract energy from carbohydrates, fats and proteins will be discussed, as well as the regulation and integration of different pathways. An emphasis will be placed on real-world applications of biochemistry to metabolism. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3and <a href="/course/CHMB41H3">CHMB41H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/CHMB62H3">CHMB62H3</a>, BCH210H, BCH242Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC13H3">
BIOC14H3:  Genes, Environment and Behaviour<p>Course Description</p><p>This class will provide a survey of the role of genes in behaviour, either indirectly as structural elements or as direct participants in behaviour. Topics to be covered are methods to investigate complex behaviours in humans and animal models of human disease, specific examples of genetic effects on behaviour in animals and humans, and studies of gene-environment interactions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC14H3">
BIOC15H3:  Genetics<p>Course Description</p><p>Topics for this lecture and laboratory (or project) course include: inheritance and its chromosomal basis; gene interactions; sources and types of mutations and the relationship of mutation to genetic disease and evolution; genetic dissection of biological processes; genetic technologies and genomic approaches.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: BIO260H, HMB265H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC15H3">
BIOC16H3:  Evolutionary Genetics and Genomics<p>Course Description</p><p>Understanding the process of evolution is greatly enhanced by investigations of the underlying genes. This course introduces modern genetic and genomic techniques used to understand and assess microevolutionary changes at the population level. Topics include DNA sequence evolution, population genetics, quantitative genetics/genomics, positive Darwinian selection, the evolution of new genes, and comparative genomics.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/BIOC15H3">BIOC15H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC16H3">
BIOC17H3:  Microbiology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course presents an overview of the microbial world and introduces the students, in more detail, to the physiological, cellular and molecular aspects of bacteria. The laboratories illustrate principles and provide training in basic microbiological techniques essential to microbiology and to any field where recombinant DNA technology is used.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: MGY377H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC17H3">
BIOC18H3:  Looking Inside Cells: Current Methods in Cell Biology<p>Course Description</p><p>Cell biology techniques are at the forefront of biotechnology and biomedical research. This lecture/laboratory course will build on fundamental cell biology topics to understand and apply current methods used in research labs. This course will provide students with practical experience in analyzing eukaryotic cell structure and function, with a strong focus on light and fluorescence microscopy.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3and <a href="/course/BIOB12H3">BIOB12H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the following programs in Biological Sciences: Specialist Program in Molecular Biology and Biotechnology Co-op and Non-Co-op; Specialist in Human Biology Co-op and Non-Co-op; Major in Human Biology and Major in Molecular Biology, Immunology and Disease.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC18H3">
BIOC19H3:  Animal Developmental Biology<p>Course Description</p><p>Following a discussion of cellular and molecular events in early embryonic life, the development of several model systems will be analyzed such as erythropoiesis, lens development in the eye, spermatogenesis and myogenesis. Particular reference will be given to the concept that regulation of gene expression is fundamental to development.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CSB328H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC19H3">
BIOC20H3:  Principles of Virology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces viruses as infectious agents. Topics include: virus structure and classification among all kingdoms, viral replication strategies, the interactions of viruses with host cells, and how viruses cause disease. Particular emphasis will be on human host-pathogen interactions, with select lectures on antiviral agents, resistance mechanisms, and vaccines.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: BIO475H5, CSB351Y1, MGY378H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC20H3">
BIOC21H3:  Vertebrate Histology: Cells and Tissues<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of the structure of cells and the various tissue types which make up the vertebrate body; epithelial, connective, muscle, nervous, blood, and lymphatic. Emphasis is placed on how form is influenced by function of the cells and tissues.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB34H3">BIOB34H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ANA300Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC21H3">
BIOC23H3:  Practical Approaches to Biochemistry<p>Course Description</p><p>A lecture and laboratory course that introduces students to experimental approaches used in biochemical research. Topics include practical and theoretical aspects of: spectrophotometry; chromatography; electrophoresis; enzyme assays, protein purification and approaches to identify protein-protein interactions. Students are expected to solve numerical problems involving these and related procedures.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB12H3">BIOB12H3and <a href="/course/BIOC12H3">BIOC12H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: BCH370H, (BCH371H), BCH377H, BCH378H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC23H3">
BIOC29H3:  Introductory Mycology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will lead students through an exploration of the Kingdom of Fungi, covering topics in biodiversity, ecology, and evolution. Lectures will also discuss the broad application of fungi in agriculture, industry, medicine, and visual arts. In the laboratory sessions, students will learn to observe, isolate, and identify fungi using microscopy and modern biological techniques. Field trips will be opportunities to observe fungi in their native habitats and to discuss the real-world applications of diverse fungal organisms.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3and <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC29H3">
BIOC30H3:  From Genetic Codes to Fantastic Creatures<p>Course Description</p><p>In this interdisciplinary course, students will be introduced to strategies used by organisms – from microscopic bugs to plants and animals- to grow into the correct shape and size to complete their life cycle. The course will highlight molecular mechanisms controlling developmental processes common to all life forms. Within this course, students will integrate evidence and tools from across biology to advance the student understanding of how this knowledge can be critical in improving and advancing human health and well-being.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC30H3">
BIOC31H3:  Plant Development and Biotechnology<p>Course Description</p><p>A central question of developmental biology is how a single cell becomes a complex organism. This lecture course focuses on molecular and cellular mechanisms that control developmental processes in plants, including: embryonic, vegetative and reproductive development; hormone signal transduction pathways; plant-environment interaction and plant biotechnology.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CSB340H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC31H3">
BIOC32H3:  Human Physiology I<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to human physiology covering the nervous system, skeletal muscles, hormones, and the immune systems in both healthy and diseased states.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB34H3">BIOB34H3or <a href="/course/NROB60H3">NROB60H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSL300H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC32H3">
BIOC34H3:  Human Physiology II<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will cover the physiology of the human respiratory, cardiovascular, renal and digestive systems. Topics include cardiac function, ECG, blood flow/pressure regulation, pulmonary mechanics, gas transfer and transport, the control of breathing, sleep-related breathing disorders, kidney function, ion regulation, water balance, acid-base balance and digestive function/regulation. Students will complete a series of computer-simulated laboratory exercises on their own time.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB34H3">BIOB34H3or <a href="/course/NROB60H3">NROB60H3or BIO271H<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (BIOC33H3), (PSL302Y), PSL301H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC34H3">
BIOC35H3:  Principles in Parasitology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces principles in parasitic lifestyles. Topics that will be covered include common parasite life strategies, host-parasite interactions and co-evolution, parasite immune evasion strategies, impacts on public health, and treatment and prevention strategies.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC35H3">
BIOC37H3:  Plants: Life on the Edge<p>Course Description</p><p>Plants have evolved adaptations to maximize growth, survival and reproduction under various taxing environmental conditions. This course covers the great diversity of plant structures and function in relation to ecology, focusing mainly on flowering plants.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB38H3">BIOB38H3or <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3or <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: EEB340H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC37H3">
BIOC39H3:  Immunology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces the molecular and cellular basis of the immune system. Topics include self versus non-self recognition, humoral and cell-mediated immune responses, and the structure and function of antibodies. The importance of the immune system in health and disease will be emphasized and topics include vaccination, autoimmunity, and tumour immunology.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: IMM340H, IMM341H, IMM350H, IMM351H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC39H3">
BIOC40H3:  Plant Physiology<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to plant biology. Topics include plant and cell structure, water balance, nutrition, transport processes at the cell and whole plant level, physiological and biochemical aspects of photosynthesis, and growth and development in response to hormonal and environmental cues.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: BIO251H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC40H3">
BIOC44H3:  Neuroethology<p>Course Description</p><p>Neural basis of natural behaviour; integrative function of the nervous system; motor and sensory systems; mechanisms of decision-making, initiating action, co-ordination, learning and memory. Topics may vary from year to year.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB34H3">BIOB34H3or <a href="/course/NROB60H3">NROB60H3or <a href="/course/NROB61H3">NROB61H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (NROC34H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC44H3">
BIOC50H3:  Macroevolution<p>Course Description</p><p>An overview of recent developments in evolutionary biology that focus on large-scale patterns and processes of evolution. Areas of emphasis may include the evolutionary history of life on earth, phylogenetic reconstruction, patterns of diversification and extinction in the fossil record, the geography of evolution, the evolution of biodiversity, and the process of speciation.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3and <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: EEB362H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC50H3">
BIOC51H3:  Biodiversity Field Course<p>Course Description</p><p>A course with preparatory lectures on the UTSC campus and a field experience in natural settings where ecological, evolutionary, and practical aspects of biodiversity will be explored. Field work will involve outdoor activities in challenging conditions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3and <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3and <a href="/course/BIOB52H3">BIOB52H3and permission of instructor.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Students should contact the instructor 4 months before the start of the course. Additional course fees are applied, and students will need to place a deposit towards the cost of travel.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC51H3">
BIOC52H3:  Ecology Field Course<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides students with the opportunity to experience hands-on learning through informal natural history walks, and group and individual research projects, in a small-class setting. The course covers basic principles and selected techniques of field ecology and ecological questions related to organisms in their natural settings. Most of the field work takes place in the Highland Creek ravine.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-corequisite"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-corequisite">Corequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3and <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (EEB305H)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC52H3">
BIOC54H3:  Animal Behaviour<p>Course Description</p><p>Survey of the study of animal behaviour with emphasis on understanding behavioural patterns in the context of evolutionary theory. Topics include sexual selection and conflict, parental care, social behaviour, and hypothesis testing in behavioural research.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3and <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: EEB322H,<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC54H3">
BIOC58H3:  Biological Consequences of Global Change<p>Course Description</p><p>A lecture and tutorial course that addresses the key environmental factor that will dominate the 21st Century and life on the planet: Global Climate Change. The course will examine the factors that influence climate, from the formation of the earth to the present time, how human activities are driving current and future change, and how organisms, populations, and ecosystems are and will respond to this change. Finally, it will cover human responses and policies that can permit an adaptive response to this change.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3and <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: EEB428H, GGR314H, (BIO428H)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC58H3">
BIOC59H3:  Advanced Population Ecology<p>Course Description</p><p>The study of the interactions that determine the distribution and abundance of organisms on the earth. The topics will include an understanding of organism abundance and the factors that act here: population parameters, demographic techniques, population growth, species interactions (competition, predation, herbivory, disease), and population regulation. It will include an understanding of organism distribution and the factors that act here: dispersal, habitat selection, species interactions, and physical factors.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: EEB319H, (BIO319H)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC59H3">
BIOC60H3:  Winter Ecology<p>Course Description</p><p>Canada is characterized by its long and harsh winters. Any Canadian plant or animal has evolved one of three basic survival strategies: (1) migration (avoidance), (2) hibernation, and (3) resistance. These evolutionary adaptations are investigated by the example of common organisms from mainly southern Ontario.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3or <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC60H3">
BIOC61H3:  Community Ecology and Environmental Biology<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of the theory and methodology of community analysis, with an emphasis on the factors regulating the development of communities and ecosystems. The application of ecological theory to environmental problems is emphasized. We will examine the impacts of various factors, such as primary productivity, species interactions, disturbance, variable environments, on community and metacommunity structure, and on ecosystem function. We will also examine the impacts of climate change on the world's ecosystems.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: EEB321H, (BIO321H)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC61H3">
BIOC62H3:  Role of Zoos and Aquariums in Conservation<p>Course Description</p><p>This lecture and tutorial course explores the strategic and operational aspects of zoos and aquariums in conservation. Emphasis is on contemporary issues, including the balance between animal welfare and species conservation; nutrition, health and behavioural enrichment for captive animals; <em>in situ</em> conservation by zoos and aquariums; captive breeding and species reintroductions; and public outreach/education.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3and <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC62H3">
BIOC63H3:  Conservation Biology<p>Course Description</p><p>A lecture and tutorial course offering an introduction to the scientific foundation and practice of conservation biology. It reviews ecological and genetic concepts constituting the basis for conservation including patterns and causes of global biodiversity, the intrinsic and extrinsic value of biodiversity, the main causes of the worldwide decline of biodiversity and the approaches to save it, as well as the impacts of global climate change.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3and <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: EEB365H, (BIO365H)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC63H3">
BIOC65H3:  Environmental Toxicology<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the scientific study of the effects of toxic chemicals on biological organisms. Standard methods of assessing toxicant effects on individuals, populations, and communities are discussed. Special emphasis is placed on the chemistry of major toxicant classes, and on how toxicants are processed by the human body.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3and <a href="/course/CHMA10H3">CHMA10H3and <a href="/course/CHMA11H3">CHMA11H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC65H3">
BIOC70H3:  An Introduction to Bias in STEMM (Science, Technology, Engineering, Mathematics and Medicine)<p>Course Description</p><p>Research, teaching, and practice in science, technology, engineering, mathematics and medicine (STEMM) often rest on the unquestioned assertion of the impartial analyses of facts. This course will take a data-informed approach to understanding how human biases can, and have, affected science and its applications in a range of fields, with a particular focus on biology. Case studies may include reviews of how science has been used to justify or sustain racism, colonialism, enslavement, and the exploitation of marginalized groups. Links will be drawn to contemporary societal challenges, practices, and technologies. Topics will include how biases can shape science in terms of the questions under study, scientific inferences, and the types of knowledge and assumptions that inform applications, shape teaching, and influence popular understanding. Data on bias and societal costs of bias will be reviewed, as well as evidence-informed practices, structures, and individual actions which could ensure that STEMM disrupts, rather than enables, social inequities.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [Any of the following A-level courses: <a href="/course/ANTA01H3">ANTA01H3</a>, [<a href="/course/BIOA01H3">BIOA01H3and <a href="/course/BIOA02H3">BIOA02H3</a>], <a href="/course/BIOA11H3">BIOA11H3</a>, [<a href="/course/HLTA02H3">HLTA02H3and <a href="/course/HLTA03H3">HLTA03H3</a>] or [<a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a>]] and [Any of the following B-level courses: any B-level BIO course, any B-level PSY course, <a href="/course/ANTB14H3">ANTB14H3</a>, <a href="/course/ANTB15H3">ANTB15H3</a>, <a href="/course/HLTB20H3">HLTB20H3or <a href="/course/HLTB22H3">HLTB22H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC70H3">
BIOC90H3:  Integrative Multimedia Documentary Project<p>Course Description</p><p>In this course, students will produce engaging, documentary-style multimedia narratives that relay scientific evidence on a topic of interest to a lay audience. In order to create their documentaries, students will distill research findings reported in the primary literature and integrate knowledge from multiple fields of biology.</p> <p>Note: Students in all Specialists/Specialist Co-op and Major programs in Biological Sciences are required to complete <a href="/course/BIOC90H3">BIOC90H3prior to graduation.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB90H3">BIOB90H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOC90H3">
BIOC99H3:  Biology Team Research<p>Course Description</p><p>In this introduction to academic research, a group of 3-5 students work with a faculty supervisor and TA to develop a research proposal or implement a research project. Prior to registering, students must find a faculty supervisor, form a group, then submit a permission form to the department. The permission form may be downloaded from the <a href="https://www.utsc.utoronto.ca/biosci/welcome-biological-sciences">Biological Sciences website</a>.<br><br><strong>Note: </strong>Completion of this course can be used to fulfill a course requirement for the Certificate in Biological Sciences Research Excellence. BIOD06H3:  Advanced Topics in Neural Basis of Motor Control<p>Course Description</p><p>Lecture/seminar-based course addressing advanced topics in the neural basis of motor control in vertebrates. The emphasis will be placed on cellular-level understanding of how motor circuits operate.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC32H3">BIOC32H3or <a href="/course/BIOC44H3">BIOC44H3</a>, (NROC34H3) or <a href="/course/NROC64H3">NROC64H3or <a href="/course/NROC69H3">NROC69H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD06H3">
BIOD07H3:  Advanced Topics and Methods in Neural Circuit Analysis<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will survey different fields in neural circuit research ranging from sensory systems to motor control. Emphasis will be placed on new methodologies used to deconstruct circuit function, including advanced functional imaging, optogenetics, anatomical reconstruction and the latest behavioural approaches.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC32H3">BIOC32H3or <a href="/course/BIOC44H3">BIOC44H3or (NROC34H3) or <a href="/course/NROC64H3">NROC64H3or <a href="/course/NROC69H3">NROC69H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD07H3">
BIOD08H3:  Theoretical Neuroscience<p>Course Description</p><p>A seminar covering topics in the theory of neural information processing, focused on perception, action, learning and memory. Through reading, discussion and working with computer models students will learn fundamental concepts underlying current mathematical theories of brain function including information theory, population codes, deep learning architectures, auto-associative memories, reinforcement learning and Bayesian optimality.<br>Same as <a href="/course/NROD08H3">NROD08H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/BIOC44H3">BIOC44H3or (NROC34H3) or <a href="/course/NROC64H3">NROC64H3or <a href="/course/NROC69H3">NROC69H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/MATA29H3">MATA29H3or <a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3or <a href="/course/MATA31H3">MATA31H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (NROD08H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD08H3">
BIOD12H3:  Protein Homeostasis<p>Course Description</p><p>A lecture/seminar course on the cellular mechanisms of protein quality control. Animal and plant models will be used to highlight the mechanisms of action of selected protein folding and degradation machineries critical to cell functions. Primary literature in protein homeostasis and possible consequence of malfunction in eukaryotic cells will also be discussed.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC10H3">BIOC10H3or <a href="/course/BIOC12H3">BIOC12H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD12H3">
BIOD13H3:  Herbology: The Science Behind Medicinal Plants<p>Course Description</p><p>The use of plants in medicine has been documented for over 2,000 years. Their use is immersed in major ancient civilizations from around the World. This lecture/seminar/lab course will take the knowledge from indigenous medicine as a starting point and expand it with more recent advances in plant biochemistry, genetics and biotechnology.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC12H3">BIOC12H3or <a href="/course/BIOC13H3">BIOC13H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD13H3">
BIOD15H3:  Mechanism of Gene Regulation in Health and Disease<p>Course Description</p><p>Complex mechanisms of gene regulation (e.g., epigenetics, epitranscriptomics, regulatory RNAs) govern life-trajectories in health and disease. This advanced lecture, problem-based learning and seminar course equips students with critical thinking tools to dissect advanced concepts in genetics, including biological embedding, transgenerational inheritance, genetic determinism, gene therapy, and ethics in 21st century transgenics.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC15H3">BIOC15H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD15H3">
BIOD17H3:  Seminars in Cellular Microbiology<p>Course Description</p><p>An overview of the most significant advances in cellular microbiology. The curriculum will include cellular mechanisms of microbial pathogenesis, as well as recognition and elimination of pathogens by cells. Students will be required to participate in class discussions, and give oral presentations of scientific papers.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC17H3">BIOC17H3or <a href="/course/BIOC39H3">BIOC39H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD17H3">
BIOD18H3:  Practical Approaches in Infection and Immunity<p>Course Description</p><p>Cell Biology methods are pivotal to study the molecular and cellular mechanisms of infection and immunity. This lecture/laboratory course will build on understanding the role of microbes in health and disease by analyzing how different microbe models interact with eukaryotic host cells. Techniques such as fluorescence microscopy, flow cytometry and immunoassays will unravel the fascinating world of host cell-microbe interactions. Students will also explore the use of model systems using primary research to support their understanding of these interactions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC17H3">BIOC17H3and <a href="/course/BIOC18H3">BIOC18H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/BIOC39H3">BIOC39H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Biological Sciences programs: Specialist Program in Molecular Biology and Biotechnology &amp; Specialist program in Human Biology. As space permits, this course will be also opened to students in the Major program in Human Biology and Major program in Molecular Biology, Immunology, and Disease.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD18H3">
BIOD19H3:  Epigenetics in Health and Disease<p>Course Description</p><p>A lecture/seminar/discussion class on the emerging field of environmental epigenetics.&nbsp; Course will cover basic epigenetic mechanisms, methods in epigenetic research, epigenetic control of gene function, and the role of epigenetics in normal development and human disease.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC14H3">BIOC14H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD19H3">
BIOD20H3:  Special Topics in Virology<p>Course Description</p><p>This is a lecture/seminar course that will discuss advanced topics in human virology. The course focus will be on human viruses, pathogenicity in human hosts, and current literature on emerging pathogens.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC20H3">BIOC20H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: MGY440H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD20H3">
BIOD21H3:  Advanced Molecular Biology Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>Applications of molecular technology continue to revolutionize our understanding of all areas of life sciences from biotechnology to human disease. This intensive laboratory, lecture / tutorial course provides students with essential information and practical experience in recombinant DNA technology, molecular biology and bio-informatics.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB12H3">BIOB12H3and <a href="/course/BIOC15H3">BIOC15H3and <a href="/course/BIOC17H3">BIOC17H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC12H3">BIOC12H3(Note: Although listed as a corequisite, it is recommended that <a href="/course/BIOC12H3">BIOC12H3be taken in advance of <a href="/course/BIOD21H3">BIOD21H3</a>.)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist programs in Molecular Biology and Biotechnology (Co-op and non-Co-op). BIOD22H3:  Molecular Biology of the Stress Response<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is organized around a central theme, namely the expression of heat shock (stress) genes encoding proteins is important in cellular repair/protective mechanisms. Topics include heat shock transcription factors, heat shock proteins as 'protein repair agents' that correct protein misfolding, and diseases triggered by protein misfolding such as neurodegenerative disorders.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC10H3">BIOC10H3or <a href="/course/BIOC12H3">BIOC12H3or <a href="/course/BIOC15H3">BIOC15H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD22H3">
BIOD23H3:  Special Topics in Cell Biology<p>Course Description</p><p>A lecture/seminar/discussion class on contemporary topics in Cell Biology.&nbsp; Students will explore the primary literature becoming familiar with experimental design and methodologies used to decipher cell biology phenomena.&nbsp; Student seminars will follow a series of lectures and journal club discussions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC12H3">BIOC12H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD23H3">
BIOD24H3:  Human Stem Cell Biology and Regenerative Medicine<p>Course Description</p><p>In this lecture seminar course, we will explore how human stem cells generate the diverse cell types of the human body, and how they can be harnessed to understand and treat diseases that arise during embryonic development or during aging. We will also discuss current ethical issues that guide research practices and policies, including the destruction of human embryos for research, gene editing, and the premature clinical translation of stem cell interventions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC19H3">BIOC19H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CSB329H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD24H3">
BIOD25H3:  Genomics<p>Course Description</p><p>A course considering the principles of genome organization and the utilization of genomic approaches to studying a wide range of problems in biology. Topics to be presented will include innovations in instrumentation and automation, a survey of genome projects, genomic variation, functional genomics, transcription profiling (microarrays), database mining and extensions to human and animal health and biotechnology.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC15H3">BIOC15H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD25H3">
BIOD26H3:  Fungal Biology and Pathogenesis<p>Course Description</p><p>A lecture and tutorial based course designed to provide an overview of the fungal kingdom and the properties of major fungal pathogens that contribute to disease in animals (including humans) and plants. This course will address the mechanisms and clinical implications of fungal infections and host defence mechanisms. Topics include virulence factors and the treatment and diagnosis of infection.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC17H3">BIOC17H3or <a href="/course/BIOC39H3">BIOC39H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD26H3">
BIOD27H3:  Vertebrate Endocrinology<p>Course Description</p><p>A lecture/discussion class on the structure and function of the major endocrine organs of vertebrates. The course provides knowledge of endocrine systems encompassing hormone biosynthesis, secretion, metabolism, feedback, physiological actions, and pathophysiology. Recent advances in hormone research as well as contemporary issues in endocrinology will be examined.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB34H3">BIOB34H3and [<a href="/course/BIOC32H3">BIOC32H3or <a href="/course/BIOC34H3">BIOC34H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD27H3">
BIOD29H3:  Pathobiology of Human Disease<p>Course Description</p><p>This lecture/seminar format course will critically examine selected topics in human disease pathogenesis. Infectious and inherited diseases including those caused by human retroviruses, genetic defects and bioterrorism agents will be explored. Discussions of primary literature will encompass pathogen characteristics, genetic mutations, disease progression and therapeutic strategies.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC10H3">BIOC10H3or <a href="/course/BIOC20H3">BIOC20H3or <a href="/course/BIOC39H3">BIOC39H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD29H3">
BIOD30H3:  Plant Research and Biotechnology: Addressing Global Problems<p>Course Description</p><p>Plant scientists working to address pressing global challenges will give presentations. Student teams will identify and develop background knowledge and go beyond speaker’s presentations with new questions and/or applications.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC15H3">BIOC15H3or <a href="/course/BIOC30H3">BIOC30H3or <a href="/course/BIOC31H3">BIOC31H3or <a href="/course/BIOC40H3">BIOC40H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Major Program in Plant Biology. BIOD32H3:  Human Respiratory Pathophysiology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will examine how lung disease and other respiratory insults affect pulmonary physiology and lung function. Topics will include methods used to diagnose respiratory disease, pulmonary function in patients with various lung diseases as well as treatment options for both lung disease and lung failure.</p> <br><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/BIOC34H3">BIOC34H3or CSB346H1 or PSL301H1]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given the students in the Human Biology Specialist and Human Biology Major programs.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD32H3">
BIOD33H3:  Comparative Animal Physiology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will examine how various physiological systems and anatomical features are specialised to meet the environmental challenges encountered by terrestrial and aquatic animals. Topics include respiratory systems and breathing, hearts and cardiovascular systems, cardiorespiratory control, animal energetics, metabolic rate, thermoregulation, defenses against extreme temperatures, hibernation and osmotic/ionic/volume regulation. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: (BIOC33H3) or <a href="/course/BIOC34H3">BIOC34H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD33H3">
BIOD34H3:  Conservation Physiology<p>Course Description</p><p>This is a combined lecture and seminar course that will discuss topics such as climate change and plastics/microplastics effects on the physiology of animals, and physiological tools and techniques used in conservation efforts. The course will focus on how physiological approaches have led to beneficial changes in human behaviour, management or policy.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB34H3">BIOB34H3and [Completion of at least 0.5 credit at the C level in Biological Sciences]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD34H3">
BIOD36H3:  Advanced Topics in Molecular Parasitology<p>Course Description</p><p>This is a lecture and seminar course that will discuss advanced topics in the molecular study of parasites. The course will cover eukaryotic parasites (helminths, arthropods, and protozoans) that affect both human and plant hosts. The material will focus on the study of host-parasite interactions at the molecular level with a focus on current literature.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC35H3">BIOC35H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD36H3">
BIOD37H3:  Biology of Plant Stress<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines resistance mechanisms (anatomical, cellular, biochemical, molecular) allowing plants to avoid or tolerate diverse abiotic and biotic stresses. Topics include: pathogen defence; responses to temperature, light, water and nutrient availability, salinity, and oxygen deficit; stress perception and signal transduction; methods to study stress responses; and strategies to improve stress resistance.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC31H3">BIOC31H3or <a href="/course/BIOC40H3">BIOC40H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD37H3">
BIOD43H3:  Animal Movement and Exercise<p>Course Description</p><p>A lecture and seminar/discussion course covering integrative, comparative animal locomotion and exercise physiology. Topics will include muscle physiology, neurophysiology, metabolism, energetics, thermoregulation and biomechanics. These topics will be considered within evolutionary and ecological contexts.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: (BIOC33H3) or <a href="/course/BIOC34H3">BIOC34H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: HMB472H</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: Completion of an A-level Physics course.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD43H3">
BIOD45H3:  Animal Communication<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will examine how animals send and receive signals in different sensory modalities, and the factors that govern the evolution and structure of communication signals. Using diverse examples (from bird songs to electric fish) the course will demonstrate the importance of communication in the organization of animal behaviour, and introduce some theoretical and empirical tools used in studying the origins and structure of animal communication.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC44H3">BIOC44H3or <a href="/course/BIOC54H3">BIOC54H3or (NROC34H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD45H3">

BIOD52H3:  Biodiversity and Conservation<p>Course Description</p><p>A seminar exploration of current topics in biodiversity and conservation, including genetic, organismal, and community levels.&nbsp; Examples include DNA barcoding, adaptive radiations, phylogenetic trees, and biodiversity hotspots. &nbsp; Skills development in critical thinking and interpretation of the primary literature is emphasized, with coursework involving group presentations, discussions, and written analyses.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC50H3">BIOC50H3or <a href="/course/BIOC63H3">BIOC63H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD52H3">
BIOD53H3:  Special Topics in Animal Behaviour<p>Course Description</p><p>An exploration into current topics in the study of the evolutionary and ecological influences on animal behaviour. Topics may include sexual selection and conflict, social behaviour, communication, and behavioural mechanisms. Emphasis will be on current research and the quantitative and qualitative reasoning underlying our ability to understand and predict animal behaviour.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC54H3">BIOC54H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: EEB496Y, (BIO496Y)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD53H3">
BIOD54H3:  Applied Conservation Biology<p>Course Description</p><p>Canada has a complex conservation landscape. Through lectures and interactive discussions with leading Canadian conservation practitioners, this course will examine how conservation theory is put into practice in Canada from our international obligations to federal, provincial, and municipal legislation and policies.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC62H3">BIOC62H3or <a href="/course/BIOC63H3">BIOC63H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD54H3">
BIOD55H3:  Experimental Animal Behaviour<p>Course Description</p><p>A hands-on course emphasizing the logic, creative thinking, and careful methodology required to conduct rigorous research on animal behaviour from an evolutionary perspective. Students will devise and run behavioural experiments, primarily using invertebrate models.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC54H3">BIOC54H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD55H3">
BIOD59H3:  Models in Ecology, Epidemiology and Conservation<p>Course Description</p><p>Modelling is a critical tool for describing the complex dynamics of ecosystems and for addressing urgent management questions in ecology, epidemiology and conservation. In this practical introduction, students learn how to formulate ecological and epidemiological models, link them to data, and implement/analyze them using computer simulations.&nbsp; The course includes approaches for modelling individuals, populations, and communities, with applications in population viability assessments, natural resource management and food security, invasive species and pest control, disease eradication, and climate change mitigation.&nbsp; While not a requirement, some experience with computer programming will be beneficial for this course.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3and [<a href="/course/MATA29H3">MATA29H3or <a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3or <a href="/course/MATA31H3">MATA31H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD59H3">
BIOD60H3:  Spatial Ecology<p>Course Description</p><p>The study of how space and scale influence ecological patterns and species coexistence. The course will cover three main topics: 1) spatial dynamics, such as spatial spread and dispersal models; 2) species coexistence with metapopulation/metacommunity, neutral and lottery models; and 3) spatial analysis of ecological communities. Basic concepts will be applied to ecological problems such as: species invasions, reserve design and understanding threats to island biodiversity.<br>Priority will be given to students enrolled in the specialist program in Biodiversity, Ecology and Evolution.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3and <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3and [Completion of at least 0.5 credit at the C level in Biological Sciences]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD60H3">
BIOD62H3:  Symbiosis: Interactions Between Species <p>Course Description</p><p>A species is the basic unit of evolution and symbiotic interactions are integral to the rise of global biodiversity. Using a multidisciplinary approach, this course will study symbiotic systems such as plant-animal, microbe-plant, and microbe-animal interactions. This course thus provides the student with a deeper understanding of how Earth's biodiversity is maintained through natural selection.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC16H3">BIOC16H3or <a href="/course/BIOC50H3">BIOC50H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: EEB340H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD62H3">
BIOD63H3:  From Individuals to Ecosystems: Advanced Topics in Ecology<p>Course Description</p><p>This lecture/seminar course will discuss advanced topics in behavioural ecology, ecosystem and landscape ecology, and evolutionary ecology, with an emphasis on the impacts of past and present species interactions. Topics will vary based on current scientific literature and student interests. This course will strengthen the research, writing, and presentation skills of students while deepening their understanding of ecology.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB50H3">BIOB50H3and <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3and [0.5 credit from the following: <a href="/course/BIOC51H3">BIOC51H3</a>, <a href="/course/BIOC52H3">BIOC52H3</a>,  <a href="/course/BIOC54H3">BIOC54H3</a>, <a href="/course/BIOC58H3">BIOC58H3</a>, <a href="/course/BIOC59H3">BIOC59H3</a>, <a href="/course/BIOC60H3">BIOC60H3</a>, <a href="/course/BIOC61H3">BIOC61H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD63H3">
BIOD65H3:  Pathologies of the Nervous System<p>Course Description</p><p>An intensive examination of selected pathologies affecting the nervous system such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis, and stroke. These pathologies will be examined from an integrative perspective encompassing the pathogeneses, resulting symptoms, and current therapeutic approaches. This course requires critical examination of research articles.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3and [0.5 credits from the following: <a href="/course/BIOC32H3">BIOC32H3</a>, <a href="/course/NROC61H3">NROC61H3</a>, <a href="/course/NROC64H3">NROC64H3or <a href="/course/NROC69H3">NROC69H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD65H3">
BIOD66H3:  Quantitative Ecological and Biodiversity Analysis <p>Course Description</p><p>Advanced analytical skills are required for understanding and addressing the impacts of global change. This course will cover fundamental statistical approaches and techniques for analyzing biodiversity, ecological, and environmental data. In order to prepare for positions in the field, advanced analytical skills are required for addressing the impacts for global change on the environment. Students will have the opportunity to work with and develop skill sets using the R programming language.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB51H3">BIOB51H3and [Completion of at least 0.5 credit at the C level in Biological Sciences]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/BIOD66H3">
BIOD67H3:  Inter-University Biology Field Course<p>Course Description</p><p>Field courses offered by the Ontario Universities Program in Field Biology (OUPFB) in a variety of habitats and countries, usually during the summer. BIOD95H3:  Supervised Study in Biology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is designed to permit an intensive examination of the primary literature of a select topic. Frequent consultation with the supervisor is necessary and extensive library research is required. BIOD98Y3:  Directed Research in Biology<p>Course Description</p><p>A course designed to permit laboratory or field research or intensive examination of a selected topic in biology. CHMA10H3:  Introductory Chemistry I: Structure and Bonding<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will introduce the study of chemical properties and transformations of matter. The course starts with the quantum mechanical model of the atom and the principles of how the periodic table is organized. Key reaction types are explored including acid/base, redox, and precipitation as well as a quantitative description of gases. Bonding and structure in chemical compounds is examined followed by a close look at solutions, solids and intermolecular forces. The course concludes with nuclear chemistry. This course includes a three-hour laboratory every other week. </p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: CHM120H5, CHM151Y1</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: Grade 12 Chemistry and [Grade 12 Advanced Functions or Grade 12 Calculus] are highly recommended<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: [<a href="/course/MATA29H3">MATA29H3or <a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/MATA35H3">MATA35H3or <a href="/course/MATA36H3">MATA36H3</a>] are required for some higher level Physical and Environmental Sciences courses.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMA10H3">
CHMA11H3:  Introductory Chemistry II: Reactions and Mechanisms<p>Course Description</p><p>This course quantitatively examines reactions and equilibria in chemical systems with an emphasis on their thermodynamic properties and chemical kinetics. The course begins with a close examination of solutions followed by dynamic chemical equilibrium. This leads directly to acid/base and solubility equilibria and thermochemistry, including calorimetry. The course concludes with thermodynamics, kinetics and electrochemistry with a strong emphasis on the how these are connected to Gibbs Free Energy. This course includes a three hour laboratory every other week. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CHMA10H3">CHMA10H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/CHMA12H3">CHMA12H3</a>, CHM110H5, CHM135H1, CHM139H1, CHM151Y1</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: [<a href="/course/MATA29H3">MATA29H3or <a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: [<a href="/course/MATA29H3">MATA29H3or <a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/MATA35H3">MATA35H3or <a href="/course/MATA36H3">MATA36H3</a>] are required for some higher level Physical and Environmental Sciences courses.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMA11H3">
CHMA12H3:  Advanced General Chemistry<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will build on the topics from <a href="/course/CHMA10H3">CHMA10H3</a>, including a close examination of solutions, dynamic chemical equilibrium, acid/base and solubility equilibria and thermochemistry, including calorimetry and thermodynamics, kinetics and electrochemistry as they relate to Gibbs Free Energy. In this course, students will explore these ideas in more detail both from a theoretical and practical point of view, in comparison to <a href="/course/CHMA11H3">CHMA11H3</a>. The lecture portion will focus on how chemical concepts are applied in cutting edge research. The weekly laboratory period will provide students with access to the most current equipment used in both industrial and research settings as well as workshops that will explore how to analyze and extract data from published, peer-reviewed journal articles.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CHMA10H3">CHMA10H3with a grade of 70% or higher and [<a href="/course/MATA29H3">MATA29H3or <a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/CHMA11H3">CHMA11H3</a>, CHM151Y1, CHM135H1, CHM110H5</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMA12H3">
CHMB16H3:  Techniques in Analytical Chemistry<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the principles and methods of classical analysis and the provision of practical experience in analytical laboratory techniques. The course deals primarily with quantitative chemical analysis. Classical methods of volumetric analysis, sampling techniques, statistical handling of data are studied, as well as a brief introduction to spectro-chemical methods. This course includes a four hour laboratory every week.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CHMA10H3">CHMA10H3and [<a href="/course/CHMA11H3">CHMA11H3or <a href="/course/CHMA12H3">CHMA12H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/MATA29H3">MATA29H3or <a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/MATA35H3">MATA35H3or <a href="/course/MATA36H3">MATA36H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CHM211H5, CHM217H1</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMB16H3">
CHMB20H3:  Chemical Thermodynamics and Elementary Kinetics<p>Course Description</p><p>The concept of chemical potential; phase equilibria; solutions; chemical equilibria (including electrochemical applications); elementary reactions; multi-step and coupled reactions (with biochemical applications); elementary collision theory and transition state theory. This course includes a weekly tutorial.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/CHMA11H3">CHMA11H3or <a href="/course/CHMA12H3">CHMA12H3</a>] and [ <a href="/course/MATA35H3">MATA35H3or <a href="/course/MATA36H3">MATA36H3or <a href="/course/MATA37H3">MATA37H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PHYA10H3">PHYA10H3or <a href="/course/PHYA11H3">PHYA11H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/CHMB23H3">CHMB23H3</a>, CHM220H1, CHM222H1, CHM225Y1, JCP221H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Students interested in taking C-level Physical Chemistry courses should take <a href="/course/PHYA10H3">PHYA10H3instead of <a href="/course/PHYA11H3">PHYA11H3</a>. CHMB21H3:  Chemical Structure and Spectroscopy<p>Course Description</p><p>This course uses quantum mechanics to describe atomic and molecular structure and bonding. The theory of these systems is treated first and their spectroscopy afterwards. The following topics are covered: motivation for quantum mechanics, Schrödinger’s equations, quantum postulates and formalisms, solutions of the time-independent Schrödinger equation for model systems (particle in a box, harmonic oscillator, rigid rotor, hydrogen-like atoms), angular momentum operator, electron spin, many electron atoms, theories of chemical bonding (valence bond theory and molecular orbital theory), quantum mechanics of the internal motion of molecules, spectroscopy of atomic and molecular systems.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CHMB20H3">CHMB20H3or <a href="/course/CHMB23H3">CHMB23H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CHM223H1, CHM225Y1</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/MATA23H3">MATA23H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Students in the Specialist and Specialist Co-op programs in Medicinal and Biological Chemistry are advised to complete <a href="/course/CHMB23H3">CHMB23H3rather than <a href="/course/CHMB20H3">CHMB20H3prior to enrolling in <a href="/course/CHMB21H3">CHMB21H3</a>.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMB21H3">

CHMB31H3:  Introduction to Inorganic Chemistry<p>Course Description</p><p>Fundamental periodic trends and descriptive chemistry of the main group elements are covered. CHMB41H3:  Organic Chemistry I<p>Course Description</p><p>This course begins with a review of chemical bonding in organic structures, followed by an in depth look at conformational analysis and stereochemistry. It explores the reactivity of organic molecules, starting with acid-base reactions, simple additions to carbonyl compounds, reactions of alkenes and alkynes, and substitution reactions. The course includes weekly tutorials and a four hour laboratory every other week.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/CHMA11H3">CHMA11H3or <a href="/course/CHMA12H3">CHMA12H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CHM136H1, CHM138H1, CHM151Y1, CHM242H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMB41H3">
CHMB42H3:  Organic Chemistry II<p>Course Description</p><p>This course builds on the topics seen in Organic Chemistry I. Major reactions include electrophilic and nucleophilic aromatic substitutions, and the chemistry of carbonyl compounds. Spectroscopic methods for structure determination are explored (NMR, MS, IR), along with the chemistry of biologically important molecules such as heterocycles and carbohydrates. This course includes a four-hour laboratory every other week, as well as weekly one-hour tutorials.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/CHMA11H3">CHMA11H3or <a href="/course/CHMA12H3">CHMA12H3</a>] and <a href="/course/CHMB41H3">CHMB41H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CHM243H5, CHM247H1, CHM249H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMB42H3">

Topics will include the physical and chemical properties of proteins, enzymes, fatty acids, lipids, carbohydrates, metabolism and biosynthesis. Emphasis will be placed on the relationships between the chemical structure and biological function.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CHMA10H3">CHMA10H3and [<a href="/course/CHMA11H3">CHMA11H3or <a href="/course/CHMA12H3">CHMA12H3</a>] and <a href="/course/CHMB41H3">CHMB41H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/BIOC12H3">BIOC12H3and <a href="/course/BIOC13H3">BIOC13H3and BCH210H and BCH242Y and BCH311H and CHM361H and CHM362H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: This course cannot be taken by students enrolled in the Specialist Program in Medicinal and Biological Chemistry and Major Program in Biochemistry.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMB62H3">
CHMC11H3:  Principles of Analytical Instrumentation<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the workings and application of modern analytical instrumentation. A range of modern instrumentation including NMR spectroscopy, Mass Spectrometry, Microscopy. Light Spectroscopy (visible, Ultra Violet, Infrared, Fluorescence, Phosphorescence), X-ray, Chromatography and electrochemical separations will be addressed. Principles of measurement; detection of photons, electrons and ions; instrument and experiment design and application; noise reduction techniques and signal-to-noise optimization will be covered.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CHMB16H3">CHMB16H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CHM317H1, CHM311H5</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CHMB20H3">CHMB20H3and <a href="/course/CHMB21H3">CHMB21H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMC11H3">

CHMC31Y3:  Intermediate Inorganic Chemistry<p>Course Description</p><p>A detailed discussion of the structure, bonding, spectroscopy and reactivity of transition metal compounds. After an overview of descriptive chemistry, the focus is on coordination and organometallic chemistry, with an introduction to catalysis and biocoordination chemistry. The laboratory focuses on intermediate and advanced inorganic syntheses, and classical and instrumental characterization methods. This laboratory is six hours in duration and occurs every week.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CHMB16H3">CHMB16H3and [<a href="/course/CHMB20H3">CHMB20H3or <a href="/course/CHMB23H3">CHMB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/CHMB31H3">CHMB31H3and <a href="/course/CHMB42H3">CHMB42H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CHM338H and CHM331H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist programs in Medicinal and Biological Chemistry and Chemistry.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMC31Y3">
