CHMC47H3:  Bio-Organic Chemistry<p>Course Description</p><p>The chemistry of heterocycles, nucleic acids, terpenes, steroids and other natural products; amino acids, proteins and carbohydrates; introduction to enzyme structure and catalysis.<br>This course includes a four hour laboratory every week.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CHMB41H3">CHMB41H3and <a href="/course/CHMB42H3">CHMB42H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CHM347H1, CHM347H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMC47H3">
CHMC71H3:  Medicinal Chemistry<p>Course Description</p><p>The course focuses on the important concepts in the design and synthesis of drugs. The course may include the principles of pharmacology, drug metabolism and toxicology. Strategies for generating valuable active compounds and structure/activity relationships involved in selective transformations of available building blocks into diversely functionalized derivatives will be discussed. The course provides an overview of reactions used at different stages of the drug development process, using representative examples from the literature and case studies of drugs where applicable.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CHMC47H3">CHMC47H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (CHMD71H3), CHM440H1, CHM444H5</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/BIOC12H3">BIOC12H3or <a href="/course/CHMB62H3">CHMB62H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMC71H3">
CHMD11H3:  Application of Spectroscopy in Chemical Structure Determination <p>Course Description</p><p>In this course students will learn about the following analytical techniques used in organic structure determination: mass spectrometry, IR spectroscopy, NMR spectroscopy, and ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy. There will be focus on a systematic approach in structure determination through various spectroscopy. Students will receive hands-on training in spectral interpretation, processing and analysis as well as training on the use of different computer software for the purpose of analysis.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CHMB16H3">CHMB16H3and <a href="/course/CHMC11H3">CHMC11H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CHM442H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Environmental Chemistry. Additional students will be admitted as space permits.&nbsp;</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMD11H3">
CHMD16H3:  Environmental and Analytical Chemistry<p>Course Description</p><p>Students will learn about analytical techniques used in environmental chemistry, including: gas and liquid chromatography, mass spectrometry, atomic absorption, and ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy. Environmental sampling and ecotoxicology will also be covered. Students will carry out laboratory analyses and receive hands-on training with analytical instrumentation commonly used in environmental chemistry.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CHMB55H3">CHMB55H3and <a href="/course/CHMC11H3">CHMC11H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CHM317H, CHM410H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op in Environmental Chemistry. The exact topic will be announced in the Winter Session prior to the course being offered.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Permission of the instructor. CHMD41H3:  Physical Organic Chemistry<p>Course Description</p><p><span>This course offers</span><span> an in-depth understanding of organic chemistry</span><span> by </span><span>systematically explor</span><span>ing </span><span>the factors and principles that govern </span><span>o</span><span>rganic reactions. The first half of the course covers fundamentals including boding theories, kinetics, thermodynamics, transition state theory, isotope effects, </span><span>and </span><span>Hammett equations</span><span>. </span><span>In the second half, these </span><span>topics are</span><span> applied to </span><span>the </span><span>study </span><span>of </span><span>different types of organic reactions, such as nucleophilic substitution</span><span>s</span><span>, polar addition</span><span>s</span><span>/elimination</span><span>s</span><span>, pericyclic reaction</span><span>s</span><span> and radical reaction</span><span>s</span><span>.</span></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CHMB41H3">CHMB41H3and <a href="/course/CHMB42H3">CHMB42H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (CHMC41H3), CHM341H5, CHM348H1, CHM443H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMD41H3">
CHMD47H3:  Advanced Bio-Organic Chemistry<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will teach biochemical reactions in the context of Organic Chemistry. This course will build on topics from <a href="/course/CHMC47H3">CHMC47H3</a>. Application of enzymes in organic synthesis, chemical synthesis of complex carbohydrates and proteins, enzyme catalyzed proton transfer reactions and co-enzymes will be discussed in depth with recent literature examples. Experiential learning is an integral part of this course. Students will explore the applications of Bio-Organic Chemistry in healthcare and industrial settings as part of an experiential learning project</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOC12H3">BIOC12H3and <a href="/course/BIOC13H3">BIOC13H3and <a href="/course/CHMC47H3">CHMC47H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CHM447H</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CHMB20H3">CHMB20H3</a></span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMD47H3">
CHMD59H3:  Modelling the Fate of Organic Chemicals in the Environment<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces quantitative approaches to describe the behaviour of organic chemicals in the environment. Building upon a quantitative treatment of equilibrium partitioning and kinetically controlled transfer processes of organic compounds between gaseous, liquid and solid phases of environmental significance, students will learn how to build, use and evaluate simulation models of organic chemical fate in the environment. The course will provide hands-on experience with a variety of such models.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Permission of the instructor. Normally recommended for individuals who have completed 15.0 credits, including at least 1.0 credit at the C-level in CHM courses, and who are enrolled in one of the Chemistry programs.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: JNC2503H, CHE460H1</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMD59H3">
CHMD69H3:  Bioinorganic Chemistry<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will explore the role of the chemical elements other than “the big six” (C, H, O, N, P, S) in living systems, with a focus on metal cations. The topic includes geochemistry and early life, regulation and uptake of metallic elements, structure-function relationships in metalloproteins.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [[ <a href="/course/BIOC12H3">BIOC12H3and <a href="/course/BIOC13H3">BIOC13H3</a>] or <a href="/course/CHMB62H3">CHMB62H3</a>] and <a href="/course/CHMB31H3">CHMB31H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CHM333H, CHM437H</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CHMC31Y3">CHMC31Y3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CHMD69H3">

CITC02H3:  Placements in Community Development<p>Course Description</p><p>With a focus on building knowledge and skills in community development, civic engagement, and community action, students will ‘learn by doing’ through weekly community-based placements with community organizations in East Scarborough and participatory discussion and written reflections during class time. The course will explore topics such as community-engaged learning, social justice, equity and inclusion in communities, praxis epistemology, community development theory and practice, and community-based planning and organizing. Students will be expected to dedicate 3-4 hours per week to their placement time in addition to the weekly class time. Community-based placements will be organized and allocated by the course instructor.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: At least 1.5 credits at the B-level in CIT courses</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CITC01H3">CITC01H3and <a href="/course/CITC08H3">CITC08H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority enrolment is given students registered in the City Studies programs, students from other programs may request admission through the Program Advisor at <a href="mailto:cit-advisor@utsc.utoronto.ca">cit-advisor@utsc.utoronto.ca</a></span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITC02H3">
CITC03H3:  Housing Policy and Planning<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines how planning and housing policies help shape the housing affordability landscape in North American cities. The course will introduce students to housing concepts, housing issues, and the role planning has played in (re)producing racialized geographies and housing inequality (e.g., historical and contemporary forms of racial and exclusionary zoning). We will also explore planning’s potential to address housing affordability issues.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits including at least 1.5 credits at the B-level from Anthropology, City Studies, Health Studies, Human Geography, Political Science, or Sociology<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in Specialist, Major and Minor Programs in City Studies and Human Geography; and Minor in Urban Public Policy and Governance. Additional students will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITC03H3">
CITC04H3:  Current Municipal and Planning Policy and Practice in Toronto<p>Course Description</p><p>Constitutional authority, municipal corporations, official plans, zoning bylaws, land subdivision and consents, development control, deed restrictions and common interest developments, Ontario Municipal Board.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits including at least 1.5 credits at the B-level from City Studies, Human Geography, Political Science, or Sociology.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITC04H3">
CITC07H3:  Urban Social Policy<p>Course Description</p><p>In recent years social policy has been rediscovered as a key component of urban governance. This course examines the last half-century of evolving approaches to social policy and urban inequality, with particular emphasis on the Canadian urban experience. Major issues examined are poverty, social exclusion, labour market changes, housing, immigration and settlement.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits including at least 1.5 credits at the B-level from City Studies, Human Geography, Political Science, or Sociology<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/CITC10H3">CITC10H3if taken in the 2011 Winter session<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITC07H3">
CITC08H3:  Cities and Community Development<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of community development as the practice of citizens and community organizations to empower individuals and groups to improve the social and economic wellbeing of their communities and neighbourhoods. The course will consider different approaches to community development and critically discuss their potential for positive urban social change.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits including at least 1.5 credits at the B-level from City Studies, Human Geography, Political Science, or Sociology.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority enrolment is given students registered in the City Studies programs. Students from other programs may request admission through the Program Advisor at <a href="mailto:cit-advisor@utsc.utoronto.ca">cit-advisor@utsc.utoronto.ca</a></span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITC08H3">
CITC09H3:  Introduction to Planning History: Toronto and Its Region<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the study of the history of urban planning with particular emphasis on the investigation of the planning ideas, and the plans, that have shaped Toronto and its surrounding region through the twentieth century. The course will consider international developments in planning thought together with their application to Toronto and region.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits including at least 1.5 credits at the B-level from City Studies, Human Geography, Political Science, or Sociology<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITC09H3">
CITC10H3:  Selected Issues in City Studies<p>Course Description</p><p>Examination of one or more current issues in cities. The specific issues will vary depending on the instructor.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits including at least 1.5 credits at the B-level from City Studies, Human Geography, Political Science, or Sociology<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITC10H3">
CITC12H3:  City Structures, Problems, and Decisions: Field Research in Urban Policy Making <p>Course Description</p><p><span><span>Local governments are constantly making </span><span>policy </span><span>decisions that shape the lives of residents and the futures of cities. This course focuses on how these decisions get made, who has power to make them, and </span><span>their </span><span>impact </span><span>on </span><span>urban citizens. We will address how </span><span>challenges in cities are understood by city council, staff, and the public</span><span>, and how certain “policy solutions” win out over others. In the process, we will draw from both classical and contemporary theories of local government as well as </span><span>the latest</span><span> research on urban policy</span> <span>making. </span><span>We </span><span>will also </span><span>be learning</span><span> field research methods</span><span> to study</span><span> policy</span> <span>making as it happens on the ground in cites</span><span>.</span></span></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits including: <a href="/course/CITB04H3">CITB04H3and at least 1.0 other credits at the B-level from City Studies, Human Geography, Economics for Management Studies, Management, Political Science, or Sociology.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITC12H3">
CITC14H3:  Environmental Planning<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to questions of urban ecology and environmental planning, and examines how sustainability and environmental concerns can be integrated into urban planning processes and practices.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits, including at least 1.5 credits at the B-level from City Studies, Human Geography, Environmental Studies, Political Science, or Sociology<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITC14H3">
CITC15H3:  Money Matters: How Municipal Finance Shapes the City<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the role of municipal finance in shaping all aspects of urban life. Putting Canada into a comparative perspective, we look at how local governments provide for their citizens within a modern market economy and across different societies and time periods. The course also explores the relationship between municipal finance and various social problems, including movements for racial justice and the ongoing housing crisis.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits, including at least 1.5 credits at the B-level from City Studies, Human Geography, Economics for Management Studies, Management, Political Science, or Sociology<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITC15H3">
CITC16H3:  Planning and Governing the Metropolis<p>Course Description</p><p>Most of the world's population now lives in large urban regions. How such metropolitan areas should be planned and governed has been debated for over a century. Using examples, this course surveys and critically evaluates leading historical and contemporary perspectives on metropolitan planning and governance, and highlights the institutional and political challenges to regional coordination and policy development.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits, including at least 1.0 credits at the B-level from City Studies, Human Geography, Management, Political Science, or Sociology</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <br><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITC16H3">
CITC17H3:  Civic Engagement in Urban Politics<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the engagement of citizen groups, neighbourhood associations, urban social movements, and other non-state actors in urban politics, planning, and governance. The course will discuss the contested and selective insertion of certain groups into city-regional decision-making processes and structures.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits, including at least 1.5 credits at the B-level from City Studies, Human Geography, Political Science, or Sociology<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITC17H3">
CITC18H3:  Urban Transportation Policy Analysis<p>Course Description</p><p>Demand forecasting; methodology of policy analysis; impacts on land values, urban form and commuting; congestion; transit management; regulation and deregulation; environmental impacts and safety.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or equivalent] and [8.0 credits, including at least 1.5 credits at the B-level from City Studies, Human Geography, Economics for Management Studies, Management, or Political Science]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: GGR324H and (GGRC18H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITC18H3">
CITC54H3:  City Studies Field Trip Course<p>Course Description</p><p>A central focus of city studies is the attempt to understand the diversity of cities and urbanization processes globally. This course provides an opportunity to engage in field research work on a common research topic in a city outside Toronto. Students will prepare case study questions; engage in data collection including interviews, archives, and observation; networking; and case analysis in a final report.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CITB05H3">CITB05H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/GGRC54H3">GGRC54H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CITB07H3">CITB07H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITC54H3">
CITD01H3:  City Issues and Strategies<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is designed as a culminating City Studies course in which participants are able to showcase the application of their research skills, and share their professional and disciplinary interests in a common case study. Lectures and guests will introduce conceptual frameworks, core questions and conflicts. Students will be expected to actively participate in discussions and debates, and produce shared research resources. Each student will prepare a substantial research paper as a final project.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 15.0 credits; including 1.5 credits from [<a href="/course/CITB01H3">CITB01H3or <a href="/course/CITB03H3">CITB03H3or <a href="/course/CITB04H3">CITB04H3or <a href="/course/CITB07H3">CITB07H3or <a href="/course/CITB08H3">CITB08H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/CITB05H3">CITB05H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3(or equivalent)] and 0.5 credits from [<a href="/course/GGRA30H3">GGRA30H3or <a href="/course/GGRB30H3">GGRB30H3or <a href="/course/GGRC31H3">GGRC31H3or <a href="/course/GGRC42H3">GGRC42H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITD01H3">

CITD12H3:  Planning and Building Public Spaces in Toronto<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is designed to develop career-related skills such as policy-oriented research analysis, report writing, and presentation and networking skills through experiential learning approaches. The policy focus each year will be on a major current Toronto planning policy issue, from ‘Complete Streets’ to improvements to parks and public space infrastructure, to public transit-related investments. Students work closely in the course with planners and policymakers from the City of Toronto, policy advocates, and community organizers.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <span><span>15.0 credits; including 1.5 credits from [<a href="/course/CITB01H3">CITB01H3or <a href="/course/CITB03H3">CITB03H3or <a href="/course/CITB04H3">CITB04H3or <a href="/course/CITB07H3">CITB07H3or <a href="/course/CITB08H3">CITB08H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/CITB05H3">CITB05H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3(or equivalent)] and 0.5 credits from [<a href="/course/GGRA30H3">GGRA30H3or <a href="/course/GGRB30H3">GGRB30H3or <a href="/course/GGRC31H3">GGRC31H3or <a href="/course/GGRC42H3">GGRC42H3</a>]</span></span><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/CITD10H3">CITD10H3(if taken in the 2018 Fall Session and 2020 Winter session)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CITD12H3">

CLAB09H3:  Between Two Empires: The World of Late Antiquity <p>Course Description</p><p>A course to introduce students of history and classical studies to the world of late antiquity, the period that bridged classical antiquity and the Middle Ages. This course studies the period for its own merit as a time when the political structures of the Medieval period were laid down and the major religions of the Mediterranean (Judaism, Christianity, Islam, Zoroastrianism) took their recognizable forms.</p> <p>Same as <a href="/course/HISB09H3">HISB09H3</a></p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISB09H3">HISB09H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CLAA04H3">CLAA04H3</a>/<a href="/course/HISA07H3">HISA07H3The Ancient Mediterranean<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAB09H3">
CLAB20H3:  The Ancient World in Film<p>Course Description</p><p>The representation of the classical world and historical events in film. How the Greek and Roman world is reconstructed by filmmakers, their use of spectacle, costume and furnishings, and the influence of archaeology on their portrayals. Films will be studied critically for historical accuracy and faithfulness to classical sources.<br>Same as <a href="/course/HISB12H3">HISB12H3</a></p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISB12H3">HISB12H3</a>, CLA388H</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CLAA05H3">CLAA05H3or <a href="/course/CLAA06H3">CLAA06H3or (CLAA02H3) or (CLAA03H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAB20H3">
CLAC01H3:  Selected Topics in Classical Literature<p>Course Description</p><p>A detailed study of an author or a genre in Classical Literature in Translation.<br>Topics will vary from session to session and will alternate between Greek and Roman Epic, Greek and Roman Tragedy and Greek and Roman Comedy.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: One full credit in Classics or in English or another literature<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CLA300H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAC01H3">
CLAC02H3:  Selected Topics in Classical Civilization<p>Course Description</p><p>A detailed study of a theme in Classical Civilization.<br>Topics will vary from session to session and may be drawn from such areas as the archaeological history of the Roman world, Greek and Roman religion, ancient education or Roman law.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: One full credit in Classics or History<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAC02H3">
CLAC05H3:   Beyond Cleopatra: Decolonial Approaches to Ancient Egypt <p>Course Description</p><p>This course focuses on the History of ancient Egypt, with a focus on the Hellenistic to early Arab periods (4th c. BCE to 7th c. CE). Lectures will emphasize the key role played by Egypt’s diverse environments in the shaping of its socio-cultural and economic features as well as in the policies adopted by ruling authorities. Elements of continuity and change will be emphasized and a variety of primary sources and sites will be discussed. Special attention will also be dedicated to the role played by imperialism, Orientalism, and modern identity politics in the emergence and trajectory of the fields of Graeco-Roman Egyptian history, archaeology, and papyrology.<br>Same as (IEEC52H3), <a href="/course/HISC10H3">HISC10H3</a>.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 2.0 credits in CLA or HIS courses, including 1.0 credit from the following: <a href="/course/CLAA04H3">CLAA04H3</a>/<a href="/course/HISA07H3">HISA07H3or <a href="/course/CLAB05H3">CLAB05H3</a>/<a href="/course/HISB10H3">HISB10H3or <a href="/course/CLAB06H3">CLAB06H3</a>/<a href="/course/HISB11H3">HISB11H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISC10H3">HISC10H3</a>,(IEEC52H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAC05H3">
CLAC11H3:  Classical Literature I: Poetry<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of the main genres, authors and works of ancient Greek and Latin poetry, with particular emphasis on epic, drama and lyrics. Attention will be dedicated to the study of how these works reflect the socio-cultural features of Classical Antiquity and influenced later literatures. Texts will be studied in translation.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: One full credit in Classics or English</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CLAA06H3">CLAA06H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAC11H3">
CLAC12H3:  Classical Literature II: Prose<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of the main genres, authors and works of ancient Greek and Latin prose. History, rhetoric, biography, letters and the novel will be studied. Attention will be dedicated to the study of how these works reflect the socio-cultural features of Classical Antiquity and influenced later literatures. Texts will be studied in translation.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: One full credit in Classics or English</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CLAA06H3">CLAA06H3and <a href="/course/CLAC11H3">CLAC11H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAC12H3">
CLAC22H3:  Religions of the Ancient Mediterranean<p>Course Description</p><p>A comparative study of the Mesopotamian, Egyptian, Phoenician and Punic, Celtic, Palmyrene, Persian, Greco-Roman and Judeo-Christian religious beliefs and practices. Special attention will be dedicated to how they document the societies and cultures in which they flourished.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: One full credit in Classics or Religion<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CLA366H, NMC380Y</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CLAA05H3">CLAA05H3and <a href="/course/CLAA06H3">CLAA06H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAC22H3">
CLAC24H3:  Race and Ethnicity in the Ancient Mediterranean and West Asian Worlds<p>Course Description</p><p>A critical examination of multiculturalism and cultural identities in the Greek and Roman worlds. Special attention will be dedicated to the evidences through which these issues are documented and to their fundamental influence on the formation and evolution of ancient Mediterranean and West Asian societies and cultures.<br>Same as <a href="/course/HISC11H3">HISC11H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit in CLA or HIS courses.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISC11H3">HISC11H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CLAB05H3">CLAB05H3and <a href="/course/CLAB06H3">CLAB06H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAC24H3">
CLAC26H3:  Indigeneity and the Classics  <p>Course Description</p><p>This course will explore the representations and realities of Indigeneity in the ancient Mediterranean world, as well as the entanglements between modern settler-colonialism, historiography, and reception of the 'Classical' past. Throughout the term, we will be drawn to (un)learn, think, write, and talk about a series of topics, each of which pertains in different ways to a set of overarching questions: What can Classicists learn from ancient and modern indigenous ways of knowing? What does it mean to be a Classicist in Tkaronto, on the land many Indigenous Peoples call Turtle Island? What does it mean to be a Classicist in Toronto, Ontario, Canada? What does it mean to be a Classicist in a settler colony? How did the Classics inform settler colonialism? How does modern settler colonialism inform our reconstruction of ancient indigeneities? How does our relationship to the land we come from and are currently on play a role in the way we think about the ancient Mediterranean world? Why is that so? How did societies of the ancient Mediterranean conceive of indigeneity? How did those relationships manifest themselves at a local, communal, and State levels?</p> <p>Same as <a href="/course/HISC16H3">HISC16H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.0 credits, including 1.0 credit in CLA or HIS courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISC16H3">HISC16H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAC26H3">
CLAC67H3:  Early Islam: Perspectives on the Construction of a Historical Tradition<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the history and historiography of the formative period of Islam and the life and legacy of Muḥammad, Islam’s founder. Central themes explored include the Late Antique context of the Middle East, pre-Islamic Arabia and its religions, the Qur’ān and its textual history, the construction of biographical accounts of Muḥammad, debates about the historicity of reports from Muḥammad, and the evolving identity and historical conception of the early Muslim community.</p> <p>Same as <a href="/course/HISC67H3">HISC67H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.0 credits, including 0.5 credit at the A- or B-level in HIS courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISC67H3">HISC67H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAC67H3">
CLAC68H3:  Constructing the Other:  Orientalism through Time and Place<p>Course Description</p><p>This course reflects on the concept of <em>Orientalism</em> and how it informs the fields of Classical Studies and Anthropology. Topics to be discussed include the Orientalization of the past and the origin, role, and significance of ancient representations of the "Other" in contemporary discourses.<br> Same as <a href="/course/ANTC58H3">ANTC58H3and <a href="/course/HISC68H3">HISC68H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.0 credits, including 0.5 credit at the A- or B-level in ANT, HIS or CLA courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/ANTC58H3">ANTC58H3</a>, <a href="/course/HISC68H3">HISC68H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAC68H3">
CLAC94H3:  The Bible and the Qur’an<p>Course Description</p><p>The Qur'an retells many narratives of the Hebrew Bible and the New Testament. This course compares the Qur'anic renditions with those of the earlier scriptures, focusing on the unique features of the Qur'anic versions. It will also introduce the students to the history of ancient and late antique textual production, transmission of texts and religious contact. The course will also delve into the historical context in which these texts were produced and commented upon in later generations.<br>Same as <a href="/course/HISC94H3">HISC94H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.0 credits, including [[1.0 credit in Classical Studies or History] or [<a href="/course/WSTC13H3">WSTC13H3</a>]]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISC94H3">HISC94H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAC94H3">
CLAD05H3:  Dripping Histories: Water in the Ancient Mediterranean and West Asian Worlds <p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar type course addresses issues related to the relationships between ancient Mediterranean and West Asian societies and their hydric environments from 5000 BC to 600 AD.<br>Same as <a href="/course/HISD10H3">HISD10H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 11.0 credits including 2.0 credits in CLA or HIS courses.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISD10H3">HISD10H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CLAB05H3">CLAB05H3and <a href="/course/CLAB06H3">CLAB06H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAD05H3">
CLAD69H3:  Sufis and Desert Fathers: Mysticism in Late Antiquity and Early Islam <p>Course Description</p><p>This course is an introduction to mystical/ascetic beliefs and practices in late antiquity and early Islam. Often taken as an offshoot of or alternative to “orthodox” representations of Christianity and Islam, mysticism provides a unique look into the ways in which these religions were experienced by its adherents on a more popular, often non-scholarly, “unorthodox” basis throughout centuries. In this class we will examine mysticism in late antiquity and early Islam through the literature, arts, music, and dance that it inspired.</p> <p>The first half of the term will be devoted to the historical study of mysticism, its origins, its most well-known early practitioners, and the phases of its institutionalization in early Christianity and early Islam; the second part will look into the beliefs and practices of mystics, the literature they produced, the popular expressions of religion they generated, and their effects in the modern world. This study of mysticism will also provide a window for contemporary students of religion to examine the devotional practices of unprivileged members of the late antiquity religious communities, women and slaves in particular.</p> <p>Same as <a href="/course/HISD69H3">HISD69H3</a>.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 8.0 credits, including: [0.5 credit at the A- or B-level in CLA or HIS courses] and [0.5 credit at the C-level in CLA or HIS courses]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISD69H3">HISD69H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CLAB06H3">CLAB06H3</a>/<a href="/course/HISB11H3">HISB11H3</a>, <a href="/course/CLAB09H3">CLAB09H3</a>/<a href="/course/HISB09H3">HISB09H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CLAD69H3">

COPB33H3:  Passport to Placement III<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is designed to prepare students in the International Development Studies Co-op programs with the skills, tools and preparation to be successful during the placement year. Building on the skills developed in the first two years of the program, students will explore placement opportunities based on their skill set and interests. The course will include presentations from International Development Studies placement partners, group exercises, and individual assignments designed to prepare students for the placement experience. Pre-departure orientation activities will include intercultural learning, health and safety issues, placement research, and other key topics. COPB50H3:  Foundations for Success in Arts and Science Co-op<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides students in their first-year of Arts and Science Co-op to develop skills and tools to manage and thrive during the job search and in the workplace throughout the semester. In addition, students begin to build their job search tool kit, examine their strengths and areas of development, discover the skills employers are seeking in undergraduate Co-op students and in employees in general, and explore possible pathways to achieving their Co-op work terms and long term academic or career goals. Students will learn and practice strategies to best present their skills, knowledge and experience in foundational job search documents. The concept of interviewing is also introduced.</p> <p>This course is a compulsory requirement for the Arts and Science Co-op programs. Students need to pass the course before proceeding to seek for a Co-op work term, therefore, this course may be repeated.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Restricted to students in the Arts and Science Co-op programs.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/COPB10Y3">COPB10Y3</a>/(COPD07Y3), (COPB36H3); (COPD01H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Students should plan to complete this course in the first year of study in their selected Arts and Science Co-op program.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/COPB50H3">
COPB53H3:   Continuing your Co-op Job Search<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is for students in Arts &amp; Science Co-op who have undertaken a first work term search and successfully completed <a href="/course/COPB55H3">COPB55H3or <a href="/course/COPB57H3">COPB57H3or <a href="/course/COPB59H3">COPB59H3</a>, or (COPB52H3) but have not embarked on a first work term experience. Students in this course will continue with job search activities and receive additional support factoring in their overall learning.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/COPB55H3">COPB55H3or <a href="/course/COPB57H3">COPB57H3or <a href="/course/COPB59H3">COPB59H3or (COPB52H3); restricted to students in the Arts and Science Co-op programs.</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/COPB53H3">
COPB54H3:  Becoming Job Search Ready:  Science Co-op<p>Course Description</p><p>This course builds on the foundational job search concepts introduced in <a href="/course/COPB50H3">COPB50H3</a>, providing opportunities to refine application strategies and practice interviewing based on industry hiring practices for organizations hiring Science co-op students. Students experience a Co-op job search cycle by reviewing, selecting, and applying to job postings weekly and receiving feedback similar to when participating in job search. With this feedback, and the support of your Coordinator, students make adjustments to their job search approach and develop strategies for success to be used in the following terms for both job applications and interview performance. The importance of a job search network and research to tailor and prepare during your job search, as well as essential skills for success in the workplace and dynamics of workplace culture are also examined.</p> <p>This course is a compulsory requirement for the Arts and Science Co-op programs. Students need to pass the course before proceeding to seek for a Co-op work term, therefore, this course may be repeated.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/COPB50H3">COPB50H3</a>/(COPD01H3); restricted to students in the Arts and Science Co-op programs in Science Co-op programs - Life Science, Life Science (Health Sciences stream) Physical and Environmental Sciences, Physical and Environmental Sciences (Health Sciences stream), Psychological and Health Studies, and Psychological and Health Studies (Health Sciences stream).<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (COPD03H3), (COPB51H3), <a href="/course/COPB56H3">COPB56H3</a>, <a href="/course/COPB58H3">COPB58H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/COPB54H3">
COPB55H3:  Finding Your First Work Term and Getting Workplace Ready: Science Co-op<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is taken when students are searching for their first co-op work term. During this semester, Science Co-op students are applying to job postings on the Co-op Job Board, attending employer events and interviews until they secure a work term. This course provides students with topical information and insights about the labour market and Co‐op employers currently hiring, provides job search trends, job search support and coaching and in a combination of in‐class, group and one‐on‐one appointments.</p> <p>Students will learn how to onboard and transition to the workplace and navigate situations that may arise on&nbsp;work term in various sectors. Drawing upon the job search knowledge and strategies from previous co-op courses, this course is designed to provide students who are competing for a first Co‐op work term with resources and support necessary to meet their goal of securing a co-op work term and be successful in the workplace.</p> <br><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/COPB54H3">COPB54H3</a>/(COPB51H3); restricted to students in the Arts and Science Co-op programs in Science Co-op programs - Life Science, Life Science (Health Sciences stream) Physical and Environmental Sciences, Physical and Environmental Sciences (Health Sciences stream), Psychological and Health Studies, and Psychological and Health Studies (Health Sciences stream)<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (COPD11H3), (COPB52H3), <a href="/course/COPB57H3">COPB57H3</a>, <a href="/course/COPB59H3">COPB59H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/COPB55H3">
COPB56H3:  Becoming Job Search Ready:  CMS Co-op<p>Course Description</p><p>This course builds on the foundational job search concepts introduced in <a href="/course/COPB50H3">COPB50H3</a>, providing opportunities to refine application strategies and practice interviewing in various formats, based on industry hiring practices for organizations hiring Computer Science, Mathematics and Statistics co-op students. Students experience a Co-op job search cycle by reviewing, selecting, and applying to job postings weekly and receiving feedback similar to when participating in job search. With this feedback, and the support of your Coordinator, students make adjustments to their job search approach and develop strategies for success to be used in the following terms for both job applications and interview performance. The importance of a job search network and research to tailor and prepare during your job search, as well as essential skills for success in the workplace and dynamics of workplace culture are also examined.</p> <p>This course is a compulsory requirement for the Arts and Science Co-op programs. Students need to pass the course before proceeding to seek for a Co-op work term, therefore, this course may be repeated.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/COPB50H3">COPB50H3</a>/(COPD01H3); restricted to students in the Arts and Science Co-op programs in CMS Co-op programs - Computer Science, Mathematics or Statistics.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (COPD03H3), (COPB51H3), <a href="/course/COPB56H3">COPB56H3</a>, <a href="/course/COPB58H3">COPB58H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/COPB56H3">

COPC01H3:  Arts and Science Co-op Work Term 1<p>Course Description</p><p>While working full time with a Co-op employer, students receive support and guidance from Co-op coordinators, faculty and peers for success in the workplace, and to share and reflect on their work term experiences. A culminating project is completed to bring together industry and academic knowledge and showcase the work and skill development throughout each Co-op work experience. Students are enrolled into this course once hired for a first Co-op work term.<br></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/COPB55H3">COPB55H3or <a href="/course/COPB57H3">COPB57H3or <a href="/course/COPB59H3">COPB59H3or (COPB52H3) and permission from the Arts and Science Co-op Office; restricted to students enrolled in Arts and Science Co-op programs.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/COPC03H3">COPC03H3(if taken prior to 2025), <a href="/course/COPC05H3">COPC05H3(if taken prior to 2025), (COPC13H3), (COPC20H3), (COPC21H3), (COPC14H3), (COPC30H3), (COPC40H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Professional Work Term<p>Note</p>Note: Students may receive a No Credit (NCR) in previous instance of the course and Credit (CR) while in different work locations.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/COPC01H3">

COPC07H3:  Management Co-op Work Term<p>Course Description</p><p>In this course, Management Co-op students engage in a work term opportunity, which is a form of work-integrated learning, to improve their employability skills and workplace productivity by concentrating on key areas to foster their development.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/COPB10Y3">COPB10Y3</a>/(COPD07Y3) or (COPD08Y3) or [<a href="/course/COPB11H3">COPB11H3and <a href="/course/COPB12H3">COPB12H3</a>] or [<a href="/course/COPB13H3">COPB13H3and <a href="/course/COPB14H3">COPB14H3</a>]; restricted to students in the Management Co-op and/or Management International Business Co-op programs.</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Professional Work Term</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/COPC07H3">
COPC09H3:  International Development Studies Co-op Work Term<p>Course Description</p><p>The purpose of the work term placement is for students to gain experience in the professional world of development while applying knowledge gained in the classroom to real life experiences. The majority of students secure work terms with Canadian NGOs, research institutes or private sector consulting firms. Work terms are 8-12 months in length. The location and duration of the work terms will vary according to each student’s disciplinary and regional preferences, their experience and abilities, the availability of positions, and the practicability and safety of work.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/COPB33H3">COPB33H3and <a href="/course/IDSC01H3">IDSC01H3and <a href="/course/IDSC04H3">IDSC04H3</a>; restricted to students in the International Development Studies Co-op programs.</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Professional Work Term</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/COPC09H3">
COPC98H3:  Integrating Your Work Term Experience Part I<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is designed to provide students who have completed their first work term with tools and strategies to effectively integrate their recent work term experience into their job search documents, as well as practice articulating their new or enhanced skills and experience in an interview setting. Students are provided with opportunities to practice and refine their approach as they begin to seek their next Co-op work term. In class Apply Together sessions and one-on-one appointment consultations with your Work Term Engagement Coordinator will provide you with semester specific market trends, tools and resources to succeed in your job search. There are also online and in person forums for sharing work term and job search experience with junior Co-op students and peers.</p> <br><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/COPB55H3">COPB55H3</a>/(COPB52H3)and completion of one work term; restricted to students in the Arts and Science Co-op Programs.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (COPD12H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/COPC98H3">
COPC99H3:  Integrating Your Work Term Experience Part II<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is designed to provide students who have completed 2 work terms or more with tools and strategies to effectively integrate their recent work term experiences into their job search documents as well as practice articulating their new or enhanced skills and experience in an interview setting. Students are provided with opportunities to practice and refine their approach as they job search/compete for another Co-op work term. In class Apply Together sessions and one-on-one appointment consultations with your Work Term Engagement Coordinator will provide you with semester specific market trends, tools and resources to succeed in your job search. Having the experience of job searching and at least 8 months of work term experience, students share, compare, and contrast their individual experiences. There are also online and in person forums for sharing their work term and job search experience with junior Co-op students.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/COPC98H3">COPC98H3</a>/(COPD12H3) and completion of at least two work terms; restricted to students in the Arts and Science Co-op programs.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (COPD13H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Students complete this course each time they are job searching for a work term beyond their second work term.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/COPC99H3">
CRTB01H3:  Introduction to Curating Art<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the theory, ethics and contexts of art museum/gallery curatorial practice. Emphasis on communication through exploring interpretations and considering ethical practice. Students will learn specialized knowledge, resources, references and methodologies and explore professional and academic responsibilities of art-based curatorial work.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 2.0 credits at A-level<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (VPHB72H3), FAH301H5, FAH310H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: Restricted to students who have completed the A-level courses in the Major or Specialist programs in Art History, Arts Management, Studio Art, or Media Studies. Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Minor in Curatorial Studies. Additional students will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CRTB01H3">
CRTC72H3:  Art, the Museum, and the Gallery<p>Course Description</p><p>Art and the settings in which it is seen in cities today. Some mandatory classes to be held in Toronto museums and galleries, giving direct insight into current exhibition practices and their effects on viewer's experiences of art; students must be prepared to attend these classes.<br>Same as <a href="/course/VPHC72H3">VPHC72H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CRTB01H3">CRTB01H3and <a href="/course/CRTB02H3">CRTB02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/VPHC72H3">VPHC72H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CRTC72H3">
CRTC80H3:  Curator as Artist; Artist as Curator<p>Course Description</p><p>Viewed from an artist’s perspective, this course considers the exhibition as medium, and curating as a creative act. By studying the history of exhibitions organized by artists and artist collectives, this course considers their influence on contemporary curatorial practice with a focus on historical and contemporary Canadian exhibitions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CRTB01H3">CRTB01H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in programs in Curatorial Studies, Art History and Visual Culture, Arts Management, Media Studies, and Studio Art.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CRTC80H3">
CRTD43H3:  Curating Contemporary Art<p>Course Description</p><p>Curatorial practice and the responsibilities of the curator, such as the intellectual and practical tasks of producing a contemporary art exhibition, researching Canadian contemporary art and artists, building a permanent collection, administrating a public art competition, and critical writing about works of visual art in their various contexts. Studio and/or gallery visits required.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 11.0 credits including [<a href="/course/VPHB39H3">VPHB39H3and <a href="/course/CRTB01H3">CRTB01H3and <a href="/course/CRTB02H3">CRTB02H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (VPHD43H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CRTD43H3">
CRTD44H3:  Curating Historical Art<p>Course Description</p><p>Time and history bring different factors to our understanding and interpretation of artworks. Students will explore both intellectual and practical factors concerning curating historical art, from conservation, research, and handling issues to importance of provenance, collecting, and display, through workshops, critical writing and discussion, field trips, and guest speakers.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 11.0 credits including [<a href="/course/VPHB39H3">VPHB39H3</a>, <a href="/course/CRTB01H3">CRTB01H3and <a href="/course/CRTB02H3">CRTB02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (VPHD44H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CRTD44H3">
CSCA08H3:  Introduction to Computer Science I<p>Course Description</p><p>Programming in an object-oriented language such as Python. Program structure: elementary data types, statements, control flow, functions, classes, objects, methods. Lists; searching, sorting and complexity. This course is intended for students having a serious interest in higher level computer science courses, or planning to complete a computer science program.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Grade 12 Calculus and Vectors and [one other Grade 12 mathematics course or CTL Math Preparedness course with additional resources for CMS students].<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/CSCA20H3">CSCA20H3</a>, CSC108H, CSC110H, CSC120H. <a href="/course/CSCA08H3">CSCA08H3may not be taken after or concurrently with <a href="/course/CSCA48H3">CSCA48H3</a>. CSC110H cannot be taken after or concurrently with CSC111H.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning<p>Note</p>Note: This course does not require any prior exposure to computer programming.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CSCA08H3">
CSCA20H3:  Introduction to Programming<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to computer programming, with an emphasis on gaining practical skills. Introduction to programming, software tools, database manipulation. This course is appropriate for students with an interest in programming and computers who do not plan to pursue a Computer Science program.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: <a href="/course/CSCA08H3">CSCA08H3</a>, CSC108H, CSC110H, CSC120H. CSCA48H3:  Introduction to Computer Science II<p>Course Description</p><p>Abstract data types and data structures for implementing them. Linked data structures. Object Oriented Programming. Encapsulation and information-hiding. Testing. Specifications. Analyzing the efficiency of programs. Recursion.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CSCA08H3">CSCA08H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CSC148H, CSC111H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CSCA48H3">
CSCA67H3:  Discrete Mathematics<p>Course Description</p><p>Introduction to discrete mathematics: Elementary combinatorics; discrete probability including conditional probability and independence; graph theory including trees, planar graphs, searches and traversals, colouring. The course emphasizes topics of relevance to computer science, and exercises problem-solving skills and proof techniques such as well ordering, induction, contradiction, and counterexample.<br>Same as <a href="/course/MATA67H3">MATA67H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Grade 12 Calculus and Vectors and one other Grade 12 mathematics course<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (MATA67H3), (CSCA65H3), CSC165H, CSC240H, MAT102H</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CSCA08H3">CSCA08H3or <a href="/course/CSCA20H3">CSCA20H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CSCA67H3">
CSCB07H3:  Software Design<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to software design and development concepts, methods, and tools, using a statically-typed object-oriented language such as Java. Topics from: version control, build management, unit testing, refactoring, object-oriented design and development, design patterns and advanced IDE usage.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CSCA48H3">CSCA48H3and [CGPA of at least 3.5, or enrolment in a CSC Subject POSt, or enrolment in a non-CSC Subject POSt for which this specific course is a program requirement]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CSC207H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CSCB07H3">
CSCB09H3:  Software Tools and Systems Programming<p>Course Description</p><p>Software techniques in a Unix-style environment, using scripting languages and a machine-oriented programming language (typically C). What goes on in the system when programs are executed. Core topics: creating and using software tools, pipes and filters, file processing, shell programming, processes, system calls, signals, basic network programming.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CSCA48H3">CSCA48H3and [CGPA of at least 3.5, or enrolment in a CSC Subject POSt, or enrolment in a non-CSC Subject POSt for which this specific course is a program requirement]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CSC209H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CSCB09H3">
CSCB20H3:  Introduction to Databases and Web Applications<p>Course Description</p><p>A practical introduction to databases and Web app development. Databases: terminology and applications; creating, querying and updating databases; the entity-relationship model for database design. Web documents and applications: static and interactive documents; Web servers and dynamic server-generated content; Web application development and interface with databases.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Some experience with programming in an imperative language such as Python, Java or C.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: This course may not be taken after - or concurrently with - any C- or D-level CSC course.</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CSCA08H3">CSCA08H3or <a href="/course/CSCA20H3">CSCA20H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CSCB20H3">
CSCB36H3:  Introduction to the Theory of Computation<p>Course Description</p><p>Mathematical induction with emphasis on applications relevant to computer science. Aspects of mathematical logic, correctness proofs for iterative and recursive algorithms, solutions of linear and divide-and-conquer recurrences, introduction to automata and formal languages.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CSCA48H3">CSCA48H3and [(CSCA65H3) or <a href="/course/CSCA67H3">CSCA67H3</a>] and [CGPA  of at least 3.5, or enrolment in a CSC Subject POSt, or enrolment in a non-CSC Subject POSt for which this specific course is a program requirement]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CSC236H, CSC240H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CSCB36H3">
CSCB58H3:  Computer Organization<p>Course Description</p><p>Principles of the design and operation of digital computers. Binary data representation and manipulation, Boolean logic, components of computer systems, memory technology, peripherals, structure of a CPU, assembly languages, instruction execution, and addressing techniques. There are a number of laboratory periods in which students conduct experiments with digital logic circuits.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/CSCA48H3">CSCA48H3or <a href="/course/PHYB57H3">PHYB57H3</a>/(PSCB57H3)] and [CGPA of at least 3.5, or enrolment in a CSC Subject POSt, or enrolment in a non-CSC Subject POSt for which this specific course is a program requirement]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CSC258H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CSCB58H3">
CSCB63H3:  Design and Analysis of Data Structures<p>Course Description</p><p>Design, analysis, implementation and comparison of efficient data structures for common abstract data types. Priority queues: heaps and mergeable heaps. Dictionaries: balanced binary search trees, B-trees, hashing. Amortization: data structures for managing dynamic tables and disjoint sets. Data structures for representing graphs. Graph searches.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CSCB36H3">CSCB36H3and [CGPA of at least 3.5, or enrolment in a CSC Subject POSt, or enrolment in a non-CSC Subject POSt for which this specific course is a program requirement]<p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/STAB52H3">STAB52H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CSC263H, CSC265H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CSCB63H3">
CSCC01H3:  Introduction to Software Engineering<p>Course Description</p><p>Introduction to software development methodologies with an emphasis on agile development methods appropriate for rapidly-moving projects. Basic software development infrastructure; requirements elicitation and tracking; prototyping; basic project management; basic UML; introduction to software architecture; design patterns; testing.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CSCB07H3">CSCB07H3</a>, <a href="/course/CSCB09H3">CSCB09H3</a>, and [CGPA of at least 3.5, or enrolment in a CSC Subject POSt, or enrolment in a non-CSC Subject POSt for which this specific course is a program requirement]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CSC301H, (CSCC40H3), (CSCD08H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CSCC01H3">
CSCC09H3:  Programming on the Web<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to software development on the web. Concepts underlying the development of programs that operate on the web. Operational concepts of the internet and the web, static and dynamic client content, dynamically served content, n-tiered architectures, web development processes and security on the web.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CSCB09H3">CSCB09H3and [CGPA 3.5 or enrolment in a CSC Subject POSt]<p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/CSCC43H3">CSCC43H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CSC309H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CSCC09H3">
CSCC10H3:  Human-Computer Interaction<p>Course Description</p><p>The course will provide an introduction to the field of Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) with emphasis on guidelines, principles, methodologies, and tools and techniques for analyzing, designing and evaluating user interfaces. Subsequent topics include usability assessment of interactive systems, prototyping tools, information search and visualization, mobile devices, social media and social networking, and accessibility factors.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CSCB07H3">CSCB07H3and [CGPA 3.5 or enrolment in a CSC Subject POSt]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CCT380H, CSC318H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CSCC10H3">
CSCC11H3:  Introduction to Machine Learning and Data Mining<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to methods for automated learning of relationships on the basis of empirical data. Classification and regression using nearest neighbour methods, decision trees, linear and non-linear models, class-conditional models, neural networks, and Bayesian methods. Clustering algorithms and dimensionality reduction. Model selection. Problems of over-fitting and assessing accuracy. Problems with handling large databases.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MATB24H3">MATB24H3and <a href="/course/MATB41H3">MATB41H3and <a href="/course/STAB52H3">STAB52H3and [CGPA of at least 3.5 or enrolment in a CSC Subject POSt or enrolment in a non-CSC Subject POSt for which this specific course is a program requirement].<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CSC411H, (CSCD11H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/CSCC37H3">CSCC37H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CSCC11H3">
CSCC24H3:  Principles of Programming Languages<p>Course Description</p><p>Major topics in the design, definition, analysis, and implementation of modern programming languages. Study of programming paradigms: procedural (e.g., C, Java, Python), functional (e.g., Scheme, ML, Haskell) and logic programming (e.g., Prolog, Mercury).</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CSCB07H3">CSCB07H3and <a href="/course/CSCB09H3">CSCB09H3and <a href="/course/CSCB36H3">CSCB36H3and [CGPA 3.5 or enrolment in a CSC Subject POSt]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CSC324H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/CSCC24H3">

CSCC43H3:  Introduction to Databases<p>Course Description</p><p>Introduction to database management systems. The relational data model. Relational algebra. Querying and updating databases: the SQL query language. Application programming with SQL. Integrity constraints, normal forms, and database design.
