Possible topics might include science fiction, fantasy, gothic, horror, romance, children’s or young adult fiction, or comics and graphic novels.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credits at the C-level in ENG courses<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ENGD53H3">

ENGD68H3:  Topics in Literature and Religion<p>Course Description</p><p>Topics might explore the representation of religion in literature, the way religious beliefs might inform the production of literature and literary values, or literature written by members of a particular religious group.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit at C-level in ENG or FLM courses</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ENGD68H3">
ENGD71H3:  Studies in Arab North-American Literature<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of Arab North-American writers from the twentieth century to the present. Surveying one hundred years of Arab North-American literature, this course will examine issues of gender, identity, assimilation, and diaspora in poetry, novels, short stories, autobiographies and nonfiction.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit at C-level in ENG or FLM courses<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ENGD71H3">
ENGD80H3:  Women and Canadian Writing<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of the remarkable contribution of women writers to the development of Canadian writing. Drawing from a variety of authors and genres (including novels, essays, poems, autobiographies, biographies, plays, and travel writing), this course will look at topics in women and Canadian literature in the context of theoretical questions about women's writing.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit at C-level in ENG or FLM courses</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ENGB06H3">ENGB06H3or <a href="/course/ENGB07H3">ENGB07H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ENGD80H3">
ENGD84H3:  Canadian Writing in the 21st Century<p>Course Description</p><p>An analysis of features of Canadian writing at the end of the twentieth and the beginning of the twenty-first century. This course will consider such topics as changing themes and sensibilities, canonical challenges, and millennial and apocalyptic themes associated with the end of the twentieth century.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit at the C-level in ENG courses.</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ENGB06H3">ENGB06H3or <a href="/course/ENGB07H3">ENGB07H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ENGD84H3">
ENGD89H3:  Topics in the Victorian Period<p>Course Description</p><p>Topics vary from year to year and might include Victorian children's literature; city and country in Victorian literature; science and nature in Victorian writing; aestheticism and decadence; or steampunk.<br>Pre-1900 course</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit at the C-level in ENG courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ENG443Y</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ENGD89H3">
ENGD90H3:  Creative Writing: Genre Bending and Other Methods of Breaking Form<p>Course Description</p><p>Feminist scholar, Gloria Anzaldua writes in Borderlands/La Frontera, “I cannot separate my writing from any part of my life. It is all one.” In this class, students will engage with a genre-expansive survey of non-linear and experimental forms of life writing in which lived experience inspires and cultivates form. Some of these genres include flash fiction, auto-theory, auto-fiction, book length essays, ekphrasis, anti-memoir, performance texts, and many others. This course is rooted in intersectional feminist philosophy as a foundational tool for interdisciplinary practice. Throughout the semester, we will explore theoretical approaches that center decolonial literary analysis. We will pair these readings with literature that exemplifies these approaches. In this class, “the personal is political” is the fertile center for our rigorous process of writing and craft excavation.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [0.5 credit at the B-level in Creative Writing] and [0.5 credit at the C-level in Creative Writing]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ENGD90H3">
ENGD95H3:  Creative Writing as a Profession<p>Course Description</p><p>A practical introduction to the tools, skills and knowledge-base required to publish in the digital age and to sustain a professional creative writing career. Topics include: the publishing landscape, pitching creative work, and employment avenues for creative writers. Will also include a workshop component (open to all genres).</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit at the C-level in Creative Writing courses<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ENGD95H3">
ENGD98Y3:  Senior Essay and Capstone Seminar<p>Course Description</p><p>An intensive year-long seminar that supports students in the development of a major independent scholarly project. Drawing on workshops and peer review, bi-monthly seminar meetings will introduce students to advanced research methodologies in English and will provide an important framework for students as they develop their individual senior essays. Depending on the subject area of the senior essay, this course can be counted towards the Pre-1900 requirement.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Minimum GPA of 3.5 in English courses; 15.0 credits, of which at least 2.0 must be at the C-or D-level in ENG or FLM courses.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ENG490Y</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: 0.5 credit at the D-level in ENG or FLM courses</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ENGD98Y3">
ESTB01H3:  Introduction to Environmental Studies<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces the Environmental Studies major and the interdisciplinary study of the environment through a team-teaching format. Students will explore both physical and social science perspectives on the environment, sustainability, environmental problems and their solutions. Emphasis will be on critical thinking, problem solving, and experiential learning.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ESTB01H3">
ESTB02H3:  Whose Land? Indigenous-Canada-Land Relations<p>Course Description</p><p>Introduces students to the geography of Indigenous-Crown-Land relations in Canada. Beginning with pre-European contact and the historic Nation-to-Nation relationship, the course will survey major research inquiries from the Royal Commission on Aboriginal Peoples to Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women and Girls. Students will learn how ongoing land and treaty violations impact Indigenous peoples, settler society, and the land in Canada.</p> <p>Same as <a href="/course/GGRB18H3">GGRB18H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 4.0 credits, including at least 0.5 credit in ANT, CIT, EST, GGR, HLT, IDS, POL or SOC<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/GGRB18H3">GGRB18H3</a></span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ESTB02H3">
ESTB03H3:  Back to the Land: Restoring Embodied and Affective Ways of Knowing<p>Course Description</p><p>In this course students will learn about sustainability thinking, its key concepts, historical development and applications to current environmental challenges. More specifically, students will gain a better understanding of the complexity of values, knowledge, and problem framings that sustainability practice engages with through a focused interdisciplinary study of land. This is a required course for the Certificate in Sustainability, a certificate available to any student at UTSC.</p> <p>Same as <a href="/course/VPHB69H3">VPHB69H3</a>.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: <a href="/course/VPHB69H3">VPHB69H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ESTB03H3">
ESTB04H3:  Addressing the Climate Crisis<p>Course Description</p><p>Addressing the climate crisis is a profound challenge for society. This course explores climate change and what people are doing about it. This course emphasizes the human dimensions of the climate crisis. It introduces students to potential solutions, ethical and justice considerations, climate change policies and politics, and barriers standing in the way of effective action. With an emphasis on potential solutions, students will learn how society can eliminate greenhouse gas emissions through potential climate change mitigation actions and about adaptation actions that can help reduce the impacts of climate change on humans. This course is intended for students from all backgrounds interested in understanding the human dimensions of the climate crisis and developing their ability to explain potential solutions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.0 credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: GGR314H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ESTB04H3">
ESTB05H3:  Climate Science for Everyone<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides a conceptual and qualitative overview of climate science and a discussion of climate science misinformation. The course is intended to be accessible to arts and humanities students seeking to better understand and gain fluency in the physical science basis of climate change. Major topics will include the Earth’s climate system, reconstruction of past climates, factors that impact the Earth’s climate, climate measurements and models, and future climate change scenarios. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.0 credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: GGR314H1, GGR377H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority enrollment for students in the Environmental Studies Major Program in Climate Change (Arts)</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ESTB05H3">
ESTC34H3:  Sustainability in Practice<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is intended for students who would like to apply theoretical principles of environmental sustainability learned in other courses to real world problems. Students will identify a problem of interest related either to campus sustainability, a local NGO, or municipal, provincial, or federal government. Class meetings will consist of group discussions investigating key issues, potential solutions, and logistical matters to be considered for the implementation of proposed solutions. Students who choose campus issues will also have the potential to actually implement their solutions. Grades will be based on participation in class discussions, as well as a final report and presentation.<br><br>Same as <a href="/course/EESC34H3">EESC34H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 9.5 credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/EESC34H3">EESC34H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ESTC34H3">
ESTC35H3:  Environmental Science and Technology in Society<p>Course Description</p><p>In this course students will engage critically, practically and creatively with environmental controversies and urgent environmental issues from the standpoint of the sociology of science and technology (STS). This course will contribute to a better understanding of the social and political construction of environmental science and technology.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ESTB01H3">ESTB01H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Environmental Studies Program. ESTC36H3:  Knowledge, Ethics and Environmental Decision-Making<p>Course Description</p><p>Most environmental issues have many sides including scientific, social, cultural, ethical, political, and economic. ESTC37H3:  Energy and Sustainability<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will address energy systems and policy, focusing on opportunities and constraints for sustainable energy transitions. The course introduces energy systems, including how energy is used in society, decarbonization pathways for energy, and the social and political challenges of transitioning to zero carbon and resilient energy systems. Drawing on real-world case studies, students will learn about energy sources, end uses, technologies, institutions, politics, policy tools and the social and ecological impacts of energy. Students will learn integrated and interdisciplinary approaches to energy systems analysis and gain skills in imagining and planning sustainable energy futures.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 10.0 credits including <a href="/course/ESTB04H3">ESTB04H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ENV350H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ESTC37H3">
ESTC38H3:  The Anthropocene <p>Course Description</p><p>“The Anthropocene” is a term that now frames wide-ranging scientific and cultural debates and research, surrounding how humans have fundamentally altered Earth’s biotic and abiotic environment. This course explores the scientific basis of the Anthropocene, with a focus on how anthropogenic alterations to Earth’s atmosphere, biosphere, cryosphere, lithosphere, and hydrosphere, have shifted Earth into a novel geological epoch. Students in this course will also discuss and debate how accepting the Anthropocene hypothesis, entails a fundamental shift in how humans view and manage the natural world.<br>Same as <a href="/course/EESC38H3">EESC38H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ESTB01H3">ESTB01H3and [1.0 credit from the following: <a href="/course/EESB03H3">EESB03H3</a>, <a href="/course/EESB04H3">EESB04H3and <a href="/course/EESB05H3">EESB05H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/EESC38H3">EESC38H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ESTC38H3">
ESTC40H3:  Technical Methods for Climate Change Mitigation <p>Course Description</p><p>Addressing the climate crisis requires designing and implementing effective climate change mitigation targets, strategies, policies and actions to eliminate human-caused greenhouse gas emissions. In this course, students will learn the various technical methods required in climate change mitigation. Students will explore the opportunities, barriers, and tools that exist to implement effective climate change mitigation in the energy, industry, waste, and agriculture, forestry and land-use sectors. The emphasis of the course is on the technical methods that climate change mitigation experts require.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 10.0 credits including <a href="/course/ESTB04H3">ESTB04H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ESTC40H3">

ESTD17Y3:  Cohort Capstone Course in Environmental Studies<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is designed to provide a strong interdisciplinary focus on specific environmental problems including the socioeconomic context in which environmental issues are resolved. ESTD19H3:  Risk<p>Course Description</p><p>A practical introduction to the concept of 'risk' as utilized in environmental decision-making. Students are introduced to risk analysis and assessment procedures as applied in business, government, and civil society. Three modules take students from relatively simple determinations of risk (e.g., infrastructure flooding) towards more complex, real-world, inclusive considerations (e.g., ecosystem impacts of climate change).</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 14.5 credits and <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3(or equivalent)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ESTD19H3">
ESTD20H3:  Integrated Natural Resource and Climate Change Governance <p>Course Description</p><p>Climate change affects all sectors of society, natural ecosystems, and future generations. Addressing climate change, either in terms of mitigation or adaptation, is complex due to its pervasive scope, the heterogeneity of its impacts and the uneven distribution of responsibilities, resources and capacities to respond to it between different levels of government, stakeholder groups, and rightholder groups. This course focuses on nexus approaches in climate policy development and assessment across different public policy domains. In this course, students will learn about how different levels of government frame climate change and climate policy objectives, how they interact with stakeholders (e.g., economic interests and environmental groups) and rightholders (Indigenous people), and how to approach complexity in climate governance. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 14.0 credits including <a href="/course/ESTB04H3">ESTB04H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/ESTD20H3">
FLMA70H3:  How to Read a Film <p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the critical study of cinema, including films from a broad range of genres, countries, and eras, as well as readings representing the major critical approaches to cinema that have developed over the past century.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: INI115Y, (ENGB70H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMA70H3">
FLMB71H3:  Writing About Movies<p>Course Description</p><p>In this course, students will learn to write critically about movies. We will watch movies and read film criticism, learning to write about film for various audiences and purposes. Forms of writing covered will include movie reviews, blogs, analytical essays, and research-based essays. This is a writing-intensive course that will include revision and peer review. Students will learn how to write academic essays about movies, while also learning about the goals and tools for writing about film for other audiences and venues.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: CIN369H1, (ENGB71H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/FLMA70H3">FLMA70H3</a>/(ENGB70H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMB71H3">
FLMB75H3:  Cinema and Modernity<p>Course Description</p><p>An investigation of film genres such as melodrama, <em>film noir,</em> and the western from 1895 to the present alongside examples of twentieth-century prose and poetry. We will look at the creation of an ideological space and of new mythologies that helped organize the experience of modern life.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (ENGB75H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMB75H3">
FLMB77H3:  Cinema and Colonialism<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to cinema’s relationship to colonialism, decolonization, and postcolonialism. How has film constructed, perpetuated, and challenged colonial logic? We will explore this question by examining colonial cinema, ethnography, Hollywood genres, anti-colonial film, and postcolonial film practices.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: <a href="/course/HISC08H3">HISC08H3</a>, VCC306H5, (ENGB77H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/FLMA70H3">FLMA70H3</a>/(ENGB70H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language, History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in p<span><span>rograms from the Department of English.</span></span></span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMB77H3">
FLMB80H3:  Cinema, Race, and Representation<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines representations of race in cinema, focusing on methods for analyzing the role of race in the politics and aesthetics of various cinematic modes. Topics may include: ideology, stereotypes, representation, dominant and counter-cinemas, cultural hegemony, and popular culture. Contemporary and classic films will be studied through the lens of race and representation.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: CIN332Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMB80H3">
FLMC44H3:  Self and Other in Literature and Film<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of the relation between self and other in narrative fiction. This course will examine three approaches to the self-other relation: the moral relation, the epistemological relation, and the functional relation. Examples will be chosen to reflect engagements with gendered others, with historical others, with generational others, and with cultural and national others.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 6.0 credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ENGC44H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: [<a href="/course/ENGA01H3">ENGA01H3and <a href="/course/ENGA02H3">ENGA02H3</a>] or <a href="/course/FLMA70H3">FLMA70H3</a>/(ENGB70H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMC44H3">
FLMC55H3:  The Documentary Film<p>Course Description</p><p>This study of films from major movements in the documentary tradition including ethnography, cinéma vérité, social documentary, personal documentary, and "reality television". FLMC56H3:  Literature and Media: From Page to Screen<p>Course Description</p><p>An exploration of the relationship between written literature and film and television. What happens when literature influences film and vice versa, and when literary works are recast as visual media (including the effects of rewriting, reproduction, adaptation, serialization and sequelization)?</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 6.0 credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ENGC56H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: [<a href="/course/ENGA01H3">ENGA01H3and <a href="/course/ENGA02H3">ENGA02H3</a>] or <a href="/course/FLMA70H3">FLMA70H3</a>/(ENGB70H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMC56H3">
FLMC75H3:  Freaks and Geeks: Children in Contemporary Film and Media<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will look at the depiction of childhood and youth in contemporary film and television, especially focusing on films that feature exceptional, difficult, or magical children. The course will explore how popular culture represents children and teens, and how these films reflect cultural anxieties about parenting, childhood, technology, reproduction, disability and generational change. Films and television shows may include: Mommy, The Babadook, Boyhood, Girlhood, A Quiet Place, We Need to Talk About Kevin, The Shining, Looper, Elephant, Ready Player One, Stranger Things, Chappie, Take Shelter, and Moonlight.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 6.0 credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ENGC75H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ENGA01H3">ENGA01H3and <a href="/course/ENGA02H3">ENGA02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMC75H3">
FLMC78H3:  Dystopian Visions in Fiction and Film<p>Course Description</p><p>An exploration of negative utopias and post-apocalyptic worlds in film and literature. The course will draw from novels such as <em>1984</em>, <em>Brave New World</em>, <em>Clockwork Orange</em>, and <em>Oryx and Crake</em>, and films such as <em>Metropolis, Mad Max</em>, <em>Brazil,</em> and <em>The Matrix</em>. Why do we find stories about the world gone wrong so compelling?</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 6.0 credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ENGC78H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: [<a href="/course/ENGA01H3">ENGA01H3and <a href="/course/ENGA02H3">ENGA02H3</a>] or <a href="/course/FLMA70H3">FLMA70H3</a>/(ENGB70H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMC78H3">
FLMC81H3:  Topics in National Cinemas<p>Course Description</p><p>This is a course on the nation as a framework for film analysis. The topic will be the cinema of a single nation, or a comparison of two or more national cinemas, explored from several perspectives: social, political, and aesthetic. The course will look at how national cinema is shaped by and in turn shapes the cultural heritage of a nation. The course will also consider how changing definitions of national cinema in Film Studies have shaped how we understand film history and global film culture.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/FLMA70H3">FLMA70H3or (ENGB70H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/FLMB71H3">FLMB71H3</a>/(ENGB71H3) or <a href="/course/FLMB77H3">FLMB77H3</a>/(ENGB77H3) or <a href="/course/FLMB80H3">FLMB80H3</a>/(ENGB80H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language, History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies<p>Note</p>Note: Priority for students enrolled in programs in the Department of English, including the Literature and Film Minor and the Film Studies Major.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMC81H3">
FLMC82H3:  Topics in Cinema Studies <p>Course Description</p><p>A variable theme course that will feature different theoretical approaches to Cinema: feminist, Marxist, psychoanalytic, postcolonial, and semiotic. Thematic clusters include "Madness in Cinema," and "Films on Films."</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 6.0 credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ENGC82H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: [<a href="/course/ENGA01H3">ENGA01H3and <a href="/course/ENGA02H3">ENGA02H3</a>] or <a href="/course/FLMA70H3">FLMA70H3</a>/(ENGB70H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMC82H3">
FLMC83H3:  World Cinema<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of Non-Western films. This course analyzes a selection of African, Asian, and Middle Eastern films both on their own terms and against the backdrop of issues of colonialism and globalization.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 6.0 credits or [<a href="/course/SOCB58H3">SOCB58H3</a>, and an additional 4.0 credits, and enrolment in the Minor in Culture, Creativity, and Cities]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ENGC83H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: [<a href="/course/ENGA01H3">ENGA01H3and <a href="/course/ENGA02H3">ENGA02H3</a>] or <a href="/course/FLMA70H3">FLMA70H3</a>/(ENGB70H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMC83H3">
FLMC84H3:  Cinema and Migration<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to cinema by, and about, immigrants, refugees, migrants, and exiles. Using a comparative world cinema approach, the course explores how the aesthetics and politics of the cinema of migration challenge theories of regional, transnational, diasporic, and global cinemas.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 6.0 credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ENGC84H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ENGA01H3">ENGA01H3and <a href="/course/ENGA02H3">ENGA02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMC84H3">
FLMC92H3:  Film Theory<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the major theorists and schools of thought in the history of film theory, from the early 20th century to our contemporary moment. What is our relationship to the screen? How do movies affect our self-image? How can we think about the power and politics of the moving image? We will think about these questions and others by watching movies in conjunction with theoretical texts touching on the major approaches to film theory over the last century.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 6.0 credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CIN301Y, (ENGC92H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ENGA01H3">ENGA01H3and <a href="/course/ENGA02H3">ENGA02H3and 0.5 credit in FLM courses<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMC92H3">
FLMC93H3:  Gender and Sexuality at the Movies<p>Course Description</p><p><span>This course is a study of gender and sexuality in cinema. What happens when we watch bodies on screen? Can cinema change the way we understand gender and sexuality? We explore these questions in relation to topics including feminist film theory, LGBTQ2S+ film cultures, women’s cinema, and queer theory.</span></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/FLMA70H3">FLMA70H3or (ENGB70H3)<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CIN336H1, CIN330Y1, (ENGC93H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language, History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMC93H3">
FLMC94H3:  Women Directors<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of select women filmmakers and the question of women's film authorship. Emphasis may be placed on the filmography of a specific director, or on film movements in which women filmmakers have made major contributions. Aspects of feminist film theory, critical theory, and world cinema will be considered, as well as the historical context of women in film more generally. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <span>A</span><span>ny 6.0 credits</span><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CIN330Y1, (ENGC94H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/FLMA70H3">FLMA70H3</a>/(ENGB70H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in programs from the Department of English.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMC94H3">
FLMC95H3:  Indian Cinemas: Bollywood, Before and Beyond<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will introduce students to various film cultures in India, with a focus on Bollywood, the world's largest producer of films. The readings will provide an overview of a diverse range of film production and consumption practices in South Asia, from popular Hindi films to 'regional' films in other languages. This is an introductory course where certain key readings and films will be selected with the aim of helping students develop their critical writing skills. These course materials will help students explore issues of aesthetics, politics and reception across diverse mainstream, regional and art cinema in the Indian subcontinent.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 6.0 credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ENGC95H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/ENGA10H3">ENGA10H3and <a href="/course/ENGA11H3">ENGA11H3and <a href="/course/ENGB19H3">ENGB19H3and <a href="/course/FLMA70H3">FLMA70H3</a>/(ENGB70H3) and <a href="/course/FLMB77H3">FLMB77H3</a>/(ENGB77H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMC95H3">
FLMD52H3:  Cinema: The Auteur Theory<p>Course Description</p><p>An exploration of the genesis of auteur theory. By focusing on a particular director such as Jane Campion, Kubrick, John Ford, Cronenberg, Chaplin, Egoyan, Bergman, Godard, Kurosawa, Sembene, or Bertolucci, we will trace the extent to which a director's vision can be traced through their body of work.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit at C-level in ENG or FLM courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: INI374H, INI375H, (ENGD52H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMD52H3">
FLMD62H3:  Topics in Postcolonial Literature and Film<p>Course Description</p><p>An exploration of multicultural perspectives on issues of power, perception, and identity as revealed in representations of imperialism and colonialism from the early twentieth century to the present.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit at C-level in ENG or FLM courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ENGD62H3)</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMD62H3">
FLMD91H3:  Avant-Garde Cinema<p>Course Description</p><p>An exploration of Avant-Garde cinema from the earliest experiments of German Expressionism and Surrealism to our own time. The emphasis will be on cinema as an art form aware of its own uniqueness, and determined to discover new ways to exploit the full potential of the "cinematic".</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit at C-level in ENG or FLM courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: INI322Y, (ENGD91H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMD91H3">
FLMD93H3:  Theoretical Approaches to Cinema<p>Course Description</p><p>Advanced study of theories and critical questions that inform current directions in cinema studies.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit at C-level in ENG or FLM courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: INI214Y, (ENGD93H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: Additional 0.5 credit at the B- or C-level in FLM courses<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMD93H3">
FLMD94H3:  Theories and Practices of Documentary Film<p>Course Description</p><p>The study of theories, methodologies, and artistic approaches to documentary film. This course takes a critical and creative approach to the study of documentaries and includes a hands-on video production component. Advanced theoretical readings and topics will explore the tensions between reality and representation, art and politics, technology and narrative, film and audience, and trust and exploitation in documentary filmmaking.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit at C-level in ENG or FLM courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ENGD94H3), CIN312Y1</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: Additional 0.5 credit at the B- or C-level in FLM courses or FLMC55<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMD94H3">
FLMD96H3:  Iranian Cinema<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the development of Iranian cinema, particularly experimental and art cinema. Questions of form, and the political and social dimensions of cinema, will be considered alongside the theory of national cinemas. The course places Iranian cinema in a global context by considering it with other national cinemas.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 0.5 credit at the B- or C-level in FLM courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ENGD96H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Minor Program in Film Studies.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FLMD96H3">
FREA01H3:  Language Practice I<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is designed to consolidate the language skills necessary for higher-level French courses through an action-oriented approach to language teaching and learning. Students will improve their communicative language competencies (listening, reading, speaking and writing) by engaging in task-based activities in real-world, contextual situations.</p> <p>By the end of <a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>, students will have completed the level A2 of the Common European Framework of Reference.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Grade 12 French or <a href="/course/FREA91Y3">FREA91Y3or <a href="/course/FREA99H3">FREA99H3or equivalent<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: Native or near-native fluency in French, (FSL161Y), (FSL181Y), FSL221Y, FSL220H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: <a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3is a prerequisite for all B-level French courses.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREA01H3">

FREA90Y3:  Intensive Introductory French <p>Course Description</p><p>This course is for students with no prior knowledge of French. Based on a communicative approach, it will help students learn French vocabulary, understand grammatical structures and concepts, and gain oral and written communication skills. Students will develop their listening, speaking, reading and writing skills through a variety of contextually specific activities. Class periods will include explanations of grammatical concepts, as well as communicative and interactive exercises. In addition to preparation at home, regular class attendance is paramount for student success.</p> <br></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (LGGA21H3), (LGGA22H3), (LGGB23H3), (LGGB24H3), <a href="/course/FREA96H3">FREA96H3</a>, <a href="/course/FREA97H3">FREA97H3</a>, [FSL100H1 or equivalent], or any prior knowledge of French<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: 1. This course does not satisfy any French program requirements. It is a 6 week, 1.0 credit course that will be offered only in the Summer semesters as part of the Summer Language Institute. Students will be expected to attend up to 12 hours of class per week. 
2. Priority will be given to students in the Specialist Co-op program in Management and International Business (MIB), Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Linguistics, and Specialist Co-op programs in International Development Studies (both BA and BSc).</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREA90Y3">

FREB01H3:  Language Practice III<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is designed to reinforce and develop fluency, accuracy of expression and style through an action-oriented approach to language teaching and learning. Students will improve their communicative language competencies (listening, reading, speaking and writing) by engaging in task-based activities in real-world, contextual situations.</p> <p>By the end of <a href="/course/FREB01H3">FREB01H3and <a href="/course/FREB02H3">FREB02H3</a>, students will have completed the level B1 of the Common European Framework of Reference.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <span><span> </span></span><span><span>FSL224H1, FSL225H1, </span></span>(FSL261Y), (FSL281Y), FSL321Y, (FSL331Y), (FSL341Y) or equivalent or native proficiency<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB01H3">
FREB02H3:  Language Practice IV<p>Course Description</p><p>A continuation of <a href="/course/FREB01H3">FREB01H3</a>. Students will continue to develop their accuracy of expression and improve their fluency by engaging in activities in real-world, contextual situations.</p> <p>By the end of <a href="/course/FREB01H3">FREB01H3and <a href="/course/FREB02H3">FREB02H3</a>, students will have completed the level B1 of the Common European Framework of Reference.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/FREB01H3">FREB01H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FSL320H1, (FSL261Y), (FSL281Y), FSL321Y, (FSL331Y), (FSL341Y) or equivalent or native proficiency<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB02H3">
FREB08H3:  Practical Translation I<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to translation. The course will use a wide selection of short texts dealing with a variety of topics. Grammatical and lexical problems will be examined with special attention to interference from English.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: Native proficiency. <a href="/course/FREB08H3">FREB08H3may not be taken after or concurrently with <a href="/course/FREC18H3">FREC18H3</a>, FRE480Y or FRE481Y.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB08H3">
FREB11H3:  French Language in the School System<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is intended for students considering a career in language teaching.<br>It involves a series of seminars as well as preparation for observations in local schools throughout the duration of the course.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: Students taking this course will need to have a police check completed with the police board in the jurisdiction for which they reside. FREB17H3:  Spoken French: Conversation and Pronunciation<p>Course Description</p><p>Designed for students who wish to improve their speaking abilities. The course examines the French sound system with the goal of improving students' pronunciation in reading and everyday speech. Theoretical concepts are put into practice via structured exercises and various dialogues involving useful colloquial expressions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/FREC01H3">FREC01H3</a>, <a href="/course/FREC02H3">FREC02H3</a>, <a href="/course/FRED01H3">FRED01H3</a>, <a href="/course/FRED06H3">FRED06H3</a>; and any Francophone students<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB17H3">
FREB18H3:  Business French<p>Course Description</p><p>The French language in a commercial or economic context. Of interest, among others, to students in French, Business, Accounting, Management, and Economics, this course emphasizes commercial writing techniques and exercises that include the vocabulary and structures of business language. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FSL366H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB18H3">
FREB20H3:  Teaching Children's Literature in French<p>Course Description</p><p>An analysis of the varied forms and contents of children's literature written in French. The course examines different texts in terms of target age, pictorial illustrations, didactic bent, socio-cultural dimensions etc., focusing on, among other things, fairy tales urban and otherwise, cartoons, detective stories, adventure tales, and art, science and history books. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FRE385H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB20H3">
FREB22H3:  The Society and Culture of Québec<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of the historical, cultural and social development of Québec society from its origins to today. Aspects such as history, literature, art, politics, education, popular culture and cinema will be examined. Emphasis will be placed on the elements of Québec culture and society that make it a distinct place in North America.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB22H3">
FREB27H3:  Modern France<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of political, social and cultural developments in France in the last hundred years. Topics will include: the impact of two World Wars; the decolonization process; the European Community; the media; the educational system; immigration etc.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB27H3">
FREB28H3:  The Francophone World<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of historical, political and cultural realities in different parts of the Francophone world excluding France and Canada. Topics to be discussed will include slavery, colonization, de-colonization and multilinguism.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FSL362Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB28H3">
FREB35H3:  Francophone Literature<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of a variety of literary texts from the French-speaking world, excluding France and Canada. Attention will be given to the cultural and historical background as well as to the close study of works from areas including the West Indies, North and West Africa.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FRE332H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB35H3">
FREB36H3:  The 20th Century Quebec Novel<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of some of the major novels written in Québec since 1945. The course will focus on the evolution of the novelistic form and its relevance within modern Western literature. We will also examine the link between the novels studied and the transformation of Québec society.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FRE210Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB36H3">
FREB37H3:  Contemporary Quebec Drama<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of contemporary Québec theatre. We will study texts representative of a variety of dramatic styles. The focus will be primarily on dramatic texts; significant theatrical performances, however, will also be considered.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FRE312H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB37H3">
FREB44H3:  Introduction to Linguistics: French Phonetics and Phonology<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of the sound system of modern French. The course will acquaint student with acoustic phonetics and the basic concept and features of the French phonetic system. Phonological interpretation of phonetic data (from speech samples) and prosodic features such as stress and intonation will be examined.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (FRE272Y), FRE272H, FRE274H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB44H3">
FREB45H3:  Introduction to Linguistics: French Morphology and Syntax<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of the internal structure of words and sentences in French.<br> Covered are topics including word formation, grammatical categories, syntactic structure of simple and complex clauses, and grammatical relations of subject, predicate and complement.<br>This course complements (FREB43H3) and <a href="/course/FREB44H3">FREB44H3</a>.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (FRE272Y) and FRE272H and FRE274H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB45H3">
FREB46H3:  History of the French Language<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the origin and development of French, from the Latin of the Gauls to current varieties of the language. The course examines the internal grammatical and phonological history undergone by the language itself as well as the external history which includes ethnic, social, political, technological, and cultural changes.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FRE273H, FRE372H, FRE373H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB46H3">
FREB50H3:  Introduction to Literature in French I<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of representative texts from the three major literary genres (fiction, drama, poetry). The course will introduce students to the critical reading of literary texts in French; students will acquire the basic concepts and techniques needed to analyze literature.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/FREB01H3">FREB01H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FRE240Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: <a href="/course/FREB50H3">FREB50H3is a pre-requisite for all other French Literature courses at the C-, and D-level.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB50H3">
FREB51H3:  Literary History in Context: From the Middle Ages to the 17th Century<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of the evolution of the major trends of French literature from the Middle Ages to the 17th century through representative texts (short novels, poetry and short stories) selected for their historical relevance and literary importance.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FRE250Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB51H3">
FREB55H3:  Literary History in Context: 18th and 19th Centuries<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of the evolution of the major trends of French literature from the 18th and 19th centuries through representative texts (short stories, poetry and novels), selected for their historical relevance and literary importance. Students will also learn to use some tools required for text analysis and will apply them in context.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FRE250Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB55H3">
FREB70H3:  Introduction to Film Analysis in French<p>Course Description</p><p><span><span>This course introduces students to the fundamental aspects of the language of cinema. By examining important films from the French-speaking world, students will learn to analyse the composition of shots and sequences, the forms of expression used in cinematographic language, film editing and certain aspects of filmic narrative.</span></span><span> </span></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB70H3">
FREB84H3:  Folktale, Myth and the Fantastic in the French-Speaking World<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of the imagined/imaginative in cultures and belief systems in the francophone world. Myths and folktales from Canada, the U.S., French Guyana, North and West Africa will be examined in terms of form, function, psychological dimensions and cultural interpretations of, for instance, life, death, food and individualism. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREA01H3">FREA01H3and <a href="/course/FREA02H3">FREA02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREB84H3">
FREC01H3:  Language Practice V<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is designed to hone students’ reading, writing, listening and speaking skills through group work, written projects, oral presentations and robust engagement with authentic materials. Students will improve their communicative language competencies by participating in activities in real-world, contextual situations.</p> <p>By the end of <a href="/course/FREC01H3">FREC01H3and <a href="/course/FREC02H3">FREC02H3</a>, students will be closer to the level B2 of the Common European Framework of Reference.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/FREB01H3">FREB01H3and <a href="/course/FREB02H3">FREB02H3</a>] or equivalent.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FSL322H1, (FSL361Y), (FSL382H), (FSL383H), FSL421Y, FSL431Y or equivalent.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/FREC01H3">
