MDSC20H3:  Advanced Studies in Media and Society<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar provides students with a theoretical toolkit to understand, analyze and evaluate media-society relations in the contemporary world. Students will, through reading and writing, become familiar with social theories that intersect with questions and issues related to media production, distribution and consumption. These theories range from historical materialism, culturalism, new materialism, network society, public sphere, feminist and queer studies, critical race theory, disability media theories, and so on. Special attention is paid to the mutually constitutive relations between digital media and contemporary societies and cultures.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Enrollment in the Major program in Media and Communication Studies, and 3.0 credits at MDS B-level and a minimum GPA of 3.3 in MDS A-, B- and C-level courses<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MDSC20H3">
MDSC21H3:  Anthropology of Language and Media<p>Course Description</p><p>Anthropology studies language and media in ways that show the impact of cultural context. This course introduces this approach and also considers the role of language and media with respect to intersecting themes: ritual, religion, gender, race/ethnicity, power, nationalism, and globalization. Class assignments deal with lectures, readings, and students' examples.<br> Same as <a href="/course/ANTC59H3">ANTC59H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/ANTB19H3">ANTB19H3and <a href="/course/ANTB20H3">ANTB20H3or any 4.0 credits in MDS<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (MDSB02H3), (ANTB21H3), <a href="/course/ANTC59H3">ANTC59H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MDSC21H3">
MDSC22H3:  Understanding Scandals<p>Course Description</p><p>This course focuses on modern-day scandals, ranging from scandals of politicians, corporate CEOs, and celebrities to scandals involving ordinary people. It examines scandals as conditioned by technological, social, cultural, political, and economic forces and as a site where meanings of deviances of all sorts are negotiated and constructed. It also pays close attention to media and journalistic practices at the core of scandals. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [Enrolment in the Major program in Media and Communication Studies and 3.0 credits at the MDS B-level] or [Enrolment in the Minor program in Media Studies and 2.0 credits at the MDS B-level<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: SOC342H5, (MDSC35H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MDSC22H3">
MDSC23H3:  Black Media Studies<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores Black media production, representation, and consumption through the analytical lenses of Black diaspora studies, critical race studies, political economy of media and more. Themes include, Black media histories, radical traditions, creative expression, and social movements. Students will explore various forms of media production created and influenced by Black communities globally. The course readings and assignments examine the interconnection between the lived cultural, social, and historical experiences of the African diaspora and the media artefacts they create as producers, or they are referenced as subjects. Students will critically examine media artefacts (music, television shows, movies, social media content) through various lenses, including race and gender theory, rhetoric, visual communication, and digital media analysis.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [Enrolment in the Major program in Media and Communication Studies - Media Studies stream and 3.0 credits at the MDS B-level] or [Enrolment in the Major program in Media and Communication Studies - Journalism stream and 3.0 credits at the MDS B-level/JOU B-level] or [Enrolment in the Minor program in Media Studies and 2.0 credits at the MDS B-level]</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MDSC23H3">
MDSC24H3:  Selfies and Society<p>Course Description</p><p>Selfies are an integral component of contemporary media culture and used to sell everyone from niche celebrities to the Prime Minister. This class examines the many meanings of selfies to trace their importance in contemporary media and digital cultures as well as their place within, and relationship to, historically and theoretically grounded concepts of photography and self portraiture.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [Enrolment in the Major program in Media and Communication Studies and 3.0 credits at the MDS B-level] or [Enrolment in the Minor in Media Studies and 2.0 credits at the MDS B-level]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (MDSC66H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MDSC24H3">
MDSC25H3:  Understanding Audiences in the Digital Age<p>Course Description</p><p>Understanding the interests and goals of audiences is a key part of media production. This course introduces communication research methods including ratings, metrics, in-depth interviews, and focus groups. The focus of class discussion and research project is to use these methods to be able to understand the nature of audiences’ media use in the digital age. </p> <p>Same as <a href="/course/JOUC80H3">JOUC80H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [2.0 credits at the B-level in MDS courses] or [2.0 credits at the B-level in JOU courses] or [4.5 credits from the Major (Joint) program in New Media Studies Group I and Group II courses]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/JOUC80H3">JOUC80H3</a>, (MDSC80H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MDSC25H3">
MDSC26H3:  Media, Technology &amp; Disability Justice <p>Course Description</p><p>This course will examine Critical Disability Studies as it intersects with and informs Media Studies and Science &amp; Technology Studies with a focus on the advancement of disability justice goals as they relate to topics that may include: interspecies assistances and co-operations, military/medical technologies that enhance "ability," the possibilities and limitations of cyborg theory for a radical disabilities politics and media practice informed by the disability justice ethics of “nothing about us without us.”</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [Enrolment in the Major program in Media and Communication Studies and 3.0 credits at the MDS B-level including <a href="/course/MDSB21H3">MDSB21H3</a>] or [Enrolment in the Minor program in Media Studies and 2.0 credits at the MDS B-level including <a href="/course/MDSB21H3">MDSB21H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MDSC26H3">

MDSC28H3:  Data and Artificial Intelligence<p>Course Description</p><p>The course explores critical data studies and considers critical understandings of artificial intelligence, with a focus on topics that may include algorithmic fairness, data infrastructures, AI colonialism, algorithmic resistance, and interplays between race/gender/sexuality issues and data/artificial intelligence.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [3.0 credits at MDS B-level and enrolment in Major program in Media and Communication Studies - Media Studies stream] or [3.0 credits at MDS B-level/JOU B-level and enrolment in Major program in Media and Communication Studies - Journalism Studies stream] or [2.0 credits at the MDS B-level and enrolment in the Minor program in Media Studies]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MDSC28H3">
MDSC29H3:  Media and Religion<p>Course Description</p><p>The advancement of religious concepts and movements has consistently been facilitated - and contested - by contemporaneous media forms, and this course considers the role of media in the creation, development, and transmission of religion(s), as well as the challenges posed to modern religiosities in a digital era.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 2.0 credits at the B-level in MDS courses<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MDSC29H3">

MDSC31H3:  Platforms and Cultural Production<p>Course Description</p><p>This course focuses on the process of platformization and how it impacts cultural production. It provides an introduction into the fields of software, platform, and app studies. MDSC37H3:  Media, Journalism and Digital Labour<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores themes of labour in <em>news</em> media and <em>new</em> media. This course is critical to the education of students preparing for a career in accounting.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGAC01H3">MGAC01H3and <a href="/course/MGAC02H3">MGAC02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGAD50H3">
MGAD60H3:  Controversial Issues in Accounting<p>Course Description</p><p>Through case analysis and literature review, this seminar addresses a variety of controversial reporting issues, impression management, the politics of standard setting and the institutional context. Topics may include: international harmonization, special purpose entities, whistle-blowing, the environment and social responsibility and professional education and career issues. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGAC01H3">MGAC01H3and <a href="/course/MGAC02H3">MGAC02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGAD60H3">
MGAD65H3:  Canadian Income Taxation II<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is designed to give the student an understanding of the more complex issues of federal income taxation, by applying current tax law to practical problems and cases. Topics include: computation of corporate taxes, corporate distributions, corporate re-organizations, partnerships, trusts, and individual and corporate tax planning.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGAC50H3">MGAC50H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: MGT429H5, RSM424H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGAD65H3">
MGAD70H3:  Advanced Accounting Case Analysis: A Capstone Course<p>Course Description</p><p>A capstone case course integrating critical thinking, problem solving, professional judgement and ethics. Business simulations will strategically include the specific technical competency areas and the enabling skills of the CPA Competency Map. This course should be taken as part of the last 5.0 credits of the Specialist/Specialist Co-op in Management and Accounting.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGAC02H3">MGAC02H3and <a href="/course/MGAC03H3">MGAC03H3and <a href="/course/MGAC10H3">MGAC10H3and <a href="/course/MGAC50H3">MGAC50H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGAD70H3">
MGAD80H3:  Accounting Issues in International Business<p>Course Description</p><p>An overview of international accounting and financial reporting practices with a focus on accounting issues related to international business activities and foreign operations. Understanding the framework used in establishing international accounting standards, preparation and translation of financial statements, transfer pricing and taxation, internal and external auditing issues and discussion of the role of accounting and performance measurement for multinational corporations.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGAB02H3">MGAB02H3and <a href="/course/MGAB03H3">MGAB03H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGAD80H3">

The special topics will vary from year to year but could include topics in bankruptcies, forensic accounting, controversial issues in financial reporting, accounting principles for non-accounting students, accounting for international business, accounting for climate change, ESG accounting and Accounting for general financial literacy. MGEB31H3:  Public Decision Making<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of decision-making by governments from an economic perspective. The course begins by examining various rationales for public involvement in the economy and then examines a number of theories explaining the way decisions are actually made in the public sector. The course concludes with a number of case studies of Canadian policy making.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/MGEA01H3">MGEA01H3or <a href="/course/MGEA02H3">MGEA02H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/MGEA05H3">MGEA05H3or <a href="/course/MGEA06H3">MGEA06H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEB31H3">
MGEB32H3:  Economic Aspects of Public Policy<p>Course Description</p><p>Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA) is a key policy-evaluation tool developed by economists to assess government policy alternatives and provide advice to governments. In this course, we learn the key assumption behind and techniques used by CBA and how to apply these methods in practice.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/MGEA01H3">MGEA01H3or <a href="/course/MGEA02H3">MGEA02H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/MGEA05H3">MGEA05H3or <a href="/course/MGEA06H3">MGEA06H3</a>]<p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB01H3">MGEB01H3or <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEB32H3">
MGEC02H3:  Topics in Price Theory<p>Course Description</p><p>Continuing development of the principles of microeconomic theory. MGEC08H3:  Economics of Markets and Financial Decision Making<p>Course Description</p><p>This course covers key concepts and theories in both microeconomics and macroeconomics that are relevant to businesses and investors. Topics to be covered include the market structures; the economics of regulations; the foreign exchange market; economic growth; and policy mix under different macro settings. Aside from enhancing students' understanding of economic analyses, this course also helps students prepare for the economics components in all levels of the CFA exams.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3and <a href="/course/MGEB06H3">MGEB06H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/MGEC41H3">MGEC41H3</a>, <a href="/course/MGEC92H3">MGEC92H3</a>, <a href="/course/MGEC93H3">MGEC93H3</a>, ECO200Y1, ECO204Y1, ECO206Y1, ECO310H1, ECO364H1, ECO365H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC08H3">
MGEC11H3:  Introduction to Regression Analysis<p>Course Description</p><p>This course builds on the introductory regression analysis learned in <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3to develop the knowledge and skills necessary to obtain and analyze cross-sectional economic data. Topics includes, multiple regression, Instrumental variables, panel data, maximum likelihood estimation, probit regression &amp; logit regression. “ R”, a standard software for econometric and statistical analysis, will be used throughout the course. By the end of the course students will learn how to estimate economic relations in different settings, and critically assess statistical results.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ECO374H5, ECM375H5, STA302H; <a href="/course/MGEC11H3">MGEC11H3may not be taken after <a href="/course/STAC67H3">STAC67H3</a>.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC11H3">
MGEC20H3:  Economics of the Media<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of the role and importance of communications media in the economy. Topics to be covered include: the challenges media pose for conventional economic theory, historical and contemporary issues in media development, and basic media-research techniques. The course is research-oriented, involving empirical assignments and a research essay. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB01H3">MGEB01H3or <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC20H3">
MGEC22H3:  Behavioural Economics<p>Course Description</p><p>Intermediate level development of the principles of behavioural economics. Behavioural economics aims to improve policy and economic models by incorporating psychology and cognitive science into economics. The course will rely heavily on the principles of microeconomic analysis.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: Grade B or higher in <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3</a>. <a href="/course/MGEC02H3">MGEC02H3and the basics of game theory would be helpful.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students who have completed <a href="/course/MGEC02H3">MGEC02H3</a>.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC22H3">
MGEC25H3:  Special Topics in Economics<p>Course Description</p><p>This course covers special topics in an area of Economics. The specific topics will vary from year to year. MGEC34H3:  Economics of Health Care<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of the economic principles underlying health care and health insurance.<br>This course is a survey of some of the major topics in health economics. Some of the topics that will be covered will include the economic determinants of health, the market for medical care, the market for health insurance, and health and safety regulation.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ECO369H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC34H3">
MGEC37H3:  Law and Economics<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of laws and legal institutions from an economic perspective. It includes the development of a positive theory of the law and suggests that laws frequently evolve so as to maximize economic efficiency. The efficiency of various legal principles is also examined. Topics covered are drawn from: externalities, property rights, contracts, torts, product liability and consumer protection, and procedure.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB01H3">MGEB01H3or <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ECO320H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC37H3">
MGEC38H3:  The Economics of Canadian Public Policy<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides a comprehensive study of selected Canadian public policies from an economic point of view. Topics may include environmental policy, competition policy, inflation and monetary policy, trade policy and others. We will study Canadian institutions, decision-making mechanisms, implementation procedures, policy rationales, and related issues.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/MGEB01H3">MGEB01H3or <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/MGEB05H3">MGEB05H3or <a href="/course/MGEB06H3">MGEB06H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ECO336H1, ECO337H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC38H3">
MGEC40H3:  Economics of Organization and Management<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the economics of the internal organization of the firm. Emphasis will be on economic relationships between various parties involved in running a business: managers, shareholders, workers, banks, and government.<br>Topics include the role of organizations in market economies, contractual theory, risk sharing, property rights, corporate financial structure and vertical integration.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB01H3">MGEB01H3or <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ECO310H1, ECO370Y5, ECO380H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC40H3">

There will be some use of calculus in this course.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/MGEC08H3">MGEC08H3</a>, <a href="/course/MGEC92H3">MGEC92H3</a>, ECO310H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC41H3">
MGEC45H3:  Sports Data, Analysis and Economics<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is intended to apply concepts of analytic management and data science to the sports world. Emphasis on model building and application of models studied previously, including economics and econometrics, is intended to deepen the students’ ability to apply these skills in other areas as well. The course will address papers at the research frontier, since those papers are an opportunity to learn about the latest thinking. The papers will both be interested in sports intrinsically, and interested in sports as a way to assess other theories that are a part of business education.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/RSM314H3">RSM314H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC45H3">
MGEC51H3:  Labour Economics I<p>Course Description</p><p>Applications of the tools of microeconomics to various labour market issues. The topics covered will include: labour supply; labour demand; equilibrium in competitive and non-competitive markets; non-market approaches to the labour market; unemployment. Policy applications will include: income maintenance programs; minimum wages; and unemployment.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ECO339H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC51H3">
MGEC54H3:  Economics of Training and Education<p>Course Description</p><p>This course studies the economic aspects of how individuals and firms make decisions: about education and on-the-job training. Economics and the business world consider education and training as investments. In this class, students will learn how to model these investments, and how to create good policies to encourage individuals and firms to make wise investment decisions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB01H3">MGEB01H3or <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ECO338H1, ECO412Y5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC54H3">
MGEC58H3:  Economics of Human Resource Management<p>Course Description</p><p>This course focuses on the various methods that firms and managers use to pay, recruit and dismiss employees. Topics covered may include: training decisions, deferred compensation, variable pay, promotion theory, incentives for teams and outsourcing.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (MGEC52H3), ECO381H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC58H3">
MGEC61H3:  International Economics: Finance<p>Course Description</p><p>Macroeconomic theories of the balance of payments and the exchange rate in a small open economy. Recent theories of exchange-rate determination in a world of floating exchange rates. The international monetary system: fixed "versus" flexible exchange rates, international capital movements, and their implications for monetary policy.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB05H3">MGEB05H3or <a href="/course/MGEB06H3">MGEB06H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ECO230Y1, ECO365H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC61H3">
MGEC62H3:  International Economics: Trade Theory<p>Course Description</p><p>An outline of the theories of international trade that explain why countries trade with each other, and the welfare implications of this trade, as well as empirical tests of these theories. The determination and effects of trade policy instruments (tariffs, quotas, non-tariff barriers) and current policy issues are also discussed. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3or [<a href="/course/MGEB01H3">MGEB01H3and <a href="/course/MATA34H3">MATA34H3</a>] or [<a href="/course/MGEB01H3">MGEB01H3and [<a href="/course/MATA29H3">MATA29H3or <a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3or <a href="/course/MATA31H3">MATA31H3or <a href="/course/MATA32H3">MATA32H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/MATA33H3">MATA33H3or <a href="/course/MATA35H3">MATA35H3or <a href="/course/MATA36H3">MATA36H3or <a href="/course/MATA37H3">MATA37H3</a>]]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/MGEC93H3">MGEC93H3</a>, ECO230Y1, ECO364H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC62H3">
MGEC65H3:  Economics of the Environment and Climate Change<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides an Economic framework to understand issues around the environment and climate change. The economic toolkit to understand these issues includes externalities, tradeoffs, cost-benefit analysis, marginal analysis, and dynamic accounting. The course will cover optimal policy approaches to pollution, carbon emissions, and resource extraction. These include carbon taxes, subsidies, cap-and-trade systems, bans, and quotas. Both theoretical and empirical approaches in Economics will be discussed.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3and <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC65H3">

MGEC72H3:  Financial Economics<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to the theoretical underpinnings of financial economics. Topics covered include: intertemporal choice, expected utility, the CAPM, Arbitrage Pricing, State Prices (Arrow-Debreu security), market efficiency, the term structure of interest rates, and option pricing models. Key empirical tests are also reviewed. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3and <a href="/course/MGEB06H3">MGEB06H3and <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ECO358H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGEC72H3">

MGEC91H3:  Economics and Government<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides an overview of what governments can do to benefit society, as suggested by economic theory and empirical research. MGED50H3:  Workshop in Economic Research<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces to students the techniques used by economists to define research problems and to do research. Students will choose a research problem, write a paper on their topic and present their ongoing work to the class.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3and <a href="/course/MGEC02H3">MGEC02H3and <a href="/course/MGEB06H3">MGEB06H3and <a href="/course/MGEC06H3">MGEC06H3and <a href="/course/MGEB11H3">MGEB11H3and <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3and <a href="/course/MGEC11H3">MGEC11H3</a>. MGED63H3:  Financial Crises: Causes, Consequences and Policy Implications<p>Course Description</p><p>This course studies the causes, consequences and policy implications of recent financial crises. It studies key theoretical concepts of international finance such as exchange-rate regimes, currency boards, common currency, banking and currency crises. The course will describe and analyze several major episodes of financial crises, such as East Asia, Mexico and Russia in the 1990s, Argentina in the early 2000s, the U.S. and Greece in the late 2000s, and others in recent years.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEC61H3">MGEC61H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGED63H3">
MGED70H3:  Financial Econometrics<p>Course Description</p><p>Financial econometrics applies statistical techniques to analyze the financial data in order to solve problems in Finance. In doing so, this course will focus on four major topics: Forecasting returns, Modeling Univariate and Multivariate Volatility, High Frequency and market microstructure, Simulation Methods and the application to risk management.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEC11H3">MGEC11H3and [<a href="/course/MGEC72H3">MGEC72H3or <a href="/course/MGFC10H3">MGFC10H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ECO462H`<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGED70H3">

MGFB10H3:  Principles of Finance<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to basic concepts and analytical tools in financial management. Building on the fundamental concept of time value of money, the course will examine stock and bond valuations and capital budgeting under certainty. Also covered are risk-return trade-off, financial planning and forecasting, and long-term financing decisions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB11H3">MGEB11H3and <a href="/course/MGAB01H3">MGAB01H3and [<a href="/course/MGTA38H3">MGTA38H3or (MGTA35H3) or (MGTA36H3)]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: MGT338H5, RSM332H1, MGM230H5, RSM230H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGFB10H3">
MGFC10H3:  Intermediate Finance<p>Course Description</p><p>This course covers mainstream finance topics. Besides a deeper examination of certain topics already covered in <a href="/course/MGFB10H3">MGFB10H3</a>, the course will investigate additional subjects such as working capital management, capital budgeting under uncertainty, cost of capital, capital structure, dividend policy, leasing, mergers and acquisitions, and international financial management.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGFB10H3">MGFB10H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: MGT339H5, RSM333H1, MGM332H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGFC10H3">

The course will benefit students in managing their personal finances, and in their future careers with financial institutions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGFB10H3">MGFB10H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGFC20H3">
MGFC30H3:  Introduction to Derivatives Markets<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to the fundamentals of derivatives markets covering futures, swaps, options and other financial derivative securities. Detailed descriptions of, and basic valuation techniques for popular derivative securities are provided. As each type of derivative security is introduced, its applications in investments and general risk management will be discussed.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-corequisite"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-corequisite">Corequisite: <a href="/course/MGFC10H3">MGFC10H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: MGT438H5, RSM435H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGFC30H3">
MGFC35H3:  Investments<p>Course Description</p><p>This course deals with fundamental elements of investments. Basic concepts and techniques are introduced for various topics such as risk and return characteristics, optimal portfolio construction, security analysis, investments in stocks, bonds and derivative securities, and portfolio performance measurements. </p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-corequisite"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-corequisite">Corequisite: <a href="/course/MGFC10H3">MGFC10H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (MGFD10H3), MGT330H5, RSM330H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGFC35H3">
MGFC45H3:  Portfolio Management: Theory and Practice<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to both the theoretical and practical elements of portfolio management. On the theoretical side, students learn the investment theories and analytic models applicable to portfolio management. Students gain fundamental knowledge of portfolio construction, optimization, and performance attribution. The hands-on component of the course aims to provide students with a unique experiential learning opportunity, through participation in the different stages of the portfolio management process. The investment exercises challenge students to apply and adapt to different risk and return scenarios, time horizons, legal and other unique investment constraints.</p> <p>Classes are conducted in the experiential learning lab, where students explore academic, research and practical components of Portfolio Management.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-corequisite"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-corequisite">Corequisite: <a href="/course/MGFC35H3">MGFC35H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGFC45H3">

It discusses foreign exchange markets, international portfolio investment and international corporate finance. Next to covering the relevant theories, students also get the opportunity to apply their knowledge to real world issues by practicing case studies.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGFC10H3">MGFC10H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: MGT439H5, RSM437H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGFC50H3">

MGFD25H3:  Financial Technologies and Applications (FinTech)<p>Course Description</p><p>Financial Technologies (FinTech) are changing our everyday lives and challenging many financial institutions to evolve and adapt. The course explores disruptive financial technologies and innovations such as mobile banking, cryptocurrencies, Robo-advisory and the financial applications of artificial intelligence (AI) etc. The course covers the various areas within the financial industry that are most disrupted, thus leading to discussions on the challenges and opportunities for both the financial institutions and the regulators. MGHC55H3:  International Management OBHR Field Course <p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the principles and challenges of Organizational Behaviour and Human Resource Management principles (e.g., Leadership, Work Team Effectiveness, Recruitment &amp; Selection, Health &amp; Safety, etc.) in international contexts, focusing on leading theories and best practices. Through interactions with host nation University faculty and business leaders, industry tours of local businesses, case examples, skill-building exercises, and academic research, students will learn about OBHR topics from the perspective of the host country.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: The completion of 7.5 credits including [<a href="/course/MGHA12H3">MGHA12H3or <a href="/course/MGIA12H3">MGIA12H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/MGHB02H3">MGHB02H3or <a href="/course/MGIB02H3">MGIB02H3</a>]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: [<a href="/course/MGTA35H3">MGTA35H3or <a href="/course/MGTA36H3">MGTA36H3or <a href="/course/MGTA38H3">MGTA38H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGHC55H3">

MGMB01H3:  Marketing Management<p>Course Description</p><p>This course builds on the introductory course in marketing and takes a pragmatic approach to develop the analytical skills required of marketing managers. The course is designed to help improve skills in analyzing marketing situations, identifying market opportunities, developing marketing strategies, making concise recommendations, and defending these recommendations. MGMC01H3:  Market Research<p>Course Description</p><p>A decision oriented course, which introduces students to the market research process. It covers different aspects of marketing research, both quantitative and qualitative, and as such teaches some essential fundamentals for the students to master in case they want to specialize in marketing. And includes alternative research approaches (exploratory, descriptive, causal), data collection, sampling, analysis and evaluation procedures are discussed. Theoretical and technical considerations in design and execution of market research are stressed. Instruction involves lectures and projects including computer analysis. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGMA01H3">MGMA01H3or <a href="/course/MGIA01H3">MGIA01H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: MGT453H5, RSM452H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGMC01H3">
MGMC02H3:  Consumer Behaviour<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides an overview of the role of products in the lives of consumers. Drawing on theories from psychology, sociology and economics, the course provides (1) a conceptual understanding of consumer behaviour (e.g. why people buy), and (2) an experience in the application of these concepts to marketing decisions. MGMC12H3:  Advertising: From Theory to Practice<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the basic communication tools used in planning, implementing and evaluating promotional strategies .The course reviews basic findings of the behavioural sciences dealing with perception, personality, psychological appeals, and their application to advertising as persuasive communication. MGMC13H3:  Pricing Strategy<p>Course Description</p><p>Pricing right is fundamental to a firm's profitability. This course draws on microeconomics to develop practical approaches for optimal pricing decision-making. Students develop a systematic framework to think about, analyze and develop strategies for pricing right. Key issues covered include pricing new product, value pricing, behavioural issues, and price segmentation.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/MGMA01H3">MGMA01H3or <a href="/course/MGIA01H3">MGIA01H3</a>] and <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGMC13H3">

MGMD21H3:  Competitive Marketing in Action<p>Course Description</p><p>This course focuses on the analysis required to support marketing decisions and aid in the formation of marketing strategy. This is a Marketing simulation course which will challenge students to make real-time decisions with realistic consequences in a competitive market scenario. As part of a team, students will make decisions on Pricing, branding, distribution strategy, commissioning and using market research, new product launches and a variety of related marketing actions while competing with other teams in a simulation that will dynamically unfold over the semester. This is an action-packed capstone course and will give students the chance to apply what they have learned and to polish their skills in a realistic environment.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/MGMA01H3">MGMA01H3or <a href="/course/MGIA01H3">MGIA01H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/MGMB01H3">MGMB01H3or <a href="/course/MGIB01H3">MGIB01H3</a>]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: Some interest in or additional knowledge of different aspects of Marketing<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGMD21H3">
MGOC10H3:  Analytics for Decision Making<p>Course Description</p><p>The course develops understanding and practical skills of applying quantitative analysis for making better management decisions. Studied analytics methodologies include linear programming; multi-criteria optimization; network and waiting-line models; decision analysis. Methodologies are practiced in a broad range of typical business problems drawn from different areas of management, using spreadsheet modelling tools.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB02H3">MGEB02H3and [<a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3or MAF2001H or CDPD00] and [<a href="/course/MGTA38H3">MGTA38H3or (MGTA36H3) or (MGTA35H3)]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning<p>Note</p>Note: Students enrolled in the Combined Degree Program with Master of Accounting and Finance may take <a href="/course/MGOC10H3">MGOC10H3and MAF2001H concurrently in the same term.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGOC10H3">

Using state-of-the-art computational tools, students learn the fundamentals of processing, visualizing, and identifying patterns from data to draw actionable insights and improve decision making in business processes.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MGOC10H3">MGOC10H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: MGT458H5, (MGOD30H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGOC15H3">

MGOD40H3:  Simulation and Analysis of Business Processes<p>Course Description</p><p>Students will learn how to construct and implement simulation models for business processes using a discrete-event approach. They will gain skills in the statistical analysis of input data, validation and verification of the models. Using these models, they can evaluate the alternative design and make system improvements. Students will also learn how to perform a Monte Carlo simulation. Spreadsheet and simulation software are integral components to this course and will enhance proficiency in Excel.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGOC10H3">MGOC10H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MGOC20H3">MGOC20H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: MIE360H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGOD40H3">

MGSB01H3:  Introduction to Strategy<p>Course Description</p><p>This course offers an introduction to strategic management. It analyzes strategic interactions between rival firms in the product market, provides conceptual tools for analyzing these interactions, and highlights the applications of these tools to key elements of business strategy. The course then moves beyond product market competition and considers (among other things) strategic interactions inside the organization, and in the context of corporate strategy.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Minimum 4.0 credits including <a href="/course/MGEA02H3">MGEA02H3and <a href="/course/MATA34H3">MATA34H3or [[<a href="/course/MATA29H3">MATA29H3or <a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3or <a href="/course/MATA31H3">MATA31H3or (MATA32H3)] and [(MATA33H3) or <a href="/course/MATA35H3">MATA35H3or <a href="/course/MATA36H3">MATA36H3or <a href="/course/MATA37H3">MATA37H3</a>]]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: RSM392H1 and MGT492H5</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/MGAB01H3">MGAB01H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGSB01H3">

MGSC05H3:  The Changing World of Business - Government Relations<p>Course Description</p><p>How regulation, privatization and globalization are affecting today's managers.<br>Most major management issues and business opportunities involve government (domestic or foreign) at some level - whether as lawmaker, customer, partner, investor, tax-collector, grant-giver, licensor, dealmaker, friend or enemy. This course provides students with an understanding of the issues and introduces some of the skills necessary to successfully manage a business's relationship with government.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 4.0 credits or [<a href="/course/POLB56H3">POLB56H3and <a href="/course/POLB57H3">POLB57H3</a>/(POLB50Y3)]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: <div><a href="/course/POLB56H3">POLB56H3and <a href="/course/POLB57H3">POLB57H3are prerequisites only for students enrolled in Public Policy programs</div></span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGSC05H3">
MGSC07H3:  Introduction to Case Analysis Techniques<p>Course Description</p><p>This course focuses on the theory and techniques of analyzing and writing business cases. Critical thinking and problem solving skills are developed through extensive use of case analysis.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGAB03H3">MGAB03H3and <a href="/course/MGFB10H3">MGFB10H3and <a href="/course/MGHB02H3">MGHB02H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MGMA01H3">MGMA01H3and <a href="/course/MGAB02H3">MGAB02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGSC07H3">
MGSC10H3:  Business Strategy in the Digital Age<p>Course Description</p><p>This course teaches students the ways in which business strategy and strategic decisions are affected by the recent explosion of digital technologies. Key considerations include the market and organizational context, process design, and managerial practices that determine value from data, digital infrastructures, and AI. It provides classic frameworks augmented by frontier research to make sense of digital transformation from the perspective of a general manager. Leaning on case study analysis and in-class discussion, this course will surface both practical and ethical pitfalls that can emerge in an increasingly digital world and equip students to operate effectively in professional contexts affected by these fast-moving trends.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Completion of 10.0 credits including <a href="/course/MGEB11H3">MGEB11H3and <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: Introductory Logic, Probability and Statistics Econometrics (Linear Regression)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGSC10H3">

Technology and demography are changing the nature of work. Instead of having secure progressive careers, you will work on contract or as consultants. MGSC26H3:  Venture Capital<p>Course Description</p><p>Venture capital and other sources of private equity play a critical role in the founding and development of new enterprises. In this course, we will review all aspects of starting and operating a venture capital firm. At the end of the course, students will better understand how the venture capital industry works; what types of businesses venture capitalists invest in and why; how contract structures protect investors; how venture capitalists create value for their investors and for the companies in which they invest; and how the North American venture capital model ports to other contexts.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGFB10H3">MGFB10H3and <a href="/course/MGEC40H3">MGEC40H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist program in Strategic Management: Entrepreneurship Stream. Policy considerations, such as the patentability of life forms, copyright in an Internet age of easy copying and patents and international development will be included.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MGSC30H3">MGSC30H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: 9.5 credits in addition to the prerequisite.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MGSD30H3">

MUZB41H3:  DJ Cultures: Analogue Innovations and Digital Aesthetics<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores the aesthetic innovations of DJs from various musical genres, from disco to drum’n’bass to dub. We also spend time exploring the political, legal, and social aspects of DJs as their production, remixes, touring schedules, and community involvement reveal what is at stake when we understand DJs as more than entertainers. The course utilizes case studies and provides a hands-on opportunity to explore some of the basic elements of DJ-ing, while simultaneously providing a deep dive into critical scholarly literature.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MUZA80H3">MUZA80H3</a>/(VPMA95H3)<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (VPMC88H3) if taken in Winter 2020 session<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MUZB41H3">

MUZC20H3:  Movies, Music and Meaning<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the synergistic relationship between the moving image and music and how these synergies result in processes of meaning-making and communication. Drawing on readings in cultural theory, cultural studies, musicology and film studies, the course considers examples from the feature film, the Hollywood musical, and the animated cartoon.<br><br></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 2.0 credits at the B-level in MUZ/(VPM) courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (MDSC85H3), (VPMC85H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: No Specialist knowledge in Musicology or Film Studies required.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MUZC20H3">

MUZC80H3:  Topics in Music and Culture<p>Course Description</p><p>The investigation of an area of current interest and importance in musical scholarship. MUZC81H3:  Issues in Popular Music: Heritage, Preservation &amp; Archives<p>Course Description</p><p>Popular music, especially local music, are cultural artifacts that shape local communities and the navigation of culturally hybrid identities. Music is also a significant technology of “remembering in everyday life,” a storehouse of our memories. In this course we examine acts of popular music preservation and consider questions such as: what happens when museums house popular music exhibitions? Who has the authority to narrate popular music, and how does popular music become a site of cultural heritage? Throughout this course, we will work with a notion of “heritage” as an act that brings the past into conversation with the present and can powerfully operate to bring people together while simultaneously excluding others or strategically forgetting. We will spend time with bottom-up heritage projects and community archives to better understand how memory is becoming democratized beyond large cultural institutions. As more and more cultural heritage becomes digitally born, new possibilities and new risks emerge for the preservation of popular music cultures.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit at the B-level from the following: <a href="/course/MUZB01H3">MUZB01H3</a>, <a href="/course/MUZB20H3">MUZB20H3</a>, or <a href="/course/MUZB80H3">MUZB80H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MUZC81H3">
MUZD01H3:  Senior Seminar: Music in Our Communities<p>Course Description</p><p>Through advanced studies in community music, students will combine theory and practice through intensive seminar-style discussions and an immersive service-learning placement with a community music partner. Off-campus site visits are required.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MUZC01H3">MUZC01H3</a>/(VPMC01H3)<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (VPMD01H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Major and Minor programs in Music and Culture. Additional students will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MUZD01H3">
