MUZD81H3:  Independent Study in Music<p>Course Description</p><p>A directed research, composition or performance course for students who have demonstrated a high level of academic maturity and competence. Students in performance combine a directed research project with participation in one of the performance ensembles.<br>Note: Students must submit a proposed plan of study for approval in the term prior to the beginning of the course, and must obtain consent from the supervising instructor and the Music Program Director.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: A minimum overall average of B+ in MUZ/VPM courses, and at least 1.0 full credit in music at the C-level. Students in the Composition option must also have completed <a href="/course/MUZC40H3">MUZC40H3</a>/(VPMC90H3). Students in the Performance/research option must complete at least one course in performance at the C-level.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (VPMD80H3)</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/MUZD81H3">
NMEA01H3:  Digital Fundamentals<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces basic hardware and software for new media. Students will learn basics of HTML (tags, tables and frames) and JavaScript for creation of new media. Discusses hardware requirements including storage components, colour palettes and different types of graphics (bitmap vs. vector-based). Students will be introduced to a variety of software packages used in new media production. This course is taught at Centennial College.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 5.0 credits including <a href="/course/MDSA01H3">MDSA01H3and <a href="/course/MDSA02H3">MDSA02H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/NMEA02H3">NMEA02H3</a>, <a href="/course/NMEA03H3">NMEA03H3</a>, <a href="/course/NMEA04H3">NMEA04H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: This course is only open to students registered in the Joint Major Program in New Media.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NMEA01H3">
NMEA02H3:  Introduction to New Media Communications<p>Course Description</p><p>This course enables students to develop strong written communications skills for effective project proposals and communications, as well as non-linear writing skills that can be applied to a wide range of interactive media projects. The course examines the difference between successful writing for print and for new media, and how to integrate text and visual material. This course is taught at Centennial College.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 5.0 credits including <a href="/course/MDSA01H3">MDSA01H3and <a href="/course/MDSA02H3">MDSA02H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/NMEA01H3">NMEA01H3</a>, <a href="/course/NMEA03H3">NMEA03H3</a>, <a href="/course/NMEA04H3">NMEA04H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies<p>Note</p>Note: This course is only open to students registered in the Joint Major Program in New Media.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NMEA02H3">
NMEA03H3:  The Language of Design<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces the fundamentals of two-dimensional design, graphic design theory, graphic design history, colour principles, typographic principles and visual communication theories applied to New Media Design. Working from basic form generators, typography, two-dimensional design principles, colour and visual communication strategies, learners will be introduced to the exciting world of applied graphic design and multi-media. This course is taught at Centennial College.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 10 full credits<p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: 5.0 credits including <a href="/course/MDSA01H3">MDSA01H3and <a href="/course/MDSA02H3">MDSA02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: This course is only open to students registered in the Joint Major Program in New Media.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NMEA03H3">
NMEA04H3:  Interface Design, Navigation and Interaction I<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to the discipline of user interface and software design, and in particular their impact and importance in the world of new media. The course uses theory and research in combination with practical application, to bring a user-centred design perspective to developing new media software. This course is taught at Centennial College.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 5.0 credits including <a href="/course/MDSA01H3">MDSA01H3and <a href="/course/MDSA02H3">MDSA02H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/NMEA01H3">NMEA01H3</a>, <a href="/course/NMEA02H3">NMEA02H3</a>, <a href="/course/NMEA03H3">NMEA03H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: This course is only open to students registered in the Joint Major Program in New Media.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NMEA04H3">
NMEB05H3:  Interface Design, Navigation and Interaction II<p>Course Description</p><p>Extends work on interface design. Students have opportunities to gain real world experience in the techniques of user interface design. Participants learn to do a "requirements document" for projects, how to design an interface which meets the needs of the requirements of the document and how to test a design with real world users.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/NMEA01H3">NMEA01H3and <a href="/course/NMEA02H3">NMEA02H3and <a href="/course/NMEA03H3">NMEA03H3and <a href="/course/NMEA04H3">NMEA04H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: This course is only open to students registered in the Joint Major Program in New Media.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NMEB05H3">
NMEB06H3:  Project Development and Presentation<p>Course Description</p><p>This course enables the participant to understand the new media production process. Learners will develop the skills to conduct benchmarking, scoping and testing exercises that lead to meaningful project planning documents. Learners will develop and manage production schedules for their group projects that support the development efforts using the project planning documents. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/NMEA01H3">NMEA01H3and <a href="/course/NMEA02H3">NMEA02H3and <a href="/course/NMEA03H3">NMEA03H3and <a href="/course/NMEA04H3">NMEA04H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/NMEB05H3">NMEB05H3and <a href="/course/NMEB08H3">NMEB08H3and <a href="/course/NMEB09H3">NMEB09H3and <a href="/course/NMEB10H3">NMEB10H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: This course is only open to students registered in the Joint Major Program in New Media.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NMEB06H3">
NMEB08H3:  Application Software for Interactive Media<p>Course Description</p><p>This course builds on <a href="/course/NMEA01H3">NMEA01H3</a>. It enables learners to extend their understanding of software requirements and of advanced software techniques. Software used may include Dreamweaver, Flash, Director, and animation (using Director).</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/NMEA01H3">NMEA01H3and <a href="/course/NMEA02H3">NMEA02H3and <a href="/course/NMEA03H3">NMEA03H3and <a href="/course/NMEA04H3">NMEA04H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: This course is only open to students registered in the Joint Major Program in New Media.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NMEB08H3">
NMEB09H3:  Sound Design<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to the scope of sound design - creative audio for new media applications. Students will work with audio applications software to sample, create and compress files, and in the planning and post-production of new media. Students will also learn to use audio in interactive ways such as soundscapes.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/NMEA01H3">NMEA01H3and <a href="/course/NMEA02H3">NMEA02H3and <a href="/course/NMEA03H3">NMEA03H3and <a href="/course/NMEA04H3">NMEA04H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/NMEB05H3">NMEB05H3and <a href="/course/NMEB06H3">NMEB06H3and <a href="/course/NMEB08H3">NMEB08H3and <a href="/course/NMEB10H3">NMEB10H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: This course is only open to students registered in the Joint Major Program in New Media.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NMEB09H3">
NMEB10H3:  New Media Design<p>Course Description</p><p>This course discusses the integration of multiple media with the art of good design. The course examines the conventions of typography and the dynamics between words and images, with the introduction of time, motion and sound. The course involves guest speakers, class exercises, assignments, field trips, group critiques and major projects.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/NMEA01H3">NMEA01H3and <a href="/course/NMEA02H3">NMEA02H3and <a href="/course/NMEA03H3">NMEA03H3and <a href="/course/NMEA04H3">NMEA04H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/NMEB05H3">NMEB05H3and <a href="/course/NMEB06H3">NMEB06H3and <a href="/course/NMEB08H3">NMEB08H3and <a href="/course/NMEB09H3">NMEB09H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: This course is only open to students registered in the Joint Major Program in New Media.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NMEB10H3">
NMEC01H3:  Theory and Practice of New Media<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar examines the ideological, political, structural, and representational assumptions underlying new media production and consumption from both theoretical and practice-based perspectives. Students critically reflect on and analyze digital media applications and artefacts in contemporary life, including business, information, communication, entertainment, and creative practices.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 4.5 credits from the Major (Joint) program in New Media Studies Group I and Group II courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (NMED20H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NMEC01H3">
NMED10Y3:  New Media Senior Project<p>Course Description</p><p>Students develop a new media project that furthers their research into theoretical issues around digital media practices and artefacts. Projects may focus on digital media ranging from the internet to gaming, to social networking and the Web, to CD-ROMS, DVDs, mobile apps, and Virtual and Augmented Reality technologies.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Completion of 15.0 credits including 4.5 credits from the Major (Joint) program in New Media Studies Group I and Group II courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (NMED01H3)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NMED10Y3">
NROB60H3:  Neuroanatomy Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>This course focuses on functional neuroanatomy of the brain at both the human and animal level. Topics include gross anatomy of the brain, structure and function of neurons and glia, neurotransmitters and their receptors, and examples of major functional systems. Content is delivered through lecture and laboratories.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOA01H3">BIOA01H3and <a href="/course/BIOA02H3">BIOA02H3and <a href="/course/CHMA10H3">CHMA10H3and [<a href="/course/CHMA11H3">CHMA11H3or <a href="/course/CHMA12H3">CHMA12H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: CSB332H, HMB320H, PSY290H, PSY391H, (ZOO332H)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Neuroscience. Students in other programs will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NROB60H3">
NROB61H3:  Neurophysiology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course focuses on the electrical properties of neurons and the ways in which electrical signals are generated, received, and integrated to underlie neuronal communication. Topics include principles of bioelectricity, the ionic basis of the resting potential and action potential, neurotransmission, synaptic integration, and neural coding schemes. Content will be delivered through lectures, labs, and tutorials.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOA01H3">BIOA01H3and <a href="/course/BIOA02H3">BIOA02H3and <a href="/course/CHMA10H3">CHMA10H3and [<a href="/course/CHMA11H3">CHMA11H3or <a href="/course/CHMA12H3">CHMA12H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/NROB60H3">NROB60H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Neuroscience. Students in other programs will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NROB61H3">
NROC36H3:  Molecular Neuroscience<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will focus on the molecular mechanisms underlying neuronal communication in the central nervous system. The first module will look into synaptic transmission at the molecular level, spanning pre and postsynaptic mechanisms. The second module will focus on molecular mechanisms of synaptic plasticity and learning and memory. Additional topics will include an introduction to the molecular mechanisms of neurodegenerative diseases and channelopathies.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB11H3">BIOB11H3and <a href="/course/NROB60H3">NROB60H3and <a href="/course/NROB61H3">NROB61H3and <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/BIOC13H3">BIOC13H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience (all Stage 2 streams) and students enrolled in the Major/Major Co-op programs in Neuroscience. Students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience - Stage 1 will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NROC36H3">
NROC60H3:  Cellular Neuroscience Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>This course involves a theoretical and a hands-on cellular neuroscience laboratory component. Advanced systems, cellular and molecular neuroscience techniques will be covered within the context of understanding how the brain processes complex behaviour. Practical experience on brain slicing, immunohistochemistry and cell counting will feature in the completion of a lab project examining the cellular mechanisms underlying schizophrenia-like behavioural deficits. These experiments do not involve contact with animals.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/NROB60H3">NROB60H3and <a href="/course/NROB61H3">NROB61H3and <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/NROC69H3">NROC69H3and <a href="/course/PSYC08H3">PSYC08H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: 1. Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience - Cellular/Molecular Stream. Students enrolled in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience - Systems/Behavioural Stream and the Major/Major Co-op programs in Neuroscience will be admitted as space permits. 2. Students may complete a maximum of 0.5 credits from [<a href="/course/NROC60H3">NROC60H3or <a href="/course/NROC63H3">NROC63H3</a>].</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NROC60H3">
NROC61H3:  Learning and Motivation<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will explore the neural and neurochemical bases of learning and motivation. Topics covered under the category of learning include: Pavlovian learning, instrumental learning, multiple memory systems, and topics covered under motivation include: regulation of eating, drinking, reward, stress and sleep. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/NROB60H3">NROB60H3and <a href="/course/NROB61H3">NROB61H3and <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience (all Stage 2 streams) and students enrolled in the Major/Major Co-op programs in Neuroscience. Students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience - Stage 1 will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NROC61H3">
NROC63H3:  Behavioural Neuroscience Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>This is a lecture and hands-on laboratory course that provides instruction on various experimental approaches, design, data analysis and scientific communication of research outcomes in the field of systems/behavioural neuroscience, with a focus on the neural basis of normal and abnormal learning and cognition. Topics covered include advanced pharmacological and neurological manipulation techniques, behavioural techniques and animal models of psychological disease (e.g., anxiety, schizophrenia). The class involves the use of experimental animals.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/NROB60H3">NROB60H3and <a href="/course/NROB61H3">NROB61H3and <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/NROC61H3">NROC61H3and <a href="/course/PSYC08H3">PSYC08H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: 1. Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience - Systems/Behavioural stream. Students enrolled in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience -Cellular/Molecular stream and the Major/Major Co-op programs in Neuroscience will be admitted as space permits. 2. Students may complete a maximum of 0.5 credits from [<a href="/course/NROC60H3">NROC60H3or <a href="/course/NROC63H3">NROC63H3</a>].</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NROC63H3">
NROC64H3:  Sensorimotor Systems<p>Course Description</p><p>A focus on the mechanisms by which the nervous system processes sensory information and controls movement. The topics include sensory transduction and the physiology for sensory systems (visual, somatosensory, auditory, vestibular). Both spinal and central mechanisms of motor control are also covered.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/NROB60H3">NROB60H3and <a href="/course/NROB61H3">NROB61H3and <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience (all Stage 2 streams) and students enrolled in the Major/Major Co-op programs in Neuroscience. Students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience - Stage 1 will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NROC64H3">
NROC69H3:  Synaptic Organization and Physiology of the Brain<p>Course Description</p><p>The course will provide an in-depth examination of neural circuits, synaptic connectivity and cellular mechanisms of synaptic function. Similarities and differences in circuit organization and intrinsic physiology of structures such as the thalamus, hippocampus, basal ganglia and neocortex will also be covered. The goal is to engender a deep and current understanding of cellular mechanisms of information processing in the CNS. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/NROB60H3">NROB60H3and <a href="/course/NROB61H3">NROB61H3and <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience (all Stage 2 streams) and students enrolled in the Major/Major Co-op programs in Neuroscience. Students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience - Stage 1 will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NROC69H3">
NROC90H3:  Supervised Study in Neuroscience<p>Course Description</p><p>An intensive research project intended to provide laboratory/field experience in data collection and analysis. The project must be completed over 2 consecutive terms. NROC90H and <a href="/course/NROC93H3">NROC93H3provide an opportunity to engage in research in an area after completing basic coverage in regularly scheduled courses. The student must demonstrate a background adequate for the project proposed and should present a clear rationale to prospective supervisors. Regular consultation with the supervisor is necessary, and extensive data collection and analysis will be required. Such a project will culminate in a written research report. Students must first find a supervisor before the start of the academic term in which the project will be initiated. They must then obtain a <a href="https://utoronto.sharepoint.com/sites/utsc-psychology/SitePages/UG_Research-Courses.aspx">permission formfrom the Department of Psychology's website that is to be completed and signed by the intended supervisor, and returned to the Psychology Office. Students seeking supervision off campus are further advised to check the appropriateness of the proposed advisor with the Program Supervisor. If the proposed supervisor is not appointed to the Neuroscience faculty at UTSC then a secondary supervisor who is a member of the Neuroscience group at UTSC will be required.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/NROB60H3">NROB60H3and <a href="/course/NROB61H3">NROB61H3and [(PSYB01H3) or (PSYB04H3) or <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3or (PSYB65H3)] and permission of the proposed supervisor.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/PSYC90H3">PSYC90H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NROC90H3">

The project must be completed over 2 consecutive terms. <a href="/course/NROC90H3">NROC90H3and <a href="/course/NROC93H3">NROC93H3provide an opportunity to engage in research in an area after completing basic coverage in regularly scheduled courses. Students seeking supervision off campus are further advised to check the appropriateness of the proposed advisor with the Program Supervisor. NROD61H3:  Emotional Learning Circuits<p>Course Description</p><p>A seminar based course covering topics on emotional learning based on animal models of fear and anxiety disorders in humans. Through readings, presentations and writing students will explore the synaptic, cellular, circuit and behavioural basis of fear memory processing, learning how the brain encodes fearful and traumatic memories, how these change with time and developmental stage, as well as how brain circuits involved in fear processing might play a role in depression and anxiety.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/NROC61H3">NROC61H3and <a href="/course/NROC64H3">NROC64H3and <a href="/course/NROC69H3">NROC69H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: [<a href="/course/NROD60H3">NROD60H3if taken in Fall 2018]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/NROC60H3">NROC60H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Neuroscience. NROD66H3:  Drug Addiction<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of the major phases of the addiction cycle, including drug consumption, withdrawal, and relapse. Consideration will be given to what basic motivational and corresponding neurobiological processes influence behaviour during each phase of the cycle. Recent empirical findings will be examined within the context of major theoretical models guiding the field.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/NROC61H3">NROC61H3or <a href="/course/NROC64H3">NROC64H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYC62H3">PSYC62H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/PSYC08H3">PSYC08H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Neuroscience. NROD67H3:  Neuroscience of Aging<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will characterize various anatomical, biochemical, physiological, and psychological changes that occur in the nervous system with age. NROD98Y3:  Thesis in Neuroscience<p>Course Description</p><p>This course offers the opportunity to engage in a year long research project under the supervision of an interested member of the faculty in Neuroscience. The project will culminate in a written report in the form of a thesis and a poster presentation. During the course of the year, at appropriate times, students will meet to present their own research proposals, to appraise the proposals of others, and to discuss the results of their investigation. Students must first find a supervisor, which is usually confirmed before the start of the academic term in which the project will be initiated. Students will meet as a group with the coordinator as well as individually with their supervisor. Students planning to pursue graduate studies are especially encouraged to enrol in the course. Students must obtain a <a href="https://utoronto.sharepoint.com/sites/utsc-psychology/SitePages/UG_Research-Courses.aspx">permission formfrom the Department of Psychology's website that is to be completed and signed by the intended supervisor, and submitted to the Psychology Office. Students seeking supervision off campus will need to arrange co supervision with a faculty member in Neuroscience at UTSC.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/BIOB10H3">BIOB10H3and <a href="/course/NROB60H3">NROB60H3and <a href="/course/NROB61H3">NROB61H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3and [0.5 credits from the NRO C-level courses or PSY 50-series C-level courses or <a href="/course/BIOC44H3">BIOC44H3</a>] and [enrolment in the Specialist Co-op, Specialist, or Major Program in Neuroscience] and [GPA of 3.3 or higher over the last 5.0 credit equivalents completed] and permission of the proposed neuroscience faculty supervisor.<p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYC08H3">PSYC08H3or <a href="/course/PSYC09H3">PSYC09H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/BIOD98Y3">BIOD98Y3</a>, <a href="/course/BIOD99Y3">BIOD99Y3</a>, <a href="/course/PSYD98Y3">PSYD98Y3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/NROD98Y3">
PHLA10H3:  Reason and Truth<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to philosophy focusing on issues of rationality, metaphysics and the theory of knowledge. Topics may include: the nature of mind, freedom, the existence of God, the nature and knowability of reality. These topics will generally be introduced through the study of key texts from the history of philosophy.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: PHL100Y1, PHL101Y1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLA10H3">
PHLA11H3:  Introduction to Ethics<p>Course Description</p><p>Ethics is concerned with concrete questions about how we ought to treat one another as well as more general questions about how to justify our ethical beliefs. This course is an introduction that both presents basic theories of ethics and considers their application to contemporary moral problems.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: PHL275H, PHL100Y1, PHL101Y1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLA11H3">
PHLB02H3:  Environmental Ethics<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines ethical issues raised by our actions and our policies for the environment. Do human beings stand in a moral relationship to the environment? Does the environment have moral value and do non-human animals have moral status? These fundamental questions underlie more specific contemporary issues such as sustainable development, alternative energy, and animal rights.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: PHL273H</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/PHLA11H3">PHLA11H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB02H3">
PHLB03H3:  Philosophy of Aesthetics<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of challenges posed by the radical changes and developments in modern and contemporary art forms. For example, given the continuously exploding nature of art works, what do they have in common - what is it to be an artwork?</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: PHL285H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB03H3">
PHLB04H3:  Philosophy and Literature<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines some of the classic problems concerning literary texts, such as the nature of interpretation, questions about the power of literary works and their relationship to ethical thought, and problems posed by fictional works - how can we learn from works that are fictional and how can we experience genuine emotions from works that we know are fictional?</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB04H3">

PHLB06H3:  Business Ethics<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of philosophical issues in ethics, social theory, and theories of human nature as they bear on business. What moral obligations do businesses have? Can social or environmental costs and benefits be calculated in a way relevant to business decisions? Do political ideas have a role within business?</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: <a href="/course/MGSC14H3">MGSC14H3</a>/(MGTC59H3), PHL295H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB06H3">
PHLB07H3:  Ethics<p>Course Description</p><p>What is the difference between right and wrong? What is 'the good life'? What is well-being? What is autonomy? These notions are central in ethical theory, law, bioethics, and in the popular imagination. In this course we will explore these concepts in greater depth, and then consider how our views about them shape our views about ethics.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB07H3">

PHLB20H3:  Belief, Knowledge, and Truth<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of the nature of knowledge, and our ability to achieve it. PHLB30H3:  Existentialism<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of the views and approaches pioneered by such writers as Kierkegaard, Husserl, Jaspers, Heidegger and Sartre. Existentialism has had influence beyond philosophy, impacting theology, literature and psychotherapy. Characteristic topics include the nature of the self and its relations to the world and society, self-deception, and freedom of choice.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: PHL220H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB30H3">
PHLB31H3:  Introduction to Ancient Philosophy<p>Course Description</p><p>A survey of some main themes and figures of ancient philosophical thought, concentrating on Plato and Aristotle. PHLB33H3:  God, Self, World<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is a thematic introduction to the history of metaphysics, focusing on topics such as the nature of God, our own nature as human beings, and our relation to the rest of the world. We will read a variety of texts, from ancient to contemporary authors, that will introduce us to concepts such as substance, cause, essence and existence, mind and body, eternity and time, and the principle of sufficient reason. We will also look at the ethical implications of various metaphysical commitments. &nbsp;</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB33H3">
PHLB35H3:  Introduction to Early Modern Philosophy<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is an introduction to the major themes and figures of seventeenth and eighteenth century philosophy, from Descartes to Kant, with emphasis on metaphysics, epistemology, and ethics. &nbsp;</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: PHL210Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB35H3">
PHLB50H3:  Symbolic Logic I<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to formal, symbolic techniques of reasoning. Sentential logic and quantification theory (or predicate logic), including identity will be covered. The emphasis is on appreciation of and practice in techniques, for example, the formal analysis of English statements and arguments, and for construction of clear and rigorous proofs.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: PHL245H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB50H3">
PHLB55H3:  Puzzles and Paradoxes<p>Course Description</p><p></p><div> <div> <div> <div> <div> <div> <div>Time travel, free will, infinity, consciousness: puzzling and paradoxical issues like these, brought under control with logic, are the essence of philosophy. Through new approaches to logic, we will find new prospects for understanding philosophical paradoxes.</div> </div> </div> </div> </div> </div> </div></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB55H3">
PHLB58H3:  Reasoning Under Uncertainty<p>Course Description</p><p>Much thought and reasoning occur in a context of uncertainty. How do we know if a certain drug works against a particular illness? Who will win the next election? This course examines various strategies for dealing with uncertainty. Topics include induction and its problems, probabilistic reasoning and the nature of probability, the assignment of causes and the process of scientific confirmation and refutation. Students will gain an appreciation of decision making under uncertainty in life and science.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: PHL246H1, PHL246H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB58H3">
PHLB60H3:  Introduction to Metaphysics<p>Course Description</p><p>A consideration of problems in metaphysics: the attempt to understand 'how everything fits together' in the most general sense of this phrase. Some issues typically covered include: the existence of God, the nature of time and space, the nature of mind and the problem of the freedom of the will.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (PHL231H)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB60H3">
PHLB81H3:  Theories of Mind<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of questions concerning the nature of mind. Philosophical questions considered may include: what is consciousness, what is the relation between the mind and the brain, how did the mind evolve and do animals have minds, what is thinking, what are feelings and emotions, and can machines have minds.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: PHL240H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB81H3">
PHLB91H3:  Theories of Human Nature<p>Course Description</p><p>An exploration of theories which provide answers to the question 'What is a human being?', answers that might be summarized with catchphrases such as: 'Man is a rational animal,' 'Man is a political animal,' 'Man is inherently individual,' 'Man is inherently social,' etc. Authors studied are: Aristotle, Hobbes, Rousseau, Darwin, Marx, Freud and Sartre.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: PHL244H, (PHLC91H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB91H3">
PHLB99H3:  Philosophical Writing and Methodology<p>Course Description</p><p>In this writing-intensive course, students will become familiar with tools and techniques that will enable them to competently philosophize, on paper and in person. Students will learn how to write an introduction and how to appropriately structure philosophy papers, how to accurately present someone else's position or argumentation, how to critically assess someone else's view or argumentation, and how to present and defend their own positive proposal or argumentation concerning a given topic. Students will learn many more specific skills, such as, how to `signpost' what students are doing, how to identify and charitably interpret ambiguities in another discussion, and how to recognize and apply various argumentative strategies.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 0.5 credit in PHL courses, excluding [<a href="/course/PHLB50H3">PHLB50H3and <a href="/course/PHLB55H3">PHLB55H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies<p>Note</p>Note: This course is strongly recommended for students enrolled in the Specialist and Major program in Philosophy. It is open to students enrolled in the Minor program in Philosophy as well as all other students by permission of the instructor.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLB99H3">
PHLC03H3:  Topics in the Philosophy of Aesthetics<p>Course Description</p><p>An exploration of some current issues concerning the various forms of art such as: the role of the museum, the loss of beauty and the death of art.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.5 credits and [<a href="/course/PHLB03H3">PHLB03H3and an additional 1.0 credit in PHL courses]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLC03H3">
PHLC05H3:  Ethical Theory<p>Course Description</p><p>Philosophers offer systematic theories of ethics: theories that simultaneously explain what ethics is, why it matters, and what it tells us to do. This course is a careful reading of classic philosophical texts by the major systematic thinkers in the Western tradition of ethics. Particular authors read may vary from instructor to instructor.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.5 credits and [an additional 1.5 credits in PHL courses, of which 0.5 credit must be from the Value Theory area of focus – see Table 1.0 for reference]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (PHLC01H3), PHL375H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLC05H3">
PHLC06H3:  Topics in Ethical Theory<p>Course Description</p><p>Philosophical ethics simultaneously aims to explain what ethics is, why it matters, and what it tells us to do. This is what is meant by the phrase 'ethical theory.' In this class we will explore specific topics in ethical theory in some depth. Specific topics may vary with the instructor.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.5 credits and [an additional 1.5 credits in PHL courses, of which 0.5 credit must be from the Value Theory area of focus – see Table 1.0 for reference]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (PHLC01H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLC06H3">
PHLC07H3:  Death and Dying<p>Course Description</p><p>An intermediate-level study of the ethical and legal issues raised by death and dying. Topics may vary each year, but could include the definition of death and the legal criteria for determining death, the puzzle of how death can be harmful, the ethics of euthanasia and assisted suicide, the relationship between death and having a meaningful life, and the possibility of surviving death.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.5 credits and [an additional 1.5 credits in PHL courses, of which 0.5 credit must be from the Value Theory area of focus, see Table 1.0 for reference]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHL382H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLC07H3">
PHLC08H3:  Topics in Arabic and Jewish Philosophy<p>Course Description</p><p>This is an advanced, reading and discussion intensive course in the history of Arabic and Jewish thought, beginning with highly influential medieval thinkers such as Avicenna (Ibn Sīnā), al-Ghazālī, Al Fārābī, Averroes (Ibn Rushd), and Maimonides, and ending with 20th century philosophers (among them Arendt, Freud and Levinas).</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.5 credits and [and additional 1.5 credits in PHL courses, of which 0.5 credit must be from the History of Philosophy area of focus – see Table 1.0 for reference]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLC08H3">
PHLC09H3:  Topics in Continental Philosophy<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is a reading and discussion intensive course in 20th century German and French European Philosophy. Among the movements we shall study will be phenomenology, existentialism, and structuralism. We will look at the writings of Martin Heidegger, Jean-Paul Sartre, Maurice Merleau-Ponty, Michel Foucault, and Gilles Deleuze, among others.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.5 credits and [an additional 1.5 credits in PHL courses]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLC09H3">

PHLC13H3:  Topics in Philosophy and Feminism<p>Course Description</p><p>Feminist philosophy includes both criticism of predominant approaches to philosophy that may be exclusionary for women and others, and the development of new approaches to various areas of philosophy. Particular topics will vary with the instructor.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.5 credits and [an additional 1.5 credits in PHL courses, of which 0.5 credit must be from the Value Theory sub-discipline area of focus – see Table 1.0 for reference]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLC13H3">
PHLC14H3:  Topics in Non-Western Philosophy<p>Course Description</p><p>Contemporary Philosophy, as taught in North America, tends to focus on texts and problematics associated with certain modes of philosophical investigation originating in Greece and developed in Europe and North America. There are rich alternative modes of metaphysical investigation, however, associated with Arabic, Indian, East Asian, and African philosophers and philosophizing. In this course, we will explore one or more topics drawn from metaphysics, epistemology, or value theory, from the points of view of these alternative philosophical traditions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.5 credits and an additional 1.5 credits in PHL courses</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/PHLB99H3">PHLB99H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLC14H3">

PHLC80H3:  Philosophy of Language<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of philosophical issues about language. PHLC86H3:  Issues in the Philosophy of Mind<p>Course Description</p><p>Advance Issues in the Philosophy of Mind. For example, an examination of arguments for and against the idea that machines can be conscious, can think, or can feel. Topics may include: Turing's test of machine intelligence, the argument based on Gödel's theorem that there is an unbridgeable gulf between human minds and machine capabilities, Searle's Chinese Room thought experiment.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 4.5 credits and [an additional 1.5 credits in PHL courses, of which 0.5 credit must be from the Mind, Metaphysics and Epistemology area of focus – see Table 1.0 for reference]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLC86H3">

PHLC99H3:  Philosophical Development Seminar<p>Course Description</p><p>This course aims to foster a cohesive cohort among philosophy specialists and majors. The course is an intensive seminar that will develop advanced philosophical skills by focusing on textual analysis, argumentative techniques, writing and oral presentation. Students will work closely with the instructor and their peers to develop a conference-style, research-length paper. Each year, the course will focus on a different topic drawn from the core areas of philosophy for its subject matter. Topics to be discussed may include The Nature of Knowledge, Scepticism, Epistemic Justification, Rationality and Rational Belief Formation.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 3.5 credits in PHL courses, including [[<a href="/course/PHLC20H3">PHLC20H3or <a href="/course/PHLC22H3">PHLC22H3</a>] and 0.5 credit at the C-level]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLD20H3">
PHLD31H3:  Advanced Seminar in Ancient Philosophy<p>Course Description</p><p>This course offers in-depth examination of selected topics from the philosophy of Plato and Aristotle, as well as the Epicurean and Stoic schools of thought. Topics will range from the major areas of philosophy: metaphysics, epistemology, ethics, politics and aesthetics.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 3.5 credits in PHL courses, including [[<a href="/course/PHLC31H3">PHLC31H3or <a href="/course/PHLC32H3">PHLC32H3</a>] and [an additional 0.5 credit at the C-level]]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: It is strongly recommended that students take both <a href="/course/PHLC31H3">PHLC31H3and <a href="/course/PHLC32H3">PHLC32H3</a>.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLD31H3">
PHLD35H3:  Advanced Seminar in Rationalism<p>Course Description</p><p>This course offers in-depth examination of the philosophical approach offered by one of the three principal Rationalist philosophers, Descartes, Spinoza or Leibniz.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 3.5 credits in PHL courses, including [<a href="/course/PHLC35H3">PHLC35H3and 0.5 credit at the C-level]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLD35H3">
PHLD36H3:  Advanced Seminar in Empiricism<p>Course Description</p><p>In this course, we will explore in depth certain foundational topics in the philosophy of Berkeley and Hume, with an eye to elucidating both the broadly Empiricist motivations for their approaches and how their approaches to key topics differ. Topics may address the following questions: Is there a mind-independent world? What is causation? Is the ontological or metaphysical status of persons different from that of ordinary objects? Does God exist?</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 3.5 credits in PHL courses, including [<a href="/course/PHLC36H3">PHLC36H3and an additional 0.5 credit at the C-level]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLD36H3">
PHLD43H3:  Advanced Seminar in History of Analytic Philosophy<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines Analytic Philosophy in the mid-20th century, concentrating on Wittgenstein, Ramsey, Carnap, and Quine. Special attention paid to the metaphysical foundations of logic, and the nature of linguistic meaning, including the relations between "truth-conditional" and "verificationist" theories.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 3.5 credits in PHL courses, including [<a href="/course/PHLC43H3">PHLC43H3and 0.5 credit at the C-level]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHL325H, (PHLC44H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLD43H3">
PHLD51H3:  Metalogic<p>Course Description</p><p>Symbolic Logic deals with formal languages: you work inside formal proof systems, and also consider the "semantics", dealing with truth, of formal languages. Instead of working inside formal systems, Metalogic treats systems themselves as objects of study, from the outside.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PHLC51H3">PHLC51H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHL348H, (PHLC54H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLD51H3">
PHLD78H3:  Advanced Seminar in Political Philosophy<p>Course Description</p><p>This advanced seminar will delve more deeply into an issue in political philosophy.&nbsp; Topics will vary from year to year, but some examples include: distributive justice, human rights, and the political morality of freedom.&nbsp; Students will be required to present material to the class at least once during the semester.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 3.5 credits in PHL courses, including 1.0 credit at the C-level<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLD78H3">
PHLD79H3:  Advanced Seminar in Metaphysics<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar addresses core issues in metaphysics. Topics to be discussed may include the nature of persons and personal identity, whether physicalism is true, what is the relation of mind to reality in general, the nature of animal minds and the question of whether machines can possess minds.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 3.5 credits in PHL courses, including 1.0 credit at the C-level</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLD79H3">
PHLD85H3:  Advanced Seminar in Philosophy: Socrates Project Mentorship<p>Course Description</p><p>The Socrates Project Mentorship Seminar is a half-year seminar course that provides experiential learning in philosophy in conjunction with a teaching assignment to lead tutorials and mark assignments in <a href="/course/PHLA10H3">PHLA10H3</a>. This course is designed for a select number of returning Socrates Project participants chosen to mentor new Project participants. These students will solidify their teaching/grading skills and advise new participants in the Project. The seminar course will further enhance their philosophical abilities in an extension of <a href="/course/PHLD88Y3">PHLD88Y3</a>. Roughly 75% of the seminar will be devoted to a more in-depth study of the topics taken up in the <a href="/course/PHLA10H3">PHLA10H3Reason and Truth. Students will write a seminar paper on one of these topics under the supervision of a UTSC Philosophy faculty member working in the relevant area, and they will give an oral presentation on their research topic each semester. The remaining 25% of the seminar will focus on the further exploration of the methods and challenges of teaching philosophy, benchmark grading, and grading generally and, most distinctively, issues of mentorship of new participants to the Socrates Project.<br><br><strong>Note</strong>: The teaching component of the Socrates Project will consist of the following components. Students will optionally attend two 1-hour <a href="/course/PHLA10H3">PHLA10H3lectures each week, and teach one tutorial of approximately 25 students, meeting with them for 1 hour every other week. Students will grade papers, hold office hours, and meet with the relevant professor as needed as well as provide mentorship to new participants in the Socrates Project.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PHLD88Y3">PHLD88Y3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHL489Y1, PHL489Y5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHLD85H3">

PHYA10H3:  Physics I for the Physical Sciences<p>Course Description</p><p>The course is intended for students in physical, environmental and mathematical sciences. The course introduces the basic concepts used to describe the physical world with mechanics as the working example. This includes mechanical systems (kinematics and dynamics), energy, momentum, conservation laws, waves, and oscillatory motion.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Physics 12U - SPH4U (Grade 12 Physics) and Calculus and Vectors (MCV4U) and Advanced Functions (MHF4U)<p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3or <a href="/course/MATA31H3">MATA31H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/PHYA11H3">PHYA11H3</a>, PHY131H, PHY135Y, PHY151H, (PHY110Y), (PHY138Y)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYA10H3">
PHYA11H3:  Physics I for the Life Sciences<p>Course Description</p><p>This first course in Physics at the university level is intended for students enrolled in the Life sciences. It covers fundamental concepts of classical physics and its applications to macroscopic systems. It deals with two main themes; which are Particle and Fluid Mechanics and Waves and Oscillations. The approach will be phenomenological with applications related to life and biological sciences.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Grade 12 Advanced Functions (MHF4U) and Grade 12 Calculus and Vectors (MCV4U)<p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MATA29H3">MATA29H3or <a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3or <a href="/course/MATA31H3">MATA31H3or <a href="/course/MATA32H3">MATA32H3or <a href="/course/MATA20H3">MATA20H3or <a href="/course/MATA34H3">MATA34H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/PHYA10H3">PHYA10H3</a>, PHY131H, PHY135Y, PHY151H, (PHY110Y), (PHY138Y)</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: Grade 12 Physics (SPH4U)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYA11H3">
PHYA21H3:  Physics II for the Physical Sciences<p>Course Description</p><p>This second physics course is intended for students in physical and mathematical sciences programs. Topics include electromagnetism and special relativity.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PHYA10H3">PHYA10H3and [<a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3or <a href="/course/MATA31H3">MATA31H3</a>]<p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: [<a href="/course/MATA36H3">MATA36H3or <a href="/course/MATA37H3">MATA37H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/PHYA22H3">PHYA22H3</a>, (PHY110Y1), PHY132H1, PHY135Y1, (PHY138Y1), PHY152H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYA21H3">
PHYA22H3:  Physics II for the Life Sciences<p>Course Description</p><p>The course covers the main concepts of Electricity and Magnetism, Optics, and Atomic and Nuclear Physics. It provides basic knowledge of these topics with particular emphasis on its applications in the life sciences. It also covers some of the applications of modern physics such as atomic physics and nuclear radiation.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PHYA10H3">PHYA10H3or <a href="/course/PHYA11H3">PHYA11H3or (PHYA01H3)] and [<a href="/course/MATA29H3">MATA29H3or <a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3or <a href="/course/MATA31H3">MATA31H3or <a href="/course/MATA32H3">MATA32H3</a>]<p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MATA35H3">MATA35H3or <a href="/course/MATA36H3">MATA36H3or <a href="/course/MATA37H3">MATA37H3or <a href="/course/MATA33H3">MATA33H3or (MATA21H3).<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/PHYA21H3">PHYA21H3</a>, (PHY110Y), PHY132H, PHY135Y, (PHY138Y), PHY152H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Students interested in completing programs in science are cautioned that (MATA21H3) and <a href="/course/MATA35H3">MATA35H3do not fulfill the program completion requirements of most science programs.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYA22H3">
PHYB01H3:  Modern Physics for Non-Scientists<p>Course Description</p><p>A conceptual overview of some of the most interesting advances in physics and the intellectual background in which they occurred. The interrelationship of the actual practice of physics and its cultural and intellectual context is emphasized. (Space time; Symmetries; Quantum Worlds; Chaos.)</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 4.0 credits<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYB01H3">
PHYB10H3:  Intermediate Physics Laboratory I<p>Course Description</p><p>Experimental and theoretical study of AC and DC circuits with applications to measurements using transducers and electronic instrumentation. Practical examples are used to illustrate several physical systems.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PHYA21H3">PHYA21H3and [<a href="/course/MATA36H3">MATA36H3or <a href="/course/MATA37H3">MATA37H3</a>]<p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MATB41H3">MATB41H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (PHYB23H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYB10H3">
PHYB21H3:  Electricity and Magnetism<p>Course Description</p><p>A first course at the intermediate level in electricity and magnetism. The course provides an in-depth study of electrostatics and magnetostatics. Topics examined include Coulomb's Law, Gauss's Law, electrostatic energy, conductors, Ampere's Law, magnetostatic energy, Lorentz Force, Faraday's Law and Maxwell's equations.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PHYA21H3">PHYA21H3and <a href="/course/MATB41H3">MATB41H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MATB42H3">MATB42H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHY241H, PHY251H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYB21H3">
PHYB52H3:  Thermal Physics<p>Course Description</p><p>The quantum statistical basis of macroscopic systems; definition of entropy in terms of the number of accessible states of a many particle system leading to simple expressions for absolute temperature, the canonical distribution, and the laws of thermodynamics. Specific effects of quantum statistics at high densities and low temperatures.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PHYA21H3">PHYA21H3and <a href="/course/MATB41H3">MATB41H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MATB42H3">MATB42H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHY252H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYB52H3">
PHYB54H3:  Mechanics: From Oscillations to Chaos<p>Course Description</p><p>The linear, nonlinear and chaotic behaviour of classical mechanical systems such as oscillators, rotating bodies, and central field systems. The course will develop analytical and numerical tools to solve such systems and determine their basic properties. The course will include mathematical analysis, numerical exercises (Python), and demonstrations of mechanical systems.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PHYA21H3">PHYA21H3and <a href="/course/MATB41H3">MATB41H3and <a href="/course/MATB44H3">MATB44H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MATB42H3">MATB42H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHY254H, (PHYB20H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYB54H3">
PHYB56H3:  Introduction to Quantum Physics<p>Course Description</p><p>The course introduces the basic concepts of Quantum Physics and Quantum Mechanics starting with the experimental basis and the properties of the wave function. Schrödinger's equation will be introduced with some applications in one dimension. Topics include Stern-Gerlach effect; harmonic oscillator; uncertainty principle; interference packets; scattering and tunnelling in one-dimension.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PHYA21H3">PHYA21H3and [<a href="/course/MATA36H3">MATA36H3or <a href="/course/MATA37H3">MATA37H3</a>]<p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MATB41H3">MATB41H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHY256H1, (PHYB25H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYB56H3">
PHYB57H3:  Introduction to Scientific Computing<p>Course Description</p><p>Scientific computing is a rapidly growing field because computers can solve previously intractable problems and simulate natural processes governed by equations that do not have analytic solutions. During the first part of this course, students will learn numerical algorithms for various standard tasks such as root finding, integration, data fitting, interpolation and visualization. In the second part, students will learn how to model physical systems. At the end of the course, students will be expected to write small programs by themselves. Assignments will regularly include programming exercises.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/MATA36H3">MATA36H3or <a href="/course/MATA37H3">MATA37H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/MATA22H3">MATA22H3or <a href="/course/MATA23H3">MATA23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PHYA21H3">PHYA21H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MATB44H3">MATB44H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (PSCB57H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYB57H3">
PHYC11H3:  Intermediate Physics Laboratory II<p>Course Description</p><p>The main objective of this course is to help students develop skills in experimental physics by introducing them to a range of important measuring techniques and associated physical phenomena. Students will carry on several experiments in Physics and Astrophysics including electricity and magnetism, optics, solid state physics, atomic and nuclear physics.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PHYB10H3">PHYB10H3and <a href="/course/PHYB21H3">PHYB21H3and <a href="/course/PHYB52H3">PHYB52H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (PHYB11H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYC11H3">
PHYC14H3:  Introduction to Atmospheric Physics<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides an introduction to atmospheric physics. Topics include atmospheric structure, atmospheric thermodynamics, convection, general circulation of the atmosphere, radiation transfer within atmospheres and global energy balance. Connections will be made to topics such as climate change and air pollution.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PHYB21H3">PHYB21H3and <a href="/course/PHYB52H3">PHYB52H3and <a href="/course/MATB42H3">MATB42H3and <a href="/course/MATB44H3">MATB44H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHY392H1, PHY315H1, PHY351H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYC14H3">
PHYC50H3:  Electromagnetic Theory<p>Course Description</p><p>Solving Poisson and Laplace equations via method of images and separation of variables, Multipole expansion for electrostatics, atomic dipoles and polarizability, polarization in dielectrics, Ampere and Biot-Savart laws, Multipole expansion in magnetostatics, magnetic<br>dipoles, magnetization in matter, Maxwell’s equations in matter.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PHYB54H3">PHYB54H3and <a href="/course/PHYB21H3">PHYB21H3and [<a href="/course/MATA22H3">MATA22H3or <a href="/course/MATA23H3">MATA23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/MATB42H3">MATB42H3and <a href="/course/MATB44H3">MATB44H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHY350H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYC50H3">
PHYC54H3:  Classical Mechanics<p>Course Description</p><p>A course that will concentrate in the study of symmetry and conservation laws, stability and instability, generalized co-ordinates, Hamilton’s principle, Hamilton’s equations, phase space, Liouville’s theorem, canonical transformations, Poisson brackets, Noether’s theorem.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PHYB54H3">PHYB54H3and <a href="/course/MATB44H3">MATB44H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHY354H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYC54H3">
PHYC56H3:  Quantum Mechanics I<p>Course Description</p><p>The course builds on the basic concepts of quantum theory students learned in <a href="/course/PHYB56H3">PHYB56H3</a>. Topics include the general structure of wave mechanics; eigenfunctions and eigenvalues; operators; orbital angular momentum; spherical harmonics; central potential; separation of variables; hydrogen atom; Dirac notation; operator methods; harmonic oscillator and spin.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PHYB56H3">PHYB56H3and <a href="/course/PHYB21H3">PHYB21H3and [<a href="/course/MATA22H3">MATA22H3or <a href="/course/MATA23H3">MATA23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/MATB42H3">MATB42H3and <a href="/course/MATB44H3">MATB44H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHY356H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYC56H3">
PHYC83H3:  Introduction to General Relativity<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the basic principles and mathematics of General Relativity. Tensors will be presented after a review of Special Relativity. The metric, spacetime, curvature, and Einstein's field equations will be studied and applied to the Schwarzschild solution. Further topics include the Newtonian limit, classical tests, and black holes.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MATB42H3">MATB42H3and <a href="/course/MATB44H3">MATB44H3and <a href="/course/PHYB54H3">PHYB54H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MATC46H3">MATC46H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHY483H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYC83H3">
PHYD01H3:  Research Project in Physics and Astrophysics<p>Course Description</p><p>Introduces students to current research in physics or astrophysics under the supervision of a professorial faculty member. Students undertake an independent project that can be of a theoretical, computational or experimental nature. Evaluation is by the supervising faculty member in consultation with the course supervisor. Students must obtain consent of the course supervisor to enroll in this course.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 14.0 credits and cGPA of at least 2.5 and permission from the coordinator.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHY478H, PHY479Y1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PHYD01H3">
