POLD53H3:  Political Disagreement <p>Course Description</p><p>Why do people disagree in their opinions about abortion, same-sex marriage, crime and punishment, welfare, taxes, immigration, the environment, religion, and many other subjects? This course examines the major social scientific theories of political disagreement and applies these theories to an analysis of political disagreement.<br>Areas of Focus: Canadian Government and Politics, and Comparative Politics</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [(POLB50Y3) or [<a href="/course/POLB56H3">POLB56H3and <a href="/course/POLB57H3">POLB57H3</a>]] and [1.5 credits at the C-level in POL courses]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3or equivalent<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/POLD53H3">
POLD54H3:  Michi-Saagiig Nishnaabeg Nation Governance and Politics<p>Course Description</p><p>The campuses of the University of Toronto are situated on the territory of the Michi-Saagiig Nation (one of the nations that are a part of the Nishnaabeg). This course will introduce students to the legal, political, and socio-economic structures of the Michi-Saagiig Nishnaabeg Nation and discuss its relations with other Indigenous nations and confederacies, and with the Settler societies with whom the Michi-Saagiig Nishnaabeg have had contact since 1492. In an era of reconciliation, it is imperative for students to learn and understand the Indigenous nation upon whose territory we are meeting and learning. Therefore, course readings will address both Michi-Saagiig Nishnaabeg and Settler contexts. In addition to literature, there will be guest speakers from the current six (6) Michi-Saagiig Nishnaabeg communities that exist: Alderville, Mississaugas of the Credit, Mississaugi 8, Oshkigamig (Curve Lake), Pamitaashkodeyong (Burns/Hiawatha), and Scugog.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [(POLB50Y3) or [<a href="/course/POLB56H3">POLB56H3and <a href="/course/POLB57H3">POLB57H3</a>]] and [1.5 credits at the C-level in Political Science (POL and PPG courses)]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/POLC52H3">POLC52H3or POL308H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/POLD54H3">
POLD55H3:  The Politics of Equality and Inequality in Canada<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar provides an in-depth examination of the politics of inequality in Canada, and the role of the Canadian political-institutional framework in contributing to political, social and economic (in)equality. The focus will be on diagnosing how Canada’s political institutions variously impede and promote equitable treatment of different groups of Canadians (such as First Nations, women, racial and minority groups) and the feasibility of possible institutional and policy reforms to promote goals of social and economic equity.<br><br>Area of Focus: Canadian Government and Politics</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [(POLB50Y3) or [<a href="/course/POLB56H3">POLB56H3and <a href="/course/POLB57H3">POLB57H3</a>]] and [1.5 credits at the C-level in POL courses]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/POLD55H3">

POLD58H3:  The New Nationalism in Liberal Democracies<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the recent rise of ethnic nationalism in western liberal democracies, with a particular focus on the US, Canada, UK and France. It discusses the different perspectives on what is behind the rise of nationalism and populism, including economic inequality, antipathy with government, immigration, the role of political culture and social media.<br><br>Areas of Focus: Canadian Government and Politics</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit at the C-level in POL or PPG courses</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/POLC58H3'&gt;POLC58H3&lt;/a&gt;"><span><a href="/course/POLC58H3">POLC58H3</a></span></a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/POLD58H3">
POLD59H3:  Politics of Disability<p>Course Description</p><p>An in-depth analysis of the place and rights of disabled persons in contemporary society. POLD67H3:  The Limits of Rationality<p>Course Description</p><p>This course critically examines the relationship between politics, rationality, and public policy-making. The first half of the course surveys dominant rational actor models, critiques of these approaches, and alternative perspectives. The second half of the course explores pathological policy outcomes, arrived at through otherwise rational procedures.<br>Areas of Focus: Comparative Politics; Political Theory; Public Policy</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PPGB66H3">PPGB66H3</a>/(<a href="/course/PPGC66H3">PPGC66H3</a>/(POLC67H3) or [(POLB70H3) and (POLB71H3)] or <a href="/course/POLB72H3">POLB72H3</a>] or [<a href="/course/POLB90H3">POLB90H3and <a href="/course/POLB91H3">POLB91H3</a>] and [1.0 additional credit at the C-level in POL or PPG courses]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/POLD67H3">
POLD70H3:  Topics in Political Theory<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar explores the ways in which political theory can deepen our understanding of contemporary political issues. POLD74H3:  The Black Radical Tradition<p>Course Description</p><p>The Black radical tradition is a modern tradition of thought and action which began after transatlantic slavery’s advent. Contemporary social science and the humanities overwhelmingly portray the Black radical tradition as a critique of Black politics in its liberal, libertarian, and conservative forms. This course unsettles that framing: first by situating the Black radical tradition within Black politics; second, through expanding the boundaries of Black politics to include, yet not be limited to, theories and practices emanating from Canada and the United States; and third, by exploring whether it is more appropriate to claim the study of *the* Black radical tradition or a broader network of intellectual traditions underlying political theories of Black radicalism.</p> <p>Area of Focus: Political Theory</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/POLB72H3">POLB72H3or <a href="/course/POLC31H3">POLC31H3</a>] and [1.0 credit at the C-level in Political Science (POL and PPG courses)]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/POLD74H3">
POLD75H3:  Property and Power<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the concept of property as an enduring theme and object of debate in the history of political thought and contemporary political theory. Defining property and justifying its distribution has a significant impact on how citizens experience authority, equality, freedom, and justice. The course will analyze different theoretical approaches to property in light of how they shape and/or challenge relations of class, race, gender, and other lines of difference and inequality.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 0.5 credit from: [<a href="/course/POLB72H3">POLB72H3</a>, <a href="/course/POLC70H3">POLC70H3</a>, <a href="/course/POLC71H3">POLC71H3or <a href="/course/POLC73H3">POLC73H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/POLD75H3">

POLD90H3:  Public Policy and Human Development in the Global South<p>Course Description</p><p>While domestic and international political factors have discouraged pro human development public policies in much of the global south, there have been some important success stories. POLD91H3:  Protests and Social Movements in Comparative Perspective <p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines contentious politics from a comparative perspective, beginning with the foundational theories of Charles Tilly, Sidney Tarrow, and Doug McAdam. It explores questions such as why people protest, how they organize, and the outcomes of contention. The second half of the course challenges students to examine popular contention across a range of states in Asia, the Middle East, Europe, and Latin America. It asks students to interrogate the applicability of the dynamics of contention framework to illiberal states in a comparative context.<br>Area of Focus: Comparative Politics</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.5 credits at the C-level in POL courses<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: POL451H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/POLD91H3">
POLD92H3:  Survival and Demise of Dictatorships<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will provide an introduction to theories of why some dictatorships survive while others do not. We will explore theories rooted in regime type, resources, state capacity, parties, popular protest, and leadership. We will then examine the utility of these approaches through in-depth examinations of regime crises in Ethiopia, Iran, China, the USSR, and South Africa.<br>Area of Focus: Comparative Politics</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/POLB90H3">POLB90H3or <a href="/course/POLB91H3">POLB91H3</a>] and [an additional 2.0 credits at the C-level in any courses]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/POLD92H3">

PPGD64H3:  Comparative Public Policy<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar course explores some of the major theoretical approaches to the comparative analysis of public policies across countries. PPGD68H3:  Capstone: The Policy Process in Theory and Practice<p>Course Description</p><p>A review and application of theories of public policy. A case-based approach is used to illuminate the interplay of evidence (scientific data, etc.) and political considerations in the policy process, through stages of agenda-setting, formulation, decision-making, implementation and evaluation. Cases will be drawn from Canada, the United States and other industrialized democracies, and include contemporary and historical policies.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PPGB66H3">PPGB66H3and [(POLB50Y3) or [<a href="/course/POLB56H3">POLB56H3and <a href="/course/POLB57H3">POLB57H3</a>]] and [1.5 credits at the C-level in POL courses]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PPGD68H3">
PSCB90H3:  Physical Sciences Research Experience<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides an opportunity for students to work with a faculty member, Students will provide assistance with one of the faculty member's research projects, while also earning credit. Students will gain first-hand exposure to current research methods, and share in the excitement of discovery of knowledge acquisition. Progress will be monitored by regular meetings with the faculty member and through a reflective journal. Final results will be presented in a written report and/or a poster presentation at the end of the term. Approximately 120 hours of work is expected for the course.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Permission of the Course Coordinator</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: Completion of at least 4.0 credits in a relevant discipline.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Students must send an application to the course Coordinator for admission into this course. Applications must be received by the end of August for Fall enrolment, December 15th for Winter enrolment, and end of April for Summer enrolment. Typically, students enrolled in a program offered by the Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences and students who have a CGPA of at least 2.5 or higher are granted admission. Approved students will receive a signed course enrolment form that will be submitted to the Office of the Registrar.

Applications will include: 
1) A letter of intent indicating the student's wish to enrol in the course;
2) A list of relevant courses successfully completed by the student, as well as any relevant courses to be taken during the upcoming semester; 
3) Submission of the preferred project form, indicating the top four projects of interest to the student. This form is available from the Course Coordinator, along with the project descriptions.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSCB90H3">
PSCD01H3:  The Physical Sciences in Contemporary Society<p>Course Description</p><p>Current issues involving physical science in modern society. Topics include: complex nature of the scientific method; inter-connection between theory, concepts and experimental data; characteristics of premature, pathological and pseudo-science; organization and funding of scientific research in Canada; role of communication and publishing; public misunderstanding of scientific method. These will be discussed using issues arising in chemistry, computer science, earth sciences, mathematics and physics.<br><strong>Note:</strong> Where <a href="http://www.utsc.utoronto.ca/%7Eregistrar/calendars/calendar/Physical_Sciences.html#&lt;a href='/course/PSCD01H3'&gt;PSCD01H3&lt;/a&gt;"><a href="/course/PSCD01H3">PSCD01H3</a>is a Program requirement, it may be replaced by PHY341H with the approval of the Program supervisor. <br>Prerequisite: Completion of at least one-half of the credits required in any one of the programs offered by the Department of Physical &amp; Environmental Sciences.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-corequisite"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-corequisite">Corequisite: Continued participation in one of the Physical and Environmental Sciences programs.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHY341H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSCD01H3">
PSCD02H3:  Current Questions in Mathematics and Science<p>Course Description</p><p>Topics of current prominence arising in chemistry, computer science, earth sciences, mathematics and physics will be discussed, usually by faculty or outside guests who are close to the areas of prominence. Topics will vary from year to year as the subject areas evolve.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Completion of at least 3.5 credits of a Physical Sciences program<p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: Continued participation in one of the Physical Sciences programs or enrolment in the Minor Program in Natural Sciences and Environmental Management<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHY342H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSCD02H3">
PSCD11H3:  Communicating Science: Film, Media, Journalism, and Society<p>Course Description</p><p>Communicating complex science issues to a wider audience remains a major challenge. This course will use film, media, journalism and science experts to explore the role of science and scientists in society. Students will engage with media and academic experts to get an insight into the ‘behind the scenes’ world of filmmaking, media, journalism, and scientific reporting. The course will be of interest to all students of environmental science, media, education, journalism and political science.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any 14.5 credits<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (PSCA01H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSCD11H3">
PSCD50H3:  Advanced Topics in Quantum Mechanics<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides exposure to a variety of theoretical concepts and practical methods for treating various problems in quantum mechanics. Topics include perturbation theory, variational approach, adiabatic approximation, mean field approximation, Hamiltonian symmetry implementation, light-matter interaction, second quantization.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: Any one of the following courses [<a href="/course/PHYC56H3">PHYC56H3or <a href="/course/CHMC20H3">CHMC20H3or (CHMC25H3)]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PHY456H, CHM423H, CHM421H, JCP421H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSCD50H3">

PSYB03H3:  Introduction to Computers in Psychological Research<p>Course Description</p><p>The course will provide introductory knowledge and hands-on training in computer-based implementations of experimental design, data processing and result interpretation in psychology. PSYB07H3:  Data Analysis in Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course focuses on the fundamentals of the theory and the application of statistical procedures used in research in the field of psychology. Topics will range from descriptive statistics to simple tests of significance, such as Chi-Square, t-tests, and one-way Analysis-of-Variance. A working knowledge of algebra is assumed. </p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (ANTC35H3), <a href="/course/LINB29H3">LINB29H3</a>, <a href="/course/MGEB11H3">MGEB11H3</a>/(ECMB11H3), <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3</a>/(ECMB12H3), PSY201H, (SOCB06H3), <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a>, <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>, <a href="/course/STAB52H3">STAB52H3</a>, STA220H, STA221H, STA250H, STA257H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology, Mental Health Studies, Neuroscience, and Paramedicine. PSYB10H3:  Introduction to Social Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>Surveys a wide range of phenomena relating to social behaviour.<br>Social Psychology is the study of how feelings, thoughts, and behaviour are influenced by the presence of others. The course is designed to explore social behaviour and to present theory and research that foster its understanding.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY220H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYB10H3">
PSYB20H3:  Introduction to Developmental Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>Developmental processes during infancy and childhood.<br>This course presents students with a broad and integrative overview of child development. Major theories and research findings will be discussed in order to understand how the child changes physically, socially, emotionally, and cognitively with age. Topics are organized chronologically beginning with prenatal development and continuing through selected issues in adolescence and life-span development.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY210H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYB20H3">

The ideas of classical personality theorists will be discussed in reference to findings from contemporary personality research.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY230H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYB30H3">
PSYB32H3:  Introduction to Clinical Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>Clinical psychology examines why people behave, think, and feel in unexpected, sometimes bizarre, and typically self-defeating ways. This course will focus on the ways in which clinicians have been trying to learn the causes of various clinical disorders and what they know about preventing and alleviating it.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY240H, PSY340H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYB32H3">
PSYB38H3:  Introduction to Behaviour Modification<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to behaviour modification, focusing on attempts to regulate human behaviour. Basic principles and procedures of behaviour change are examined, including their application across different domains and populations. Topics include operant and respondent conditioning; reinforcement; extinction; punishment; behavioural data; ethics; and using behaviourally-based approaches (e.g., CBT) to treat psychopathology.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY260H1, (PSYB45H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYB38H3">
PSYB51H3:  Introduction to Perception<p>Course Description</p><p>Theory and research on perception and cognition, including visual, auditory and tactile perception, representation, and communication. Topics include cognition and perception in the handicapped and normal perceiver; perceptual illusion, noise, perspective, shadow patterns and motion, possible and impossible scenes, human and computer scene-analysis, ambiguity in perception, outline representation. The research is on adults and children, and different species. Demonstrations and exercises form part of the course work.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/NROC64H3">NROC64H3</a>, PSY280H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYB51H3">
PSYB55H3:  Introduction to Cognitive Neuroscience<p>Course Description</p><p>The course explores how the brain gives rise to the mind. It examines the role of neuroimaging tools and brain-injured patients in helping to uncover cognitive networks. Select topics include attention, memory, language, motor control, decision-making, emotion, and executive functions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY493H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYB55H3">
PSYB57H3:  Introduction to Cognitive Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>A discussion of theories and experiments examining human cognition. This includes the history of the study of human information processing and current thinking about mental computation. Topics covered include perception, attention, thinking, memory, visual imagery, language and problem solving.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY270H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYB57H3">
PSYB64H3:  Introduction to Behavioural Neuroscience<p>Course Description</p><p>A survey of the biological mechanisms underlying fundamental psychological processes intended for students who are not in a Neuroscience program. Topics include the biological basis of motivated behaviour (e.g., emotional, ingestive, sexual, and reproductive behaviours; sleep and arousal), sensory processes and attention, learning and memory, and language.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/NROC61H3">NROC61H3</a>, PSY290H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYB64H3">
PSYB70H3:  Methods in Psychological Science<p>Course Description</p><p>This course focuses on scientific literacy skills central to effectively consuming and critiquing research in psychological science. Students will learn about commonly used research designs, how to assess whether a design has been applied correctly, and whether the conclusions drawn from the data are warranted. Students will also develop skills to effectively find and consume primary research in psychology.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY203H1, (PSYB01H3), (PSYB04H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYB70H3">
PSYB80H3:  Psychology in Context <p>Course Description</p><p><span>This course builds upon foundational concepts from Introduction to Psychology</span><span> and</span> <span>examin</span><span>es</span><span> the field of psychological science from a critical perspective. </span><span>Students will </span><span>explore</span><span> the contextual underpinnings</span><span> of the field and learn </span><span>about current debates and challenges</span><span> facing various subfields of psychology</span><span>.</span> <span>Specific topics will vary by term according to the interests </span><span>and </span><span>expertise</span> <span>of the course instructor and guest lecturers. Examination of these topics will include considerations such as bias in the sciences, demographic representation in participant pools, methodological diversity, replicability, </span><span>and </span><span>ecological validity</span><span>.</span></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3</a>, <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <span><span><a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a></span></span><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op, Major/Major Co-op and Minor programs in Psychology, Mental Health Studies, and Neuroscience. PSYB83H3:  Psychology and Videogames<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will use videogames as a framework for exploring key concepts and findings in psychology, drawing on both player experiences and research findings. We will survey topics from cognitive, clinical, social, personality, and developmental psychology including intersection points between disciplines. We will also explore cross-cutting issues related to research methods, ethics, diversity, and representation.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the "Psychology &amp; Health Studies" admission category. PSYB90H3:  Supervised Introductory Research in Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides an introduction to, and experience in, ongoing theoretical and empirical research in any field of psychology. Supervision of the work is arranged by mutual agreement between student and instructor. Students will typically engage in an existing research project within a supervisor’s laboratory. Regular consultation with the supervisor is necessary, which will enhance communication skills and enable students to develop proficiency in speaking about scientific knowledge with other experts in the domain. Students will also develop documentation and writing skills through a final report and research journal. This course requires students to complete a permission form obtained from the Department of Psychology. This form must outline agreed-upon work that will be performed, must be signed by the intended supervisor, and returned to the Department of Psychology.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3with at least an 80% average across both courses] and [a minimum of 4.0 credits [including <a href="/course/PSYA01H3">PSYA01H3and <a href="/course/PSYA02H3">PSYA02H3</a>] in any discipline, with an average cGPA of 3.0] and [a maximum of 9.5 credits completed] and [enrolment in a Psychology, Mental Health Studies, Neuroscience or Psycholinguistics program].<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: ROP299Y and <a href="/course/LINB98H3">LINB98H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: B-level courses in Psychology or Psycholinguistics<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Students receive a half credit spread across two-terms; therefore, the research in this course must take place across two consecutive terms. Priority will be given to students in the Specialist and Major programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies, followed by students in the Specialist and Major programs in Neuroscience and Psycholinguistics. Enrolment will depend each year on the research opportunities available with each individual faculty member and the interests of the students who apply.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYB90H3">
PSYC01H3:  History of Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>A survey of developments in Western philosophy and science which influenced the emergence of modern psychology in the second half of the Nineteenth Century. Three basic problems are considered: mind-body, epistemology (science of knowledge), and behaviour/motivation/ethics. We begin with the ancient Greek philosophers, and then consider the contributions of European scholars from the Fifteenth through Nineteenth Centuries. Twentieth Century schools are discussed including: psychoanalysis, functionalism, structuralism, gestalt, behaviourism, and phenomenology.<br></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3and [1.0 additional credit at the B-level in PSY courses]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (PSYC85H3), PSY450H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to third- and fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. <br>Third- and fourth-year students in the Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC01H3">
PSYC02H3:  Scientific Communication in Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>How we communicate in psychology and why. The differences between scientific and non-scientific approaches to behaviour and their implications for communication are discussed. The focus is on improving the student's ability to obtain and organize information and to communicate it clearly and critically, using the conventions of the discipline.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Psychology, Mental Health Studies, and Neuroscience (Cognitive stream), and the Specialist Co-op program in Neuroscience (Stage 1). Students in the Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC02H3">
PSYC03H3:  Computers in Psychological Research: Advanced Topics<p>Course Description</p><p>The course will provide advanced knowledge and hands-on training in computer-based implementations of experimental design, data processing and result interpretation in psychology. The course covers implementations of experimental testing paradigms, computational explorations of empirical data structure, and result visualization with the aid of specific programming tools (e.g., Python).</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB03H3">PSYB03H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs Psychology, Mental Health Studies, and Neuroscience - Cognitive stream. PSYC08H3:  Advanced Data Analysis in Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>The primary focus of this course is on the understanding of Analysis-of-Variance and its application to various research designs. Examples will include <em>a priori</em> and <em>post hoc</em> tests. Finally, there will be an introduction to multiple regression, including discussions of design issues and interpretation problems.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (STAC52H3), PSY202H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Psychology, Mental Health Studies, Neuroscience, and Paramedicine. Students in the Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology, Mental Health Studies, and Neuroscience will be permitted if space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC08H3">
PSYC09H3:  Applied Multiple Regression in Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to multiple regression and its applications in psychological research. The course covers the data analysis process from data collection to interpretation: how to deal with missing data, the testing of assumptions, addressing problem of multicolinearity, significance testing, and deciding on the most appropriate model. Several illustrative data sets will be explored in detail. The course contains a brief introduction to factor analysis. The goal is to provide the students with the skills and understanding to conduct and interpret data analysis in non-experimental areas of psychology.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/LINC29H3">LINC29H3</a>, <a href="/course/MGEC11H3">MGEC11H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Psychology, Mental Health Studies, Neuroscience - Cognitive stream, and Neuroscience - Stage 1. Students in the Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology, Mental Health Studies, and Neuroscience will be permitted if space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC09H3">
PSYC10H3:  Judgment and Decision Making<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the psychology of judgment and decision making, incorporating perspectives from social psychology, cognitive psychology, and behavioral economics. Understanding these topics will allow students to identify errors and systematic biases in their own decisions and improve their ability to predict and influence the behavior of others.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3or <a href="/course/PSYB57H3">PSYB57H3or <a href="/course/PSYC57H3">PSYC57H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/MGMD02H3">MGMD02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. PSYC12H3:  The Psychology of Prejudice<p>Course Description</p><p>A detailed examination of selected social psychological topics introduced in <a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3</a>. This course examines the nature of attitudes, stereotypes and prejudice, including their development, persistence, and automaticity. PSYC13H3:  Social Cognition: Understanding Ourselves and Others<p>Course Description</p><p>A comprehensive survey of how cognitive processes (e.g., perception, memory, judgment) influence social behaviour. Topics include the construction of knowledge about self and others, attitude formation and change, influences of automatic and controlled processing, biases in judgment and choice, interactions between thought and emotion, and neural specializations for social cognition.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3or <a href="/course/PSYB57H3">PSYB57H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY326H, PSY473H, PSY417H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology, Mental Health Studies, and Neuroscience. PSYC14H3:  Cross-Cultural Social Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>A survey of the role of culture in social thought and behaviour. The focus is on research and theory that illustrate ways in which culture influences behaviour and cognition about the self and others, emotion and motivation. Differences in individualism and collectivism, independence and interdependence as well as other important orientations that differ between cultures will be discussed. Social identity and its impact on acculturation in the context of immigration will also be explored.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY321H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. PSYC15H3:  Foundations in Community Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>Community psychology is an area of psychology that examines the social, cultural, and structural influences that promote positive change, health, and empowerment among communities and community members. This course will offer an overview of the foundational components of community psychology including its theories, research methods, and applications to topics such as community mental health, prevention programs, interventions, the community practitioner as social change agent, and applications of community psychology to other settings and situations.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. PSYC16H3:  Psychology of Imagination<p>Course Description</p><p>The course will examine different aspects of imagination in a historical context, including creativity, curiosity, future-mindedness, openness to experience, perseverance, perspective, purpose, and wisdom along with its neural foundations.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB20H3">PSYB20H3or <a href="/course/PSYB30H3">PSYB30H3or <a href="/course/PSYB51H3">PSYB51H3or <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3or <a href="/course/PSYB57H3">PSYB57H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. PSYC17H3:  Meeting Minds: The Psychology of Interpersonal Interactions<p>Course Description</p><p>What happens when two (or more) minds meet—how do they interact and interconnect? Specifically, how do people “get on the same page,” and what are barriers that might stand in the way? Guided by these questions, this course will provide a broad overview of the psychological phenomena and processes that enable interpersonal connection. We will examine the various ways that people’s inner states—thoughts, feelings, intentions, and identities—connect with one another. We will study perspectives from both perceivers (i.e., how to understand others) and targets (i.e., how to be understood), at levels of dyads (i.e., how two minds become interconnected) and groups (i.e., how minds coordinate and work collectively). Throughout the course, we will consider challenges to effective interpersonal interactions, and solutions and strategies that promote and strengthen interconnection. A range of perspectives, including those from social, cognitive, personality, developmental, and cultural psychology, as well as adjacent disciplines such as communication, will be considered.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Minor program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC17H3">
PSYC18H3:  The Psychology of Emotion<p>Course Description</p><p>What is an emotion? How are emotions experienced and how are they shaped? What purpose do emotions serve to human beings? What happens when our emotional responses go awry? Philosophers have debated these questions for centuries. Fortunately, psychological science has equipped us with the tools to explore such questions on an empirical level. Building with these tools, this course will provide a comprehensive overview of the scientific study of emotion. Topics will include how emotions are expressed in our minds and bodies, how emotions influence (and are influenced by) our thoughts, relationships, and cultures, and how emotions can both help us thrive and make us sick. A range of perspectives, including social, cultural, developmental, clinical, personality, and cognitive psychology, will be considered.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3or <a href="/course/PSYB30H3">PSYB30H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY331H, PSY494H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Minor program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC18H3">
PSYC19H3:  Psychology of Self Control<p>Course Description</p><p>A detailed examination of how organisms exercise control, bringing thoughts, emotions and behaviours into line with preferred standards. Topics include executive function, the neural bases for self control, individual differences in control, goal setting and goal pursuit, motivation, the interplay of emotion and control, controversies surrounding fatigue and control, and decision-making.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. PSYC28H3:  Emotional Development<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will provide students with a comprehensive understanding of the biological, cognitive, and social factors that shape emotional development in infancy and childhood. PSYC30H3:  Advanced Personality Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is intended to advance students' understanding of contemporary personality theory and research. PSYC31H3:  Neuropsychological Assessment<p>Course Description</p><p>The clinical practice of neuropsychological assessment is an <em>applied science</em> that is concerned with the behavioural expression of personality, emotional, somatic and, or brain dysfunction with an emphasis on how diversity (e.g., cultural, racial, gender, sexuality, class, religion, other aspects of identity and the intersections among these), can further mediate this relationship. The clinical neuropsychologist uses standardized tests to objectively describe the breadth, severity and veracity of emotional, cognitive, behavioral and intellectual functioning. Inferences are made on the basis of accumulated research. The clinical neuropsychologist interprets every aspect of the examination (both quantitative and qualitative components) to ascertain the relative emotional, cognitive, behavioural and intellectual strengths and weaknesses of a patient with suspected or known (neuro)psychopathology. Findings from a neuropsychological examination can be used to make diagnoses, inform rehabilitation strategies, and direct various aspects of patient care. In this course, we will comprehensively explore the science and applied practice of neuropsychological assessment.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB32H3">PSYB32H3and <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (PSYC32H3), (PSY393H)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Minor Program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC31H3">
PSYC34H3:  The Psychology of Happiness and Meaning<p>Course Description</p><p>The philosopher Aristotle proposed long ago that a good life consists of two core elements: happiness (hedonia) and a sense of meaning (eudaimonia). What is happiness and meaning, and how do they relate to psychological wellbeing? How do these desired states or traits change across life, and can they be developed with specific interventions? What roles do self-perception and social relationships play in these phenomena? We will focus on the conceptual, methodological, and philosophical issues underlying these questions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY336H1, PSY324H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Minor program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC34H3">
PSYC36H3:  Psychotherapy<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will provide students with an introduction to prominent behavioural change theories (i.e. psychodynamic, cognitive/behavioural, humanist/existential) as well as empirical evidence on their efficacy. The role of the therapist, the patient and the processes involved in psychotherapy in producing positive outcomes will be explored.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB32H3">PSYB32H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY343H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Restricted to students in the Mental Health Studies programs.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC36H3">
PSYC37H3:  Psychological Assessment<p>Course Description</p><p>This course deals with conceptual issues and practical problems of identification, assessment, and treatment of mental disorders and their psychological symptomatology. Students have the opportunity to familiarize themselves with the psychological tests and the normative data used in mental health assessments. Lectures and demonstrations on test administration and interpretation will be provided.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB32H3">PSYB32H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY330H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Restricted to students in the Mental Health Studies programs.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC37H3">
PSYC38H3:  Adult Psychopathology <p>Course Description</p><p>This course will provide an advanced understanding of the etiology, psychopathology, and treatment of common mental disorders in adults. Theory and research will be discussed emphasizing biological, psychological, and social domains of functioning. Cultural influences in the presentation of psychopathology will also be discussed.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB32H3">PSYB32H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY340H1, PSY342H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Restricted to students in the Mental Health Studies programs.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC38H3">
PSYC39H3:  Psychology and the Law<p>Course Description</p><p>This course focuses on the application of psychology to the law, particularly criminal law including cognitive, neuropsychological and personality applications to fitness to stand trial, criminal responsibility, risk for violent and sexual recidivism and civil forensic psychology.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB32H3">PSYB32H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY328H, PSY344H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Minor program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC39H3">
PSYC50H3:  Higher-Level Cognition<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines advanced cognitive functions through a cognitive psychology lens. Topics covered include: thinking, reasoning, decision-making, problem-solving, creativity, and consciousness. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3or <a href="/course/PSYB57H3">PSYB57H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. PSYC51H3:  Cognitive Neuroscience of Vision<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will provide an in-depth examination of research in the field of visual cognitive neuroscience. Topics will include the visual perception of object features (shape, colour, texture), the perception of high-level categories (objects, faces, bodies, scenes), visual attention, and comparisons between the human and monkey visual systems.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB51H3">PSYB51H3or <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3or <a href="/course/NROC64H3">NROC64H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY380H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience, and the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Major program in Neuroscience and the Minor program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC51H3">
PSYC52H3:  Cognitive Neuroscience of Attention<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is about understanding how the human brain collects information from the environment so as to perceive it and to interact with it. The first section of the course will look into the neural and cognitive mechanisms that perceptual systems use to extract important information from the environment. Section two will focus on how attention prioritizes information for action. Additional topics concern daily life applications of attentional research.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB51H3">PSYB51H3or <a href="/course/NROC64H3">NROC64H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3or <a href="/course/PSYB57H3">PSYB57H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY475H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience, and the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Major program in Neuroscience and the Minor program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC52H3">
PSYC53H3:  Cognitive Neuroscience of Memory<p>Course Description</p><p>An exploration of how the brain supports different forms of memory, drawing on evidence from electrophysiological, patient neuropsychological and neuroimaging research. Topics include short-term working memory, general knowledge of the world (semantic memory), implicit memory, and memory for personally experienced events (episodic memory).</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY372H</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/PSYB57H3">PSYB57H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience, and the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Major program in Neuroscience and the Minor program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC53H3">
PSYC54H3:  Auditory Cognitive Neuroscience<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides an overview of the cognitive and neural processes that underlie humans’ auditory abilities. Core topics include psychoacoustics, the auditory cortex and its interconnectedness to other brain structures, auditory scene analysis, as well as special topics such as auditory disorders. Insights into these different topics will be provided from research using behavioural, neuroimaging, computational, and neuropsychological techniques.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB51H3">PSYB51H3or <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3or <a href="/course/NROC64H3">NROC64H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience, and the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Major program in Neuroscience and the Minor program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC54H3">
PSYC56H3:  Music Cognition<p>Course Description</p><p>Studies the perceptual and cognitive processing involved in musical perception and performance. This class acquaints students with the basic concepts and issues involved in the understanding of musical passages. Topics will include discussion of the physical and psychological dimensions of sound, elementary music theory, pitch perception and melodic organization, the perception of rhythm and time, musical memory, musical performance, and emotion and meaning in music.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB51H3">PSYB51H3or <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3or <a href="/course/PSYB57H3">PSYB57H3or <a href="/course/NROC64H3">NROC64H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Minor program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC56H3">
PSYC57H3:  Cognitive Neuroscience of Decision Making<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will introduce students to current understanding, and ongoing debates, about how the brain makes both simple and complex decisions. Findings from single-cell neurophysiology, functional neuroimaging, and computational modeling will be used to illuminate fundamental aspects of choice, including reward prediction, value representation, action selection, and self-control.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/PSYB03H3">PSYB03H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience, and the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Minor program in Psychology and Major program in Neuroscience will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC57H3">
PSYC59H3:  Cognitive Neuroscience of Language<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides an overview of the cognitive and neural processes and representations that underlie language abilities. Core topics include first language acquisition, second language acquisition and bilingualism, speech comprehension, and reading. Insights into these different abilities will be provided from research using behavioural, neuroimaging, computational, and neuropsychological techniques.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB51H3">PSYB51H3or <a href="/course/PSYB57H3">PSYB57H3or <a href="/course/NROC64H3">NROC64H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience, and the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op program in Psycholinguistics, the Major program in Neuroscience, and the Minor program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC59H3">
PSYC62H3:  Drugs and the Brain<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of behavioural and neurobiological mechanisms underlying the phenomenon of drug dependence. Topics will include principles of behavioural pharmacology and pharmacokinetics, neurobiological mechanisms of drug action, and psychotropic drug classification. In addition, concepts of physical and psychological dependence, tolerance, sensitization, and reinforcement and aversion will also be covered.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB64H3">PSYB64H3or <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3or <a href="/course/NROB60H3">NROB60H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY396H, PCL475Y, PCL200H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology, Mental Health Studies, and Neuroscience. Students in the Minor program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC62H3">
PSYC70H3:  Advanced Research Methods Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>The course focuses on methodological skills integral to becoming a producer of psychological research. Students will learn how to identify knowledge gaps in the literature, to use conceptual models to visualize hypothetical relationships, to select a research design most appropriate for their questions, and to interpret more complex patterns of data.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Psychology, Mental Health Studies, and Neuroscience (Cognitive stream), and the Specialist Co-op program in Neuroscience (Stage 1). Students in the Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC70H3">
PSYC71H3:  Social Psychology Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>Introduces conceptual and practical issues concerning research in social psychology, and provides experience with several different types of research.<br> This course is designed to consider in depth various research approaches used in social psychology (such as attitude questionnaires, observational methods for studying ongoing social interaction). Discussion and laboratory work.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYC02H3">PSYC02H3and <a href="/course/PSYC70H3">PSYC70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY329H, (PSYC11H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Psychology.
