PSYC72H3:  Developmental Psychology Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces conceptual and practical issues concerning research in developmental psychology. Developmental psychology focuses on the process of change within and across different phases of the life-span. Reflecting the broad range of topics in this area, there are diverse research methods, including techniques for studying infant behaviour as well as procedures for studying development in children, adolescents, and adults. This course will cover a representative sample of some of these approaches.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB20H3">PSYB20H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYC02H3">PSYC02H3and <a href="/course/PSYC70H3">PSYC70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY319H, (PSYC26H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Psychology. Students in the Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Mental Health Studies will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC72H3">
PSYC73H3:  Wellness and Resilience Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>A widespread survey on techniques derived from clinical psychology interventions and wellness and resilience research paired with the applied practice and implementation of those techniques designed specifically for students in Mental Health Studies. Students will attend a lecture reviewing the research and details of each technique/topic. The laboratory component will consist of interactive, hands-on experience in close group settings with a number of techniques related to emotion, stress, wellness, and resilience. These are specifically tailored for university student populations.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB32H3">PSYB32H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/PSYC02H3">PSYC02H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist Co-op program in Mental Health Studies. Students in the Specialist, Major, and Major Co-op programs in Mental Health Studies will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC73H3">
PSYC74H3:  Human Movement Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>In this course students will be introduced to the study of human movement across a range of topics (e.g., eye-movements, balance, and walking), and will have the opportunity to collect and analyze human movement data. Additional topics include basic aspects of experimental designs, data analysis and interpretation of such data.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/PSYC02H3">PSYC02H3and <a href="/course/PSYC70H3">PSYC70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Psychology and Neuroscience - Systems/Behavioural stream. <br>Students in the Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Mental Health Studies will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC74H3">
PSYC75H3:  Cognitive Psychology Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces conceptual and practical issues concerning research in cognitive psychology. Students will be introduced to current research methods through a series of practical exercises conducted on computers. By the end of the course, students will be able to program experiments, manipulate data files, and conduct basic data analyses.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB51H3">PSYB51H3or <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3or <a href="/course/PSYB57H3">PSYB57H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYC02H3">PSYC02H3and <a href="/course/PSYC70H3">PSYC70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY379H</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/PSYC08H3">PSYC08H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Psychology and Neuroscience - Cognitive stream. <br>Students in the Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Mental Health Studies will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC75H3">
PSYC76H3:  Brain Imaging Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>The course introduces brain imaging techniques, focusing on techniques such as high-density electroencephalography (EEG) and transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS), together with magnet-resonance-imaging-based neuronavigation. Furthermore, the course will introduce eye movement recordings as a behavioural measure often co-registered in imaging studies. Students will learn core principles of experimental designs, data analysis and interpretation in a hands-on manner.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYC02H3">PSYC02H3and <a href="/course/PSYC70H3">PSYC70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (PSYC04H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Psychology and Neuroscience - Cognitive stream. <br>Students in the Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Mental Health Studies will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC76H3">
PSYC81H3:  Psychology for Sustainability<p>Course Description</p><p><span><span>This course will introduce students to a variety of topics in psychology as they relate to climate change</span><span> and </span></span><span><span>the</span><span> psychological study of sustainable human behaviour</span></span><span><span>. Topics covered will include the threats of a changing environment to mental health and wellbeing; the development of coping mechanisms and resilience for individuals and communities affected negatively by </span></span><span><span>climate change</span> </span><span><span>and</span></span><span> <span>a changing environment</span></span><span><span>;</span><span> perceptions of risk, and how beliefs and attitudes are developed, maintained, and updated; effective principles for communicating about climate change</span> </span><span><span>and sustainable behaviour</span></span><span><span>; how social identity affects experiences and perceptions of </span></span><span><span>a changing environment</span></span><span><span>; empirically validated methods for promoting pro-environmental behaviour; and how, when required, we can best motivate people to action.</span></span></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3and [0.5 additional credits at the B-level in PSY courses]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (PSYC58H3) if taken in Winter 2022 or Winter 2023<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Minor program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC81H3">
PSYC86H3:  The Unconscious Mind<p>Course Description</p><p>The concept of the unconscious mind has been integral to our understanding of human behavior ever since Freud introduced the concept in 1915. In this course, we will survey the history of the concept of the unconscious and discuss contemporary theory and research into the nature of the unconscious. Topics such as implicit cognition, non-conscious learning, decision-making, and measurement of non-conscious processes will be discussed from social, cognitive, clinical, and neuroscience perspectives. We will explore the applications and implications of such current research on the unconscious mind for individuals, culture, and society.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB32H3">PSYB32H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3or <a href="/course/PSYB57H3">PSYB57H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Minor program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC86H3">
PSYC87H3:  Psychology and Money<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is designed for students interested in understanding the psychological influences on financial decision making, as well as the interplay between macroeconomic forces and psychological processes. Starting with a psychological and historical exploration of money's evolution, the course covers a wide range of topics. These include the impact of economic conditions like inflation and inequality on well-being, the psychology of household financial behaviours, including financial literacy and debt management, and the motivations affecting investment choices. The course also examines marketing psychology, the influence of money on interpersonal relationships, and the psychology of charitable giving. Finally, it investigates the psychological implications of emerging financial technologies.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3or <a href="/course/PSYB30H3">PSYB30H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Students in the Minor program in Psychology will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC87H3">
PSYC90H3:  Supervised Study in Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>An intensive research project intended to provide laboratory/field experience in data collection and analysis. The project must be completed over 2 consecutive terms.<br>These courses provide an opportunity to engage in research in an area after completing basic coverage in regularly scheduled courses. The student must demonstrate a background adequate for the project proposed and should present a clear rationale to prospective supervisors. Regular consultation with the supervisor is necessary, and extensive data collection/processing and analysis will ordinarily be required. Such a project will culminate in a written research report.<br>Students must first find a supervisor before the start of the academic term in which the project will be initiated. They must then obtain a <a href="https://utoronto.sharepoint.com/sites/utsc-psychology/SitePages/UG_Research-Courses.aspx">permission formfrom the Department of Psychology's website that is to be completed and signed by the intended supervisor, and returned to the Psychology Office. <br>Students seeking supervision off campus are further advised to check the appropriateness of the proposed advisor with the Program Supervisor. If the proposed supervisor is not appointed to the Psychology faculty at UTSC then a secondary advisor, that is appointed at UTSC, will be required.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3and [2.0 additional PSY credits] and [enrolment in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op, or Major/Major Co-op Program in Psychology, Mental Health Studies, Neuroscience, or Psycholinguistics] and [a CGPA of 3.0 or higher] and permission of the proposed supervisor.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (COGC91H3), <a href="/course/NROC90H3">NROC90H3</a>, PSY303H, PSY304H</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYC90H3">

PSYD10H3:  Community and Applied Social Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the applications of social psychological theory and research to understand and address social issues that affect communities. In doing so the course bridges knowledge from the areas of social psychology and community psychology. In the process, students will have the opportunity to gain a deeper understanding of how theories and research in social psychology can be used to explain everyday life, community issues, and societal needs and how, reciprocally, real-life issues can serve to guide the direction of social psychological theories and research.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3and [0.5 credit at the C-level from PSY courses in the 10-series or 30-series] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/PSYD15H3">PSYD15H3(if taken in Spring or Fall 2019)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. PSYD13H3:  The Psychology of Emotion Regulation<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar offers an in depth introduction to the recent scientific literature on how humans manage and control their emotions (emotion regulation). We will explore why, and how, people regulate emotions, how emotion regulation differs across individuals and cultures, and the influence that emotion regulation has upon mental, physical, and social well-being.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3and [<a href="/course/PSYC13H3">PSYC13H3or <a href="/course/PSYC18H3">PSYC18H3or <a href="/course/PSYC19H3">PSYC19H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/PSYD15H3">PSYD15H3if taken in Winter 2017<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. PSYD14H3:  Psychology of Morality<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides an in-depth introduction to the field of moral psychology. In recent years there has been a resurgence of interest in the science of human morality; the goal of this course is to offer an introduction to the research in this field. The course will incorporate perspectives from a variety of disciplines including philosophy, animal behaviour, neuroscience, economics, and almost every area of scientific psychology (social psychology, developmental psychology, evolutionary psychology, and cognitive psychology). By the end of the course students will be well versed in the primary issues and debates involved in the scientific study of morality.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3and [<a href="/course/PSYC12H3">PSYC12H3or <a href="/course/PSYC13H3">PSYC13H3or <a href="/course/PSYC14H3">PSYC14H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/PSYD15H3">PSYD15H3if taken in Fall 2015</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/PSYC08H3">PSYC08H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. PSYD16H3:  Critical Analysis in Social Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>The development of social psychology is examined both as a discipline (its phenomena, theory, and methods) and as a profession. The Natural and Human Science approaches to phenomena are contrasted. Students are taught to observe the lived-world, choose a social phenomenon of interest to them, and then interview people who describe episodes from their lives in which these phenomena occurred. The students interpret these episodes and develop theories to account for their phenomena before searching for scholarly research on the topic.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3and [0.5 credit at the C-level in PSY courses] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY420H</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/PSYC12H3">PSYC12H3or <a href="/course/PSYC71H3">PSYC71H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. PSYD17H3:  Social Neuroscience<p>Course Description</p><p>This course investigates how linking theory and evidence from psychology, neuroscience, and biology can aid in understanding important social behaviors. Students will learn to identify, critique, and apply cutting-edge research findings to current real-world social issues (e.g., prejudice, politics, moral and criminal behavior, stress and health).</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3or <a href="/course/PSYB64H3">PSYB64H3</a>] and [0.5 credit from the PSYC10-series or PSYC50-series courses] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY473H</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/PSYC13H3">PSYC13H3or <a href="/course/PSYC57H3">PSYC57H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology, Mental Health Studies, and Neuroscience - Cognitive Stream. PSYD18H3:  Psychology of Gender<p>Course Description</p><p>This course focuses on theory and research pertaining to gender and gender roles. The social psychological and social-developmental research literature concerning gender differences will be critically examined. Other topics also will be considered, such as gender-role socialization.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3and [1.0 credit at the C-level in PSY courses]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY323H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. PSYD19H3:  The Science of Behaviour Change<p>Course Description</p><p>How can we break bad habits? How can we start healthy habits? This course will explore the science of behaviour change, examining how to go from where you are to where you want to be. Students will learn core knowledge of the field of behaviour change from psychology and behavioural economics. Topics include goal setting and goal pursuit, self-regulation, motivation, dealing with temptations, nudges, and habits. Students will read primary sources and learn how to critically evaluate research and scientific claims. Critically, students will not only learn theory but will be instructed on how to apply what they learn in class to their everyday lives where students work on improving their own habits.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB10H3">PSYB10H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3and [0.5 credit from the PSYC10-series or <a href="/course/PSYC30H3">PSYC30H3or <a href="/course/PSYC50H3">PSYC50H3</a>]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/PSYC19H3">PSYC19H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Third-year students in these programs will be admitted as space permits. PSYD23H3:  Dyadic Processes in Psychological Development <p>Course Description</p><p>Mutual recognition is one of the hallmarks of human consciousness and psychological development. This course explores mutual recognition as a dyadic and regulatory process in development, drawing on diverse theories from developmental science, social psychology, neuroscience, philosophy, literature, psychoanalysis, and gender studies.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYC13H3">PSYC13H3or <a href="/course/PSYC18H3">PSYC18H3or <a href="/course/PSYC23H3">PSYC23H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. PSYD24H3:  Seeing, Hearing, and Moving in Children<p>Course Description</p><p>An in-depth examination of aspects related to perceptual and motor development in infancy and childhood. The topics to be covered will be drawn from basic components of visual and auditory perception, multisensory integration, and motor control, including reaching, posture, and walking. Each week, students will read a set of experimental reports, and will discuss these readings in class. The format of this course is seminar-discussion.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB20H3">PSYB20H3or <a href="/course/PLIC24H3">PLIC24H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3and [0.5 credit at the C-level in PSY courses]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY410H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. PSYD28H3:  The Development of Affective Cognition<p>Course Description</p><p>Humans’ abilities to reason and think about emotion (i.e., affective cognition) is highly sophisticated. Even with limited information, humans can predict whether someone will feel amused, excited, or moved, or whether they will feel embarrassed, disappointed, or furious. How do humans acquire these abilities? This course will delve into the development of affective cognition in infancy and childhood. Topics include infants’ and children’s abilities to infer, predict, and explain emotions, the influence of family and culture in these developmental processes, and atypical development of affective cognition. Through reading classic and contemporary papers, presenting and discussing current topics, and proposing novel ideas in this research domain, students will gain an in-depth understanding of the fundamental aspects of affective cognition over the course of development.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB20H3">PSYB20H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3and [0.5 credit at the C-level in PSY courses]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/PSYC18H3">PSYC18H3or <a href="/course/PSYC28H3">PSYC28H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies. Third-year students in these programs will be admitted as space permits. Students in a Specialist/Specialist Co-op Program in Psychology, Mental Health Studies or Neuroscience may take a maximum of 1.0 PSY/NRO D-level credits. PSYD32H3:  Personality Disorders<p>Course Description</p><p>This course reviews the latest research on the causes, longitudinal development, assessment, and treatment of personality disorders. Students will learn the history of personality disorders and approaches to conceptualizing personality pathology. Topics covered include “schizophrenia-spectrum” personality disorders, biological approaches to psychopathy, and dialectical behaviour therapy for borderline personality disorder.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB30H3">PSYB30H3and <a href="/course/PSYB32H3">PSYB32H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3and [0.5 credit at the C-level in PSY courses]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: PSY430H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Mental Health Studies, as well as Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology. PSYD35H3:  Clinical Psychopharmacology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course reviews the psychopharmacological strategies used for addressing a variety of mental health conditions including anxiety, depression, psychosis, impulsivity, and dementia. It will also address the effects of psychotropic drugs on patients or clients referred to mental health professionals for intellectual, neuropsychological and personality testing. Limitations of pharmacotherapy and its combinations with psychotherapy will be discussed.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYB55H3">PSYB55H3and [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3and <a href="/course/PSYC62H3">PSYC62H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Mental Health Studies, as well as Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology. PSYD51H3:  Current Topics in Perception<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides an intensive examination of selected topics in recent research on perception. Topics may include research in vision, action, touch, hearing and multisensory integration. PSYD52H3:  Neural Network Models of Cognition Laboratory<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides an overview of neural-network models of perception, memory, language, knowledge representation, and higher-order cognition. The course consists of lectures and a lab component. Lectures will cover the theory behind the models and their application to specific empirical domains. Labs will provide hands-on experience running and analyzing simulation models.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3or <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYB70H3">PSYB70H3and [0.5 credit at the C-level in PSY 50-series courses]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: [<a href="/course/PSYB03H3">PSYB03H3or <a href="/course/CSCA08H3">CSCA08H3or <a href="/course/CSCA20H3">CSCA20H3</a>] and [<a href="/course/MATA23H3">MATA23H3and [<a href="/course/MATA29H3">MATA29H3or <a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3</a>]]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Natural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to fourth-year students in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs and Major/Major Co-op programs in Psychology and Mental Health Studies, as well as Specialist/Specialist Co-op programs in Neuroscience Cognitive Stream. PSYD98Y3:  Thesis in Psychology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course offers the opportunity to engage in a year-long research project under the supervision of an interested member of the faculty in Psychology. Extensive data collection/processing and analysis will be required. The project will culminate in a written report in the form of a thesis and a poster presentation. During the course of the year, at appropriate times, students will meet to present their own research proposals, to appraise the proposals of others, and to discuss the results of their investigation. Students must first find a supervisor, which is usually confirmed before the start of the academic term in which the project will be initiated. Students will meet as a group with the coordinator as well as individually with their supervisor. This course is restricted to Majors and Specialists in Psychology and Mental Health Studies with a GPA of 3.3 or higher over the last 5.0 credit equivalents completed. Students planning to pursue graduate studies are especially encouraged to enroll in the course. Students must obtain a <a href="https://utoronto.sharepoint.com/sites/utsc-psychology/SitePages/UG_Research-Courses.aspx">permission formfrom the Department of Psychology website that is to be completed and signed by the intended supervisor and submitted to the Psychology Office. Students seeking supervision off campus will need to arrange co-supervision with a faculty member in Psychology at this campus.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/PSYC02H3">PSYC02H3and [<a href="/course/PSYC08H3">PSYC08H3or <a href="/course/PSYC09H3">PSYC09H3</a>] and <a href="/course/PSYC70H3">PSYC70H3and [enrollment in the Specialist Co-op, Specialist, or Major Program in Psychology or Mental Health Studies] and [GPA of 3.3 or higher over the last 5.0 credit equivalents completed] and permission of the proposed supervisor.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/NROD98Y3">NROD98Y3</a>, (COGD10H3), PSY400Y</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/PSYD98Y3">

RLGC07H3:  Topics in Buddhist Philosophy: Buddhist Ethics<p>Course Description</p><p>Buddhism is a response to what is fundamentally an ethical problem - the perennial problem of the best kind of life for us to lead. Gotama was driven to seek the solution to this problem and the associated ethical issues it raises. This course discusses the aspects of <em>sila</em>, ethics and psychology, <em>nirvana</em>; ethics in Mahayana; Buddhism, utilitarianism, and Aristotle.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/RLGA01H3">RLGA01H3or (HUMB04H3) or (PHLB42H3)<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: NEW214Y, (PHLC40H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/RLGC07H3">

This course teaches students how to think sociologically, using empirical research methods and theories to make sense of society. Students will learn about the causes and consequences of inequalities, the ways in which our social worlds are constructed rather than natural, and the role of institutions in shaping our lives</span><span>.</span><span> </span><span> </span></span><span> </span></p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: <span>(SOC101Y1), (SOCA01H3), (SOCA02H3), (</span><span>SOCA03</span><span>Y</span><span>3)</span><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCA05H3">
SOCA06H3:  Sociology in the World: Careers and Applications <p>Course Description</p><p><span><span>This </span><span>course</span><span> explores</span> <span>real</span><span>-</span><span>world </span><span>use</span><span>s </span><span>of Sociology</span><span>, </span><span>i</span><span>ncluding</span><span> the preparation Sociology </span><span>provides for</span><span> professional schools, and the advantages of Soc</span><span>iology</span> <span>training for</span><span> serving communities, </span><span>govern</span><span>ments</span><span>, and </span><span>the voluntary </span><span>and private </span><span>sector</span><span>s</span><span>. </span><span>This course focuses </span><span>in particular on</span><span> the unique skills Sociolog</span><span>ists hav</span><span>e, including </span><span>data generation and interpretation, </span><span>communication </span><span>and analysis </span><span>techniques, and</span><span> the</span> <span>evaluation of </span><span>social </span><span>processes</span><span> and outcomes</span><span>. </span><span> </span></span><span> </span></p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Major and Specialist students will be given priority access to <a href="/course/SOCA06H3">SOCA06H3</a>.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCA06H3">
SOCB05H3:  Logic of Social Inquiry<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces the logic of sociological research and surveys the major quantitative and qualitative methodologies. Students learn to evaluate the validity of research findings, develop research questions and select appropriate research designs.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)] and enrolment in a Sociology program] or [any 4.0 credits and enrolment in the Minor Critical Migration Studies] or [any 4.0 credits and enrolment in the Major Program in Public Law]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: SOC150H1, (SOC200H5), (SOC200Y5), SOC221H5, (SOCB40H3), (SOCB41H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB05H3">
SOCB22H3:  Sociology of Gender<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines gender as a sociological category that organizes and, at the same time, is organized by, micro and macro forces. By examining how gender intersects with race, ethnicity, class, sexuality, age, and other dimensions, we analyze the constitution and evolution of gendered ideology and practice.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [SOCA5H3 or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)] or [<a href="/course/WSTA01H3">WSTA01H3and <a href="/course/WSTA03H3">WSTA03H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB22H3">
SOCB26H3:  Sociology of Education<p>Course Description</p><p>This course offers a sociological perspective on a familiar experience: attending school. It examines the stated and hidden purposes of schooling; explores how learning in schools is organized; evaluates the drop-out problem; the determinants of educational success and failure; and, it looks at connections between school and work.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB26H3">
SOCB28H3:  Sociological Evidence for Everyday Life<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will engage evidence-based sociological findings that are often related to how individuals make decisions in everyday life. Special attention will be paid to how empirical findings in sociology are used as evidence in different social contexts and decision making processes. The course should enable students to make direct connections between the insights of sociology and their own lives.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB28H3">
SOCB30H3:  Political Sociology<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of power in its social context. Specific attention is devoted to how and under what conditions power is exercised, reproduced and transformed, as well as the social relations of domination, oppression, resistance and solidarity. Selected topics may include: nations, states, parties, institutions, citizenship, and social movements.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: SOC260H1, SOC335H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB30H3">
SOCB35H3:  Numeracy and Society<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces the basic concepts and assumptions of quantitative reasoning, with a focus on using modern data science techniques and real-world data to answer key questions in sociology. It examines how numbers, counting, and statistics produce expertise, authority, and the social categories through which we define social reality. This course avoids advanced mathematical concepts and proofs.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-corequisite"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-corequisite">Corequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or (SOCA03Y3) or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)]] or [any 4.0 credits and enrolment in the Major Program in Public Law] or enrolment in the Certificate in Computational Social Science.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB35H3">
SOCB37H3:  Economy, Culture, and Society<p>Course Description</p><p>This course offers a sociological account of economic phenomena. The central focus is to examine how economic activities are shaped, facilitated, or even impeded by cultural values and social relations, and show that economic life cannot be fully understood outside of its social context. The course will focus on economic activities of production, consumption, and exchange in a wide range of settings including labor and financial markets, corporations, household and intimate economies, informal and illegal economies, and markets of human goods.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or (SOCA03Y3) or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)]] or [any 4.0 credits and enrolment in the Minor in Culture, Creativity, and Cities]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB37H3">
SOCB40H3:  Thinking Like a Sociologist <p>Course Description</p><p>This course builds on <a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3through a deep engagement with 4-5 significant new publications in Sociology, typically books by department faculty and visiting scholars. By developing reading and writing skills through a variety of assignments, and participating in classroom visits with the researchers who produced the publications, students will learn to "think like a sociologist." Possible topics covered include culture, gender, health, immigration/race/ethnicity, political sociology, social networks, theory, sociology of crime and law, and work/stratification/markets.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or (SOCA03Y3) or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)]]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: SOC230H5</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB40H3">
SOCB42H3:  Theory I: Discovering the Social <p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines a group of theorists whose work provided key intellectual resources for articulating the basic concepts and tasks of sociology. Central topics include: the consequences of the division of labour, sources and dynamics of class conflict in commercial societies, the social effects of industrial production, the causes and directions of social progress, the foundations of feminism, linkages between belief systems and social structures, and the promises and pathologies of democratic societies.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)] and enrolment in a Sociology program<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: SOC201H1, (SOC203Y1), SOC231H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB42H3">
SOCB43H3:  Theory II: Big Ideas in Sociology<p>Course Description</p><p>This course studies a group of writers who in the early 20th century were pivotal in theoretically grounding sociology as a scientific discipline. Central topics include: the types and sources of social authority; the genesis and ethos of capitalism; the moral consequences of the division of labour; the nature of social facts; the origins of collective moral values; the relationship between social theory and social reform; the nature of social problems and the personal experience of being perceived as a social problem; the formal features of association; the social function of conflict; the social and personal consequences of urbanization.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)] and <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3and enrolment in a Sociology program<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (SOC203Y1)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB43H3">
SOCB44H3:  Sociology of Cities and Urban Life<p>Course Description</p><p>A theoretical and empirical examination of the processes of urbanization and suburbanization. Considers classic and contemporary approaches to the ecology and social organization of the pre-industrial, industrial, corporate and postmodern cities.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)] or [any 4.0 credits and enrolment in the Minor in Culture, Creativity, and Cities, or the Major/Major Co-op in City Studies]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (SOC205Y1), SOC205H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB44H3">
SOCB47H3:  Social Inequality<p>Course Description</p><p>A sociological examination of the ways in which individuals and groups have been differentiated and ranked historically and cross-culturally. Systems of differentiation and devaluation examined may include gender, race, ethnicity, class, sexual orientation, citizenship/legal status, and ability/disability.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)] or [any 4.0 credits and enrolment in the Major/Major Co-op in Public Policy]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: SOC301Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB47H3">
SOCB49H3:  Sociology of Family<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores the family as a social institution, which shapes and at the same time is shaped by, the society in North America. Specific attention will be paid to family patterns in relation to class, gender, and racial/ethnic stratifications. Selected focuses include: socialization; courtship; heterosexual, gay and lesbian relations; gender division of labour; immigrant families; childbearing and childrearing; divorce; domestic violence; elderly care.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)] or [<a href="/course/WSTA01H3">WSTA01H3and <a href="/course/WSTA03H3">WSTA03H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: SOC214Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB49H3">
SOCB50H3:  Deviance and Normality I<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores how deviance and normality is constructed and contested in everyday life. The course revolves around the themes of sexuality, gender, poverty, race and intoxication. Particular attention will be paid to the role of official knowledge in policing social norms.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)] or [any 4.0 credits and enrolment in the Major Program in Public Law]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: SOC212Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB50H3">
SOCB53H3:  Race and Ethnicity<p>Course Description</p><p>The course draws on a geographically varied set of case studies to consider both the historical development and contemporary state of the sociological field of race, racialization and ethnic relations.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [<a href="/course/SOCA01H3">SOCA01H3and <a href="/course/SOCA02H3">SOCA02H3</a>] or (SOCA03Y3)] or [any 4.0 credits and enrolment in the Minor in Critical Migration Studies]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: SOC210Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB53H3">
SOCB54H3:  Sociology of Work<p>Course Description</p><p>Economic activity drives human society. This course explores the nature of work, how it is changing, and the impact of changes on the transition from youth to adult life. It also examines racism in the workplace, female labour force participation, and why we call some jobs 'professions', but not others.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: SOC207H1, (SOC207Y), SOC227H5<span><span>, <a href="/course/GGRD16H3">GGRD16H3</a></span></span><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB54H3">
SOCB58H3:  Sociology of Culture<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to various ways that sociologists think about and study culture. Topics will include the cultural aspects of a wide range of social phenomena - including inequality, gender, economics, religion, and organizations. We will also discuss sociological approaches to studying the production, content, and audiences of the arts and media.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(<a href="/course/SOCA01H3">SOCA01H3)and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)] or [any 4.0 credits and enrolment in the Minor in Culture, Creativity, and Cities] or [<a href="/course/IDSA01H3">IDSA01H3and enrolment in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op/Major/Minor in International Development Studies (Arts)]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: SOC220H5, SOC280H1, (SOCC18H3),<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: History, Philosophy and Cultural Studies</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB58H3">
SOCB59H3:  Sociology of Law<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the character, authority, and processes of law in contemporary liberal democracies.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)] or [any 4.0 credits and enrolment in the Major Program in Public Law]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB59H3">
SOCB60H3:  Issues in Critical Migration Studies<p>Course Description</p><p>What are the causes and consequences of migration in today's world? This course will explore this question in two parts. First, we will examine how although people decide to migrate, they make these decisions under circumstances which are not of their own making. Then, we will focus specifically on the experiences of racialized and immigrant groups in Canada, with a particular focus on the repercussions of Black enslavement and ongoing settler-colonialism. As we explore these questions, we will also critically interrogate the primary response of the Canadian government to questions around racial and class inequality: multiculturalism. What is multiculturalism? Is it enough? Does it make matters worse? Students will come away from this course having critically thought about what types of social change would bring about a freer and more humane society.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [Completion of 1.0 credit from the following courses: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)], <a href="/course/ANTA02H3">ANTA02H3</a>, <a href="/course/GGRA02H3">GGRA02H3</a>, <a href="/course/GASA01H3">GASA01H3</a>/<a href="/course/HISA06H3">HISA06H3</a>, <a href="/course/GASA02H3">GASA02H3</a>, <a href="/course/HISA04H3">HISA04H3</a>, or <a href="/course/HISA05H3">HISA05H3</a>] or [any 4.0 credits and enrolment in the Minor in Critical Migration Studies]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Minor in Critical Migration Studies. Additional students will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB60H3">
SOCB70H3:  Social Change<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides an introductory overview of the nature and causes of social change in contemporary societies. Topics covered include: changes in political ideology, cultural values, ethnic and sexual identities, religious affiliation, family formation, health, crime, social structure, and economic inequality.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or (SOCA03Y3) or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)]] or [<a href="/course/IDSA01H3">IDSA01H3and enrolment in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op/Major/Minor in International Development Studies (Arts)]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCB70H3">

SOCC04H3:  Social Movements<p>Course Description</p><p>The development of an approach to social movements which includes the following: the origin of social movements, mobilization processes, the career of the movement and its routinization. The course readings will be closely related to the lectures, and a major concern will be to link the theoretical discussion with the concrete readings of movements.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3or <a href="/course/SOCB35H3">SOCB35H3</a>] and [0.5 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/SOCB22H3">SOCB22H3or <a href="/course/SOCB49H3">SOCB49H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC04H3">
SOCC09H3:  Sociology of Gender and Work<p>Course Description</p><p>Explores the interaction of gender and work, both paid and unpaid work. Critically assesses some cases for central theoretical debates and recent research. Considers gender differences in occupational and income attainment, housework, the relation of work and family, gender and class solidarity, and the construction of gender identity through occupational roles.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [[<a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3or <a href="/course/SOCB35H3">SOCB35H3</a>] and [0.5 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]] or [8.0 credits, including <a href="/course/WSTB05H3">WSTB05H3and enrolment in the Major program in Women's and Gender Studies]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: SOC362H5</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/SOCB22H3">SOCB22H3or <a href="/course/SOCB49H3">SOCB49H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC09H3">
SOCC11H3:  Policing and Security<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the character of policing and security programs in advanced liberal democracies. Attention will be paid to the nature and enforcement of modern law by both state and private agents of order, as well as the dynamics of the institutions of the criminal justice system. This course has been designated an Applied Writing Skills Course.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <span><span>[[</span></span><span><span><a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3and [1.0 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]</span></span><span><span>]</span></span> <span><span>or</span></span> <span><span>[any </span><span>8</span><span>.0 credits and enrolment in the Major Program in Public Law]</span></span><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (SOC306Y1), SOC326H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC11H3">
SOCC15H3:  Work, Employment and Society<p>Course Description</p><p>An upper level course that examines a number of critical issues and important themes in the sociological study of work. Topics covered will include: the changing nature and organization of work, precarious employment, different forms of worker organizing and mobilization, the professions, the transition from school to work.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3or <a href="/course/SOCB35H3">SOCB35H3</a>] and [0.5 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/SOCB54H3">SOCB54H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC15H3">
SOCC23H3:  Research in Action: Gathering and Analyzing Qualitative Data<p>Course Description</p><p>How do people navigate their everyday lives? Why do they do what they do? And how, as sociologists, can we draw meaningful conclusions about these processes and the larger, social world we live in? Qualitative research methods adhere to the interpretative paradigm. Sociologists use them to gain a richer understanding of the relationship between the minutiae of everyday life and larger societal patterns. This course will introduce students to the qualitative methods that social scientists rely on, while also providing them with hands-on experience carrying out their own research. <br>This course has been designated an Applied Writing Skills Course.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 10.0 credits including [[<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)] and <a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3</a>] and a cumulative GPA of at least 2.3<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (SOCD23H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC23H3">
SOCC24H3:  Changing Family Life in Canada<p>Course Description</p><p><span><span>This course offers a theoretical and empirical examination of different forms of family and the major changes in the structure and culture of family life in Canada. In the first half of the semester, we will examine and unsettle the notion that there is (or ever was) a ‘typical’ Canadian family. We investigate major shifts in marriage, cohabitation, and divorce, and consider the implications of these shifts for various social actors. We also study the incompatibility of employment and family responsibilities, and how various responses to this incompatibility produce gender and global inequalities. In the second half of the semester, we investigate the diversity of family forms, as well as the problem of violence within the family</span><span>. </span></span><span><span>This course has been </span><span>designated</span><span> an Applied Writing Skills Course.</span></span><span> </span></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3and [1.0 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]] or [8.0 credits, including <a href="/course/WSTB05H3">WSTB05H3and enrolment in the Major program in Women's and Gender Studies]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/SOCB22H3">SOCB22H3or <a href="/course/SOCB49H3">SOCB49H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC24H3">
SOCC25H3:  Ethnicity, Race and Migration<p>Course Description</p><p>Why do people migrate and how do they decide where to go? How does a society determine which border crossers are ‘illegal’ and which are ‘legal’? Why are some people deemed ‘refugees’ while others are not? What consequences do labels like ‘deportee’, ‘immigrant,’ ‘refugee,’ or ‘trafficking victim’ have on the people who get assigned them? This course will examine these and other similar questions. We will explore how the politics of race, class, gender, sexuality and citizenship shape the ways that states make sense of and regulate different groups of migrants as well as how these regulatory processes affect im/migrants’ life opportunities.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [[<a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3or <a href="/course/SOCB35H3">SOCB35H3</a>] and [0.5 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]] or [<a href="/course/SOCB60H3">SOCB60H3and an additional 8.0 credits and enrolment in the Minor program in Critical Migration Studies] or [<a href="/course/IDSB07H3">IDSB07H3and an additional 8.0 credits, and enrolment in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op Program/Major/Minor Program in International Development Studies (Arts)]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/SOCB22H3">SOCB22H3or <a href="/course/SOCB49H3">SOCB49H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC25H3">
SOCC26H3:  Sociology of Urban Cultural Policies<p>Course Description</p><p>A popular civic strategy in transforming post-industrial cities has been the deployment of culture and the arts as tools for urban regeneration. In this course, we analyze culture-led development both as political economy and as policy discourse. Topics include the creative city; spectacular consumption spaces; the re-use of historic buildings; cultural clustering and gentrification; eventful cities; and urban 'scenes'.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [[<a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3or <a href="/course/SOCB35H3">SOCB35H3</a>] and [0.5 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]] or [<a href="/course/SOCB58H3">SOCB58H3and enrolment in the Minor in Culture, Creativity, and Cities] or [ <a href="/course/CITA01H3">CITA01H3</a>/(CITB02H3) and enrolment in the Major/Major Co-op in City Studies]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/SOCB44H3">SOCB44H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC26H3">
SOCC27H3:  Sociology of Suburbs and Suburbanization<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the political economy of suburban development, the myth and reality of suburbanism as a way of life, the working class suburb, the increasing diversity of suburban communities, suburbia and social exclusion, and the growth of contemporary suburban forms such as gated communities and lifestyle shopping malls.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [[<a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3or <a href="/course/SOCB35H3">SOCB35H3</a>] and [0.5 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]] or [<a href="/course/SOCB58H3">SOCB58H3and enrolment in the Minor in Culture, Creativity, and Cities] or [<a href="/course/CITA01H3">CITA01H3</a>/(CITB02H3) and enrolment in the Major/Major Co-op in City Studies]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/SOCB22H3">SOCB22H3or <a href="/course/SOCB49H3">SOCB49H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC27H3">
SOCC29H3:  Family and Gender in the Middle East<p>Course Description</p><p>In this course, students read and evaluate recent research related to the sociology of families and gender in the modern Middle East. The course explores the diversity of family forms and processes across time and space in this region, where kinship structures have in the past been characterized as static and uniformly patriarchal. Topics covered include marriage, the life course, family nucleation, the work-family nexus, divorce, family violence, and masculinities.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [[<a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3or <a href="/course/SOCB35H3">SOCB35H3</a>] and [0.5 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]] or [8.0 credits, including <a href="/course/WSTB05H3">WSTB05H3</a>, and enrolment in the Major Program in Women's and Gender Studies] or [8.0 credits, including <a href="/course/ASFB01H3">ASFB01H3</a>, and enrolment in the Minor Program in African Studies] or [<a href="/course/IDSA01H3">IDSA01H3and additional 8.0 credits, and enrolment in the Specialist/Specialist Co-op/Major/Minor Program in International Development Studies (Arts)]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC29H3">
SOCC30H3:  Criminal Behaviour<p>Course Description</p><p>The young figure prominently in people's views about, and fears of, crime. This course examines definitions of crime, how crime problems are constructed and measured. It looks at schools and the street as sites of criminal behaviour, and considers how we often react to crime in the form of moral panics.<br>This course has been designated an Applied Writing Skills Course.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <span><span>[</span><span>[</span></span><span><span><a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3and [1.0 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]</span></span><span><span>]</span></span> <span><span>or</span></span> <span><span>[any </span><span>8</span><span>.0 credits and enrolment in the Major Program in Public Law]</span></span><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC30H3">
SOCC31H3:  Research in Action: Quantitative Data and Statistical Analysis<p>Course Description</p><p>This course invites students to explore their sociological interests through designing and executing their own research projects using real-world quantitative data. Participants will gain a comprehensive understanding of one or more types of quantitative data, such as social survey or census data, including how the data are collected, their value for sociological research, and how to effectively analyze and interpret them. Throughout the course, students will engage in every step of the research process, from selecting research topics and formulating questions to reviewing relevant sociological literature, creating research designs, conducting statistical analyses, and presenting findings in both oral and written formats. The course equips students with practical research skills in quantitative data analysis and interpretation, preparing them to transform raw data into meaningful sociological insights. This course has been designated an Applied Writing Skills Course.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [10.0 credits, including [<a href="/course/SOCA05H3">SOCA05H3or [(SOCA01H3) and (SOCA02H3)] or (SOCA03Y3)] and <a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3and [<a href="/course/SOCB35H3">SOCB35H3or (SOCB06H3)]] and a cumulative GPA of at least 2.3<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC31H3">
SOCC32H3:  Human Rights and Counterterrorism<p>Course Description</p><p>After 9/11, terrorism was labeled a global threat, fueling the war on terror and the adoption of extensive counterterrorism actions. These measures, however, often compromised human rights in the pursuit of national security goals. This course grapples with questions pertaining to terrorism, counterterrorism, and human rights in the age of security.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3and 0.5 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>] or [IDSA01 and an additional 8.0 credits, and enrolment in the Specialist/Major Program in IDS] or [POLB80 and an additional 8.0 credits, and enrolment in the Specialist/Major Program in Political Science]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC32H3">

SOCC37H3:  Environment and Society<p>Course Description</p><p>This course links studies in the classical sociology of resources and territory (as in the works of Harold Innis, S.D. Clark, and the Chicago School), with modern topics in ecology and environmentalism.
