SOCC46H3:  Special Topics in Sociology of Law<p>Course Description</p><p>The course covers various approaches to the study of law in society. Topics covered may include the interaction between law, legal, non-legal institutions and social factors, the social development of legal institutions, forms of social control, legal regulation, the interaction between legal cultures, the social construction of legal issues, legal profession, and the relation between law and social change.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <span><span>[</span></span><span><span>[<a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3or <a href="/course/SOCB35H3">SOCB35H3</a>] and [0.5 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]</span></span><span><span>]</span></span> <span><span>or</span></span> <span><span>[any </span><span>8</span><span>.0 credits and enrolment in the Major Program in Public Law]</span></span><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC46H3">
SOCC47H3:  Creative Industries<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to organizational and economic sociology through the lens of creative industries. Students will be introduced to different theoretical paradigms in the study of organizations, industries, and fields. The course is divided into four major modules on creative industries: inequality and occupational careers; organizational structure and decision making under conditions of uncertainty; market and field-level effects; and distribution and promotion. <br>This course has been designated an Applied Writing Skills Course.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3and [1.0 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]] or [8.0 credits including <a href="/course/SOCB58H3">SOCB58H3and enrolment in the Minor in Culture, Creativity, and Cities]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students in the Specialist and Major programs in Sociology and the Minor in Culture, Creativity, and Cities.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC47H3">
SOCC49H3:  Indigenous Health<p>Course Description</p><p>This course will examine the health and well-being of Indigenous peoples, given historic and contemporary issues. A critical examination of the social determinants of health, including the cultural, socioeconomic and political landscape, as well as the legacy of colonialism, will be emphasized. An overview of methodologies and ethical issues working with Indigenous communities in health research and developing programs and policies will be provided. The focus will be on the Canadian context, but students will be exposed to the issues of Indigenous peoples worldwide.</p> <p>Same as <a href="/course/HLTC49H3">HLTC49H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/HLTB41H3">HLTB41H3or [[<a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3or <a href="/course/SOCB35H3">SOCB35H3</a>] and [0.5 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/HLTC49H3">HLTC49H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC49H3">
SOCC50H3:  Sociology of Religion<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores the social meaning and influence of religion in social life. As a set of beliefs, symbols and motivations, as well as a structural system, religion is multifaceted and organizes many aspects of our daily life. This course surveys key theoretical paradigms on the meaning of religion and the social implications of religious transformations across time. It takes up basic questions about how religion is shaped by various political, social, and economic forces.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3and [1.0 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist, Major and Minor programs in Sociology. Additional students will be admitted as space permits.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC50H3">
SOCC51H3:  Special Topics in Health and Society<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of a current topic relevant to the study of health and society. SOCC52H3:  Immigration, Citizenship and Settler Colonialism<p>Course Description</p><p>The course examines the relationship between the displacement and dispossession of Indigenous peoples and immigration in settler-colonial states. The focus is on Canada as <span>a </span><span>traditional country of immigration. Topics considered include historical and contemporary immigration and settlement processes, precarious forms of citizenship and </span><span>noncitizenship</span><span>, racism and racial exclusion, and the politics of treaty citizenship. Discussion puts the Canadian case in comparative perspective. </span><span> </span></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [[<a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3or <a href="/course/SOCB35H3">SOCB35H3</a>] and [0.5 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]] or [<a href="/course/SOCB60H3">SOCB60H3and an additional 8.0 credits and enrolment in the Minor Program in Critical Migration Studies]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (SOCB52H3) and SOC210Y<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC52H3">
SOCC54H3:  Special Topics in Sociology of Culture<p>Course Description</p><p>Sociological analysis of the role of culture in societies is offered under this course. SOCC58H3:  Global Transformations: Politics, Economy and Society<p>Course Description</p><p>A sociological examination of contemporary global transformations including changing social, economic, and political conditions. SOCC61H3:  The Sociology of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission<p>Course Description</p><p>The Truth and Reconciliation Commission of Canada is an historic process that now directs a core area of Canadian politics and governance. SOCC70H3:  Models of the Social World<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines how quantitative models can be used to understand the social world with a focus on social inequality and social change. Students will learn the fundamentals of modern computational techniques and data analysis, including how to effectively communicate findings using narratives and visualizations. Topics covered include data wrangling, graphic design, regression analysis, interactive modelling, and categorical data analysis. Methods will be taught using real-world examples in sociology with an emphasis on understanding key concepts rather than mathematical formulas.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/SOCB35H3">SOCB35H3or [completion of 8.0 credits, including component 1 of the course requirements for the Certificate in Computational Social Science]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCC70H3">

SOCD02H3:  Global Field School: Indigenous Costa Rica<p>Course Description</p><p>The intensive international field school course is an experiential and land-based learning trip to Indigenous territories in Costa Rica, in order to learn about settler colonialism, Indigenous communities, and UNDRIP (the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples). Students will learn with Indigenous Costa Rican university students and community partners in order to draw links between policy frameworks (UNDRIP), ideologies (colonialism) and the impacts on Indigenous communities (e.g. education, health, food security, language retention, land rights). The course involves 14-16 days of in-country travel. This course has been designated as a Research Skills course. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [10.0 credits, including <a href="/course/SOCC61H3">SOCC61H3</a>] and [1.0 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, or <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Major or Specialist Programs in Sociology. Additional students will be admitted as space permits. This course requires students to register and fill out an application form. To request a <a href="/course/SOCD02H3">SOCD02H3course application form, please contact <a href="mailto:sociologyadvisor.utsc@utoronto.ca">sociologyadvisor.utsc@utoronto.ca.This form is due one week after students enroll in the course. <br> <strong>Enrolment Control: A</strong><br><strong>Restricted to students in the sociology programs.</strong><br><strong>Step 1:</strong>&nbsp;Request the course on ACORN. SOCD08H3:  Scarborough Place-Making: Indigenous Sovereignty and Settler Landholding <p>Course Description</p><p><span><span>This course explores how the land where UTSC stands has been transformed, with a focus on settler colonialism and Indigenous self-determination. By looking at the history and ongoing impacts of settler practices and Indigenous knowledge systems, students will learn about the conflicts and connections between these ways of understanding the land. The course includes guided walks across different sites on campus as part of a hands-on, land-based learning approach, helping students see and experience how these issues play out in the environment around them. This course has been </span><span>designated</span><span> a Research Skills Course.</span></span></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 10.0 credits including <a href="/course/SOCC61H3">SOCC61H3and at least 0.5 credit of from the following: [<a href="/course/ACMD98H3">ACMD98H3</a>, <a href="/course/GGRB18H3">GGRB18H3</a>, <a href="/course/POLC52H3">POLC52H3</a>, <a href="/course/POLC56H3">POLC56H3</a>, <a href="/course/POLD54H3">POLD54H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCD18H3">SOCD18H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist, Major and Minor programs in Sociology, including the Critical Migration Studies Minor. SOCD11H3:  Program and Policy Evaluation<p>Course Description</p><p>This course provides an introduction to the field of program and policy evaluation. Evaluation plays an important role in evidence based decision making in all aspects of society. Students will gain insight into the theoretical, methodological, practical, and ethical aspects of evaluation across different settings. The relative strengths and weaknesses of various designs used in applied social research to examine programs and policies will be covered.</p> <br> <p>Same as <a href="/course/HLTD11H3">HLTD11H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [[<a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>] and [0.5 credit from <a href="/course/HLTC42H3">HLTC42H3</a>, <a href="/course/HLTC43H3">HLTC43H3</a>, <a href="/course/HLTC44H3">HLTC44H3</a>] and [an additional 1.0 credit at the C-Level from courses from the Major/Major Co-op in Health Policy]] or [10.0 credits and [<a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3and <a href="/course/SOCB35H3">SOCB35H3</a>] and [1.0 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, or <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>]]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/HLTD11H3">HLTD11H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCD11H3">
SOCD12H3:  Sociology of Art<p>Course Description</p><p>An examination of sociological approaches to the study of visual art. SOCD13H3:  Sociology of Finance<p>Course Description</p><p>This is an advanced course on the sub-filed of economic sociology that focuses on money and finance. This course examines how cultural values and social relations shape money and finance in a variety of substantive settings, including the historical emergence of money as currency, the expansion of the financial system since the 1980s, financial markets, growing household involvement in the stock and credit market, and implications for social life (e.g., how credit scores shape dating).</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 10.0 credits and <a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3and [1.0 credit from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB30H3">SOCB30H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB42H3">SOCB42H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB43H3">SOCB43H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCB50H3">SOCB50H3</a>, or (SOCB51H3)]</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/SOCB35H3">SOCB35H3and <a href="/course/SOCB37H3">SOCB37H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Priority will be given to students enrolled in the Specialist, Major, and Minor programs in Sociology. SOCD18H3:  The History and Evolution of Reconciliation: The Indian Residential School Settlement<p>Course Description</p><p>This course examines the largest class action settlement in Canadian history: the Indian Residential School Settlement Agreement enacted in Canada in 2006. This analysis is framed within a 50 year history of reconciliation in Canada. Areas of study include the recent history of residential schools, the Royal Commission on Aboriginal Peoples report and the government response, and the establishment of the Aboriginal Healing Foundation.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 10.0 credits including <a href="/course/SOCB05H3">SOCB05H3and [0.5 from the following: <a href="/course/SOCB47H3">SOCB47H3</a>, <a href="/course/SOCC61H3">SOCC61H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/SOCD18H3">
SOCD20H3:  Advanced Seminar: Social Change and Gender Relations in Chinese Societies<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar examines the transformation and perpetuation of gender relations in contemporary Chinese societies. STAB22H3:  Statistics I<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is a basic introduction to statistical reasoning and methodology, with a minimal amount of mathematics and calculation. The course covers descriptive statistics, populations, sampling, confidence intervals, tests of significance, correlation, regression and experimental design. A computer package is used for calculations.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (ANTC35H3), <a href="/course/MGEB11H3">MGEB11H3</a>/(ECMB11H3), (POLB11H3), <a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3</a>, (SOCB06H3), <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a>, <a href="/course/STAB52H3">STAB52H3</a>, <a href="/course/STAB57H3">STAB57H3</a>, STA220H, (STA250H)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAB22H3">
STAB23H3:  Introduction to Statistics for the Social Sciences<p>Course Description</p><p>This course covers the basic concepts of statistics and the statistical methods most commonly used in the social sciences. The first half of the course introduces descriptive statistics, contingency tables, normal probability distribution, and sampling distributions. The second half of the course introduces inferential statistical methods. These topics include significance test for a mean (t-test), significance test for a proportion, comparing two groups (e.g., comparing two proportions, comparing two means), associations between categorical variables (e.g., Chi-square test of independence), and simple linear regression.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (ANTC35H3), <a href="/course/MGEB11H3">MGEB11H3</a>/(ECMB11H3), (POLB11H3), <a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3</a>, (SOCB06H3), <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a>, <a href="/course/STAB52H3">STAB52H3</a>, <a href="/course/STAB57H3">STAB57H3</a>, STA220H, STA250H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAB23H3">
STAB27H3:  Statistics II<p>Course Description</p><p>This course follows <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3</a>, and gives an introduction to regression and analysis of variance techniques as they are used in practice. The emphasis is on the use of software to perform the calculations and the interpretation of output from the software. The course reviews statistical inference, then treats simple and multiple regression and the analysis of some standard experimental designs.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAB22H3">STAB22H3or <a href="/course/STAB23H3">STAB23H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3</a>/(ECMB12H3), <a href="/course/STAB57H3">STAB57H3</a>, STA221H, (STA250H)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAB27H3">
STAB40H3:  Fundamentals of Investment and Credit<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is concerned with the concept of financial interest. Topics covered include: interest, discount and present values, as applied to determine prices and values of annuities, mortgages, bonds, equities, loan repayment schedules and consumer finance payments in general, yield rates on investments given the costs on investments.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <div><a href="/course/MATA30H3">MATA30H3or <a href="/course/MATA31H3">MATA31H3or <a href="/course/MATA34H3">MATA34H3</a></div><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (ACTB40H3), ACT240H1, <a href="/course/MGFB10H3">MGFB10H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning<p>Note</p>Note: Students enrolled in or planning to enrol in any of the B.B.A. programs are strongly urged not to take <a href="/course/STAB40H3">STAB40H3because <a href="/course/STAB40H3">STAB40H3is an exclusion for <a href="/course/MGFB10H3">MGFB10H3</a>/(MGTB09H3)/(MGTC03H3), a required course in the B.B.A. degree. STAB41H3:  Financial Derivatives<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of the most important types of financial derivatives, including forwards, futures, swaps and options (European, American, exotic, etc). The course illustrates their properties and applications through examples, and introduces the theory of derivatives pricing with the use of the no-arbitrage principle and binomial tree models.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [(ACTB40H3)/<a href="/course/STAB40H3">STAB40H3</a>] or <a href="/course/MGFB10H3">MGFB10H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/MGFC30H3">MGFC30H3</a>/(MGTC71H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAB41H3">
STAB52H3:  An Introduction to Probability<p>Course Description</p><p>A mathematical treatment of probability. The topics covered include: the probability model, density and distribution functions, computer generation of random variables, conditional probability, expectation, sampling distributions, weak law of large numbers, central limit theorem, Monte Carlo methods, Markov chains, Poisson processes, simulation, applications. A computer package will be used.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MATA22H3">MATA22H3and <a href="/course/MATA37H3">MATA37H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/STAB53H3">STAB53H3</a>, <a href="/course/PSYB07H3">PSYB07H3</a>, STA107H, STA237H1, STA247H1, STA257H, STA246H5, STA256H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAB52H3">

STAB57H3:  An Introduction to Statistics<p>Course Description</p><p>A mathematical treatment of the theory of statistics. The topics covered include: the statistical model, data collection, descriptive statistics, estimation, confidence intervals and P-values, likelihood inference methods, distribution-free methods, bootstrapping, Bayesian methods, relationship among variables, contingency tables, regression, ANOVA, logistic regression, applications. STAC32H3:  Applications of Statistical Methods<p>Course Description</p><p>A case-study based course, aimed at developing students’ applied statistical skills beyond the basic techniques. Students will be required to write statistical reports. Statistical software, such as SAS and R, will be taught and used for all statistical analyses.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAB27H3">STAB27H3or <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3or <a href="/course/PSYC08H3">PSYC08H3or STA221H1<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/STAC33H3">STAC33H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAC32H3">
STAC33H3:  Introduction to Applied Statistics<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to statistical software, such as R and SAS, and its use in analyzing data. Emphasis will be placed on communication and explanation of findings. Students will be required to write a statistical report.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAB57H3">STAB57H3or <a href="/course/STA248H3">STA248H3or <a href="/course/STA261H3">STA261H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/STAC32H3">STAC32H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAC33H3">
STAC50H3:  Data Collection<p>Course Description</p><p>The principles of proper collection of data for statistical analysis, and techniques to adjust statistical analyses when these principles cannot be implemented. Topics include: relationships among variables, causal relationships, confounding, random sampling, experimental designs, observational studies, experiments, causal inference, meta-analysis. Statistical analyses using SAS or R.</p> <p>Students enrolled in the Minor program in Applied Statistics should take <a href="/course/STAC53H3">STAC53H3instead.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAB57H3">STAB57H3or STA261H1. Students enrolled in the Minor program in Applied Statistics should take <a href="/course/STAC53H3">STAC53H3</a>.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: STA304H, <a href="/course/STAC53H3">STAC53H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAC50H3">
STAC51H3:  Categorical Data Analysis<p>Course Description</p><p>Statistical models for categorical data. Contingency tables, generalized linear models, logistic regression, multinomial responses, logit models for nominal responses, log-linear models for two-way tables, three-way tables and higher dimensions, models for matched pairs, repeated categorical response data, correlated and clustered responses. Statistical analyses using SAS or R.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAC67H3">STAC67H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: STA303H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAC51H3">
STAC53H3:  Applied Data Collection<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces the principles, objectives and methodologies of data collection. The course focuses on understanding the rationale for the various approaches to collecting data and choosing appropriate statistical techniques for data analysis. Topics covered include elements of sampling problems, simple random sampling, stratified sampling, ratio, regression, and difference estimation, systematic sampling, cluster sampling, elements of designed experiments, completely randomized design, randomized block design, and factorial experiments. The R statistical software package is used to illustrate statistical examples in the course. Emphasis is placed on the effective communication of statistical results.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAB27H3">STAB27H3or <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3or <a href="/course/PSYC08H3">PSYC08H3or STA221H1<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/STAC50H3">STAC50H3</a>, STA304H1, STA304H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning<p>Note</p>Note: Students enrolled in the Specialist or Major programs in Statistics should take <a href="/course/STAC50H3">STAC50H3</a>.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAC53H3">
STAC58H3:  Statistical Inference<p>Course Description</p><p>Principles of statistical reasoning and theories of statistical analysis. Topics include: statistical models, likelihood theory, repeated sampling theories of inference, prior elicitation, Bayesian theories of inference, decision theory, asymptotic theory, model checking, and checking for prior-data conflict. Advantages and disadvantages of the different theories.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAB57H3">STAB57H3and <a href="/course/STAC62H3">STAC62H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: STA352Y, STA422H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAC58H3">
STAC62H3:  Probability and Stochastic Processes I <p>Course Description</p><p>This course continues the development of probability theory begun in <a href="/course/STAB52H3">STAB52H3</a>. Topics covered include finite dimensional distributions and the existence theorem, discrete time Markov chains, discrete time martingales, the multivariate normal distribution, Gaussian processes and Brownian motion.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/MATB41H3">MATB41H3and <a href="/course/STAB52H3">STAB52H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: STA347H1<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAC62H3">
STAC63H3:  Probability and Stochastic Processes II<p>Course Description</p><p>This course continues the development of probability theory begun in <a href="/course/STAC62H3">STAC62H3</a>. Probability models covered include branching processes, birth and death processes, renewal processes, Poisson processes, queuing theory, random walks and Brownian motion.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAC62H3">STAC62H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: STA447H1, STA348H5<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAC63H3">
STAC67H3:  Regression Analysis<p>Course Description</p><p>A fundamental statistical technique widely used in various disciples. The topics include simple and multiple linear regression analysis, geometric representation of regression, inference on regression parameters, model assumptions and diagnostics, model selection, remedial measures including weighted least squares, instruction in the use of statistical software.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAB57H3">STAB57H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: STA302H; [Students who want to complete both <a href="/course/STAC67H3">STAC67H3and <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3</a>, and receive credit for both courses, must successfully complete <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3prior to enrolling in <a href="/course/STAC67H3">STAC67H3</a>; for students who complete <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3after successfully completing <a href="/course/STAC67H3">STAC67H3</a>, <a href="/course/MGEB12H3">MGEB12H3will be marked as Extra (EXT)]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAC67H3">
STAC70H3:  Statistics and Finance I<p>Course Description</p><p>A mathematical treatment of option pricing. Building on Brownian motion, the course introduces stochastic integrals and Itô calculus, which are used to develop the Black-Scholes framework for option pricing. The theory is extended to pricing general derivatives and is illustrated through applications to risk management.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [<a href="/course/STAB41H3">STAB41H3or <a href="/course/MGFC30H3">MGFC30H3</a>/(MGTC71H3)] and <a href="/course/STAC62H3">STAC62H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/MATC46H3">MATC46H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: APM466H, ACT460H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAC70H3">
STAD29H3:  Statistics for Life &amp; Social Scientists<p>Course Description</p><p>The course discusses many advanced statistical methods used in the life and social sciences. Emphasis is on learning how to become a critical interpreter of these methodologies while keeping mathematical requirements low. Topics covered include multiple regression, logistic regression, discriminant and cluster analysis, principal components and factor analysis.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAC32H3">STAC32H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: All C-level/300-level and D-level/400-level STA courses or equivalents except <a href="/course/STAC32H3">STAC32H3</a>, <a href="/course/STAC53H3">STAC53H3</a>, <a href="/course/STAC51H3">STAC51H3and STA322H.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAD29H3">
STAD37H3:  Multivariate Analysis<p>Course Description</p><p>Linear algebra for statistics. Multivariate distributions, the multivariate normal and some associated distribution theory. Multivariate regression analysis. Canonical correlation analysis. Principal components analysis. Factor analysis. Cluster and discriminant analysis. Multidimensional scaling. Instruction in the use of SAS.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAC67H3">STAC67H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: STA437H, (STAC42H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAD37H3">
STAD57H3:  Time Series Analysis<p>Course Description</p><p>An overview of methods and problems in the analysis of time series data. Topics covered include descriptive methods, filtering and smoothing time series, identification and estimation of times series models, forecasting, seasonal adjustment, spectral estimation and GARCH models for volatility.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAC62H3">STAC62H3and <a href="/course/STAC67H3">STAC67H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: STA457H, (STAC57H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAD57H3">
STAD68H3:  Advanced Machine Learning and Data Mining<p>Course Description</p><p>Statistical aspects of supervised learning: regression, regularization methods, parametric and nonparametric classification methods, including Gaussian processes for regression and support vector machines for classification, model averaging, model selection, and mixture models for unsupervised learning. Some advanced methods will include Bayesian networks and graphical models.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/CSCC11H3">CSCC11H3and <a href="/course/STAC58H3">STAC58H3and <a href="/course/STAC67H3">STAC67H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAD68H3">
STAD70H3:  Statistics and Finance II<p>Course Description</p><p>A survey of statistical techniques used in finance. Topics include mean-variance and multi-factor analysis, simulation methods for option pricing, Value-at-Risk and related risk-management methods, and statistical arbitrage. A computer package will be used to illustrate the techniques using real financial data.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAC70H3">STAC70H3and <a href="/course/STAD37H3">STAD37H3</a><p>Corequisite</p>Corequisite: <a href="/course/STAD57H3">STAD57H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAD70H3">
STAD78H3:  Machine Learning Theory<p>Course Description</p><p><span><span>Presents theoretical foundations of machine learning. Risk, empirical risk minimization, PAC learnability and its generalizations, uniform convergence, VC dimension, structural risk minimization, regularization, linear models and their generalizations, ensemble methods, stochastic gradient descent, stability, online learning.</span></span></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAB57H3">STAB57H3and <a href="/course/STAC62H3">STAC62H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-recommended-preparation"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-recommended-preparation">Recommended Preparation: <a href="/course/STAC58H3">STAC58H3and <a href="/course/STAC67H3">STAC67H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAD78H3">
STAD80H3:  Analysis of Big Data<p>Course Description</p><p>Big data is transforming our world, revolutionizing operations and analytics everywhere, from financial engineering to biomedical sciences. Big data sets include data with high-dimensional features and massive sample size. This course introduces the statistical principles and computational tools for analyzing big data: the process of acquiring and processing large datasets to find hidden patterns and gain better understanding and prediction, and of communicating the obtained results for maximal impact. Topics include optimization algorithms, inferential analysis, predictive analysis, and exploratory analysis.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAC58H3">STAC58H3and <a href="/course/STAC67H3">STAC67H3and <a href="/course/CSCC11H3">CSCC11H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAD80H3">
STAD81H3:  Causal Inference<p>Course Description</p><p>Correlation does not imply causation. Then, how can we make causal claims? To answer this question, this course introduces theoretical foundations and modern statistical and graphical tools for making causal inference. Topics include potential outcomes and counterfactuals, measures of treatment effects, causal graphical models, confounding adjustment, instrumental variables, principal stratification, mediation and interference.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/STAC50H3">STAC50H3and <a href="/course/STAC58H3">STAC58H3and <a href="/course/STAC67H3">STAC67H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Quantitative Reasoning</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/STAD81H3">

THRA10H3:  Introduction to Theatre<p>Course Description</p><p>A general introduction to theatre as a social institution and collaborative performing art. Through a combination of lectures, discussions, class exercises, and excursions to see theatre together throughout Toronto, this course will investigate why and how people commit their lives to make theatre. It will also orient students to the four areas of focus in the Theatre and Performance program's curriculum, providing a background for further theatre studies.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (VPDA10H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/THRA10H3">
THRA11H3:  Introduction to Performance<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the actor’s craft. This course provides an experiential study of the basic physical, vocal, psychological and analytical tools of the actor/performer, through a series of group and individual exercises.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/THRA10H3">THRA10H3</a>/(VPDA10H3)<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (VPDA11H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/THRA11H3">
THRB20H3:  Wrestling with the Western Canon<p>Course Description</p><p>This course challenges students to "wrestle" with the Western canon that has dominated the practice of theatre-making in colonized North America. In wrestling with it, students will become more conversant in its forms and norms, and thus better able to enter into dialogue with other theatre practitioners and scholars. They also learn to probe and challenge dominant practices, locating them within the cultural spheres and power structures that led to their initial development.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (VPDB10H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/THRB20H3">
THRB21H3:  Intercultural and Global Theatre<p>Course Description</p><p>Intercultural &amp; Global Theatre will be a study of theatre and performance as a forum for cultural representation past and present. Students will think together about some thorny issues of intercultural encounter and emerge with a fuller understanding of the importance of context and audience in interpreting performances that are more likely than ever to travel beyond the place they were created.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (VPDB11H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/THRB21H3">
THRB22H3:  Theatre in Canada<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores the history of performance on this part of Turtle Island as a way of reimagining its future. Through a series of case studies, students will grow their understanding of theatre a powerful arena for both shoring up and dismantling myths of the "imagined nation" of Canada. With a special focus on Indigenous-settler relations and the contributions of immigrant communities to diversifying the stories and aesthetics of the stage, the course will reveal theatre as an excellent forum for reckoning with the past and re-storying our shared future.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (VPDB13H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/THRB22H3">
THRB30H3:  Intermediate Performance: Scene Study<p>Course Description</p><p>By performing characters and staging scenes in scripted plays, students in this course develop and hone the physical, psychological, analytical, and vocal skills of actors. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/THRA11H3">THRA11H3</a>/(VPDA11H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/THRB30H3">
<br /><h3>THRB31H3:  Intermediate Performance: Devising Theatre<p>Course Description</p><p>This course engages students in an experiential study of devised theatre, a contemporary practice wherein a creative team (including actors, designers, writers, dramaturgs, and often a director) collaboratively create an original performance without a preexisting script. We will explore how an ensemble uses improvisation, self-scripted vignettes, movement/dance, and found materials to create an original piece of theatre. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/THRA11H3">THRA11H3</a>/(VPDA11H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/THRB31H3">
<br /><h3>THRB32H3:  Intermediate Performance: Improvisation<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to improvisation across a range of theatrical contexts. In a sequence of short units, the course will explore improv comedy, improvisation-based devising work, and the improvisation structures commonly used in the context of applied theatre work (including forum theatre and playback theatre). Simultaneously, students will read scholarly literature that addresses the ethical dilemmas, cultural collisions, and practical conundrums raised by these forms. Students will reflect on their own experiences as improvisers through the vocabulary that has been developed in this literature.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/THRA11H3">THRA11H3</a>/(VPDA11H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/THRB32H3">
<br /><h3>THRB41H3:  Theatre-Making with Communities: A Survey<p>Course Description</p><p>Students will study a wide range of "applied theatre" practice, which might include community-based theatre, prison theatre, Theatre for Development (TfD), Theatre of the Oppressed (TO), and Creative Drama in Classrooms. They will grow as both scholars and practitioners of this work, and will emerge as better able to think through the practical and ethical challenges of facilitating this work. Case studies will reflect the diversity of global practices and the importance of doing this work with marginalized groups.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/THRA10H3">THRA10H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/THRB41H3">
<br /><h3>THRB50H3:  Stagecraft<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the elements of technical theatre production. <br /><h3>THRC20H3:  Theatre and Social Justice<p>Course Description</p><p>This course invites students to consider how theatre can help to close the gap between the just world we envision and the inequitable world we inhabit. Case studies illuminate the challenges that theatre-makers face when confronting injustice, the strategies they pursue, and the impact of their work on their audiences and the larger society.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (VPDC13H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language<p>Note</p>Note: Enrolment priority is given to students enrolled in either Major or Minor program in Theatre and Performance</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/THRC20H3">
<br /><h3>THRC21H3:  Reimagining Theatre Criticism&nbsp;<p>Course Description</p><p>This course immerses students in the local theatre scene, taking them to 4-5 productions over the term. We study the performances themselves and the art of responding to live performances as theatre critics. We position theatre criticism as evolving in the increasingly digital public sphere, and as a potential tool for advocates of antiracist, decolonial, feminist, and queer cultural work.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/THRA10H3">THRA10H3and one of [<a href="/course/THRB20H3">THRB20H3</a>, <a href="/course/THRB21H3">THRB21H3</a>, or <a href="/course/THRB22H3">THRB22H3</a>]<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/THRB40H3">THRB40H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: Partnership-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/THRC21H3">

THRC40H3:  Performance and Activism<p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to the principles and creative processes associated with Theatre of the Oppressed – a movement blending activism and artistry to advance progressive causes. Students train as Theatre of the Oppressed performers and facilitators, and through a combination of lectures, readings, discussions, and field trips, they process the history, ideology, and debates associated with this movement. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/THRA10H3">THRA10H3</a>/(VPDA10H3)</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/THRC40H3">
THRC41H3:  Theatre in Education <p>Course Description</p><p>This course introduces students to the principles and creative processes of integrating theatre into K-12 classrooms and other learning environments. Lectures, readings, discussions, and field trips complement active experimentation as students learn the pedagogical value of this active, creative, imaginative, kinesthetic approach to education. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/THRA10H3">THRA10H3</a>/(VPDA10H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/THRC41H3">

VPAA10H3:  Introduction to Arts and Media Management<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the theories and practices of arts and media management within the not-for-profit, public, and social enterprise sectors. It is a general survey course that introduces the broad context of arts and media management in Canadian society and the kinds of original research skills needed for the creative and administrative issues currently faced by the arts and media community.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-breadth-requirements"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-breadth-requirements">Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPAA10H3">

VPAB10H3:  Equity and Inclusivity in Arts and Media Organizations<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to equity, inclusivity and diversity as it relates to organizational development and cultural policymaking in arts and media management. This course will take students through an overview of critical theories of systemic power and privilege, including those of race, gender, socio-economic status, sexual orientation or identity, age, ability/disability and religion and examine how these impact varied creative working environments and institutions.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/VPAA10H3">VPAA10H3and <a href="/course/VPAA12H3">VPAA12H3</a></span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPAB10H3">

VPAB18H3:  Becoming a Producer<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to the producing functions in the arts and in media management. The course will cover the genesis of creative and managing producers in arts and media, and what it is to be a producer today for internet, television, radio and some music industry and social media environments or for arts and media creative hubs, or for non-profit performing and multi-disciplinary theatres in Canada that feature touring artists. Includes individual and group skill-building in sector research to develop and present creative pitch packages and/or touring plans.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/VPAA10H3">VPAA10H3and <a href="/course/VPAA12H3">VPAA12H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPAB18H3">
VPAC13H3:  Planning and Project Management in the Arts and Cultural Sector<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is designed to provide a foundation for project management and strategic planning knowledge and skills. Topics such as project and event management as well as strategic and business planning include how to understand organizational resource-management and consultative processes, contexts, and impacts, will be discussed and practiced through group and individual assignments.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits including [<a href="/course/VPAB13H3">VPAB13H3and <a href="/course/VPAB16H3">VPAB16H3</a>]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPAC13H3">
VPAC15H3:  Cultural Policy<p>Course Description</p><p>A survey of the principles, structures, and patterns of cultural policy and how these impact arts and media funding structures in Canada, nationally and internationally. Through original research including interviews in the sector, group and individual assignments will explore a wide range of cultural policy issues, processes, and theoretical commitments underpinning the subsidized arts, commercial and public media industries, and hybrid cultural enterprises, critically exploring the role of advocacy and the strengths and weaknesses of particular policy approaches.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: [8.0 credits, including <a href="/course/VPAA10H3">VPAA10H3and <a href="/course/VPAA12H3">VPAA12H3</a>] or [8.0 credits, including: <a href="/course/SOCB58H3">SOCB58H3and registration in the Minor Program in Culture, Creativity, and Cities]<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPAC15H3">
VPAC16H3:  Contracts and Copyright <p>Course Description</p><p>A study of essential legal and practical issues relevant to the arts and media workplace, with a particular focus on contracts, contract negotiation, and copyright. </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits including <a href="/course/VPAA10H3">VPAA10H3and <a href="/course/VPAA12H3">VPAA12H3and <a href="/course/VPAB16H3">VPAB16H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPAC16H3">
VPAC17H3:  Marketing in the Arts and Media <p>Course Description</p><p>An advanced study of marketing in the arts and media sectors. Through group and individual assignments including the development of a marketing and promotions plan, this course facilitates a sophisticated understanding of the knowledge and skills required for arts and media managers to be responsive to varied market groups and changing market environments and successfully bring creative and cultural production and audiences together.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits including <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/VPAA10H3">VPAA10H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/VPAA12H3">VPAA12H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/VPAB16H3">VPAB16H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPAC17H3">
VPAC18H3:  Raising Funds in Arts and Media  <p>Course Description</p><p>An advanced study of fundraising and resource development in the arts and media sector. This course facilitates a sophisticated understanding of knowledge and skills required for arts and media managers to develop varied revenue streams, including grant writing, media funding, and contributed revenue strategies to support artistic missions. Through group and individual assignments, the course culminates in pitch packages or grant applications for real-life programs including creative briefs, budgets, financing plans, and timelines</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 8.0 credits including <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/VPAB13H3">VPAB13H3and <a href="https://utsc.calendar.utoronto.ca/course/&lt;a href='/course/VPAB16H3'&gt;VPAB16H3&lt;/a&gt;"><a href="/course/VPAB16H3">VPAB16H3</a>&nbsp;</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPAC18H3">

VPAD10H3:  Good, Better, Best: Case Study Senior Seminar <p>Course Description</p><p>This course will prepare students for the realities of working in and leading arts and media organizations by challenging them with real-world problems via case studies and simulations. Through individual and group assignments involving research in the field that culminates in an original case presentation and report, students will consider, compare, explain, and defend decisions and actions in real-life organizations to develop their ability to demonstrate effective and ethical approaches to arts and media management.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: At least 14.0 credits, including 1.0 credit at the C-level in VPA courses.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Preference is given to students enrolled in the Major programs in Arts &amp; Media Management. This course requires ancillary fees (case study fees)</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPAD10H3">
VPAD11H3:  Focus on the Field: Senior Research Seminar<p>Course Description</p><p>Are you interested in researching hands-on professional practice to synthesize and apply the theory-based learning you have undertaken in arts and media management about how people and organizations work in the culture sector and media industries? In this course, you will propose your own research project to examine how a specific creative business (such as a creative hub, media company or performing or visual arts organization) and its related practices operate, exploring specific areas of arts or media management practice, theory, history or emergent issues. While the creative ecosystem is made up of a broad and sometimes baffling array of for-profit, non-profit and hybrid ways of doing things, this course will provide insights into an organization of your choice.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: At least 14.0 full credits, including 1.0 full credit at the C level in VPA courses.</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPAD11H3">
VPAD12H3:  Senior Collaborative Projects <p>Course Description</p><p>This course is an intensive synthesis and application of prior learning through collaborative project-based practice. Students will lead and actively contribute to one or more major initiative(s) that will allow them to apply the principles and employ the practices of effective arts and media management.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: At least 14.0 credits including <a href="/course/VPAC13H3">VPAC13H3</a>.</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience<p>Note</p>Note: Restricted to students enrolled in the Specialist Program in Arts Management.</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPAD12H3">

VPHA46H3:  Ways of Seeing: Introduction to Art Histories<p>Course Description</p><p>How and why are objects defined as Art? How do these definitions vary across cultures and time periods? Studying different approaches to writing art history and considering a wide range of media from photography to printmaking and installation arts.</p></div></div><span class="views-field views-field-field-exclusion"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-exclusion">Exclusion: (FAH100Y), FAH101H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHA46H3">
VPHB39H3:  Ten Key Words in Art History: Unpacking Methodology<p>Course Description</p><p>Key concepts in art history, including intention, meaning, style, materiality, identity, production, reception, gender, visuality, and history. Students will explore critical questions such as whether and how to read artist's biographies into their art. This course helps students understand the discipline and develops critical thinking and research skills required in advanced courses.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/VPHA46H3">VPHA46H3or (ACMA01H3)<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FAH102H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHB39H3">
VPHB40H3:  Exhibiting Art<p>Course Description</p><p>This course offers a critical look at ways of exhibiting art, including exploring the exhibitions and collection of the Doris McCarthy Gallery and the public sculptures located on campus. Through readings, discussions and site visits we will consider the nature of exhibitions, their audiences and current practices juxtaposed with investigations of the history and practice of display.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/VPHA46H3">VPHA46H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/VPSB73H3">VPSB73H3</a>, (VPHB71H3), FAH310H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHB40H3">
VPHB50H3:  Africa Through the Photographic Lens<p>Course Description</p><p>The centrality of photographic practice to African cultures and histories from the period of European imperialism, the rise of modernist "primitivism" and the birth of ethnology and anthropology to contemporary African artists living on the continent and abroad.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/VPHA46H3">VPHA46H3or (ACMA01H3) or <a href="/course/AFSA01H3">AFSA01H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHB50H3">
VPHB53H3:  Medieval Art<p>Course Description</p><p>The origins of European artistic traditions in the early Christian, Mediterranean world; how these traditions were influenced by classical, Byzantine, Moslem and pagan forms; how they developed in an entirely new form of artistic expression in the high Middle Ages; and how they led on to the Renaissance.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/VPHA46H3">VPHA46H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FAH215H, FAH216H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHB53H3">
VPHB58H3:  Modern Art and Culture<p>Course Description</p><p>A study of nineteenth and twentieth-century arts and visual media, across genres and cultures. What did modernity mean in different cultural contexts? How is 'modern' art or 'modernism' defined? How did the dynamic cultural, economic, and socio-political shifts of the globalizing and industrializing modern world affect the visual ars and their framing?</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/VPHA46H3">VPHA46H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FAH245H, FAH246H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHB58H3">
VPHB59H3:  Current Art Practices<p>Course Description</p><p>Shifts in theory and practice in art of the past fifty years. Studying selected artists' works from around the world, we explore how notions of modern art gave way to new ideas about media, patterns of practice, and the relations of art and artists to the public, to their institutional contexts, and to globalized cultures.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/VPHA46H3">VPHA46H3or <a href="/course/VPHB39H3">VPHB39H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FAH245H, FAH246H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHB59H3">
VPHB63H3:  Fame, Spectacle and Glory: Objects of the Italian Renaissance<p>Course Description</p><p>This course is an introduction to art and visual culture produced in Italy ca. 1350-1550. Students will explore new artistic media and techniques, along with critical issues of social, cultural, intellectual, theoretical and religious contexts that shaped the form and function of art made during this era.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/VPHA46H3">VPHA46H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FAH230H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHB63H3">

VPHB68H3:  Art and the Everyday: Mass Culture and the Visual Arts<p>Course Description</p><p>This course explores the relationship between visuality and practices of everyday life. VPHB78H3:  Our Town, Our Art: Local Collections I<p>Course Description</p><p>Local arts institutions are often taken for granted but understanding how and why collections are formed, why they are significant, and how they relate to larger art historical contexts provides important object-based learning opportunities. VPHC41H3:  Carolingian and Romanesque Art<p>Course Description</p><p>Major artistic and architectural monuments of Europe from the Carolingian renaissance to the renaissance of the twelfth century, considered in relation to geographical context, to monasticism and pilgrimage, to artistic developments of the contemporary Mediterranean world, and to the art and architecture of the later Roman Empire, Byzantium and Armenia, Islam and the art of the invasion period.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/VPHB53H3">VPHB53H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (VPHB42H3), FAH215H<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHC41H3">
VPHC42H3:  Gothic Art and Architecture<p>Course Description</p><p>Current scholarship is expanding and challenging how we decide "what is Gothic?" We will examine a variety of artworks, considering artistic culture, social, cultural, and physical contexts as well. Style, techniques, patronage, location in time and space, and importance of decoration (sculpture, stained glass, painting, tapestry) will be among topics discussed.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/VPHB53H3">VPHB53H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: FAH328H, FAH351H5, (FAH369H)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHC42H3">

VPHC53H3:  The Silk Routes<p>Course Description</p><p>The Silk Routes were a lacing of highways connecting Central, South and East Asia and Europe. Utilizing the Royal Ontario Museum's collections, classes held at the Museum and U of T Scarborough will focus on the art produced along the Silk Routes in 7th to 9th century Afghanistan, India, China and the Taklamakhan regions.<br> Same as <a href="/course/GASC53H3">GASC53H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credit in art history or in Asian or medieval European history.<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/GASC53H3">GASC53H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHC53H3">
VPHC54H3:  Art Writing<p>Course Description</p><p>Art criticism as a complex set of practices performed not only by critics, art historians, curators and the like, but also by artists (and collectors). The traditional role of art critics in the shaping of an art world, and the parallel roles played by other forms of writing about art and culture (from anthropology, sociology, film studies). </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 2.0  credits at the B-level in VPA, VPH, and/or VPS courses.<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHC54H3">
VPHC63H3:  Explorations in Early Modern Art<p>Course Description</p><p>This seminar-format course will offer students the opportunity to investigate critical theories and methodologies of the early modern period (roughly 1400-1700). Focusing on such topics as a single artist, artwork or theme, students will become immersed in an interdisciplinary study that draws on impressive local materials from public museum and library collections.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/VPHA46H3">VPHA46H3and [one of <a href="/course/VPHB63H3">VPHB63H3or <a href="/course/VPHB64H3">VPHB64H3or <a href="/course/VPHB74H3">VPHB74H3</a>].<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><br><span class="views-field views-field-field-course-experience"><strong class="views-label views-label-field-course-experience">Course Experience: University-Based Experience</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHC63H3">
VPHC68H3:  Art in Global Cities<p>Course Description</p><p>This course looks at the global city as a hub for the creation of visual, performing arts and architecture. How have cyberspace and increased transnational flows of art and artists changed the dynamic surrounding urban arts? What are the differences between the arts within the modern and global contemporary city? </p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: <a href="/course/VPHB58H3">VPHB58H3or <a href="/course/VPHB59H3">VPHB59H3</a><p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (VPHC52H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHC68H3">
VPHC72H3:  Art and Visual Culture in Spaces, Places, and Institutions<p>Course Description</p><p>Art and the settings in which it is seen in cities today. VPHC73H3:  Home, Away, and In Between: Diaspora and Visual Culture<p>Course Description</p><p>This course considers representations of diaspora, migration, displacement, and placemaking within visual culture. We will employ a comparative, cross-cultural approach and a historical perspective, to consider how artists, theorists, media, and social institutions think about and visualize these widely-held experiences that increasingly characterize our cosmopolitan, interconnected world.</p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 1.0 credits at VPHB-level<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: (VPAB09H3)<p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Social and Behavioural Sciences</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHC73H3">
VPHC74H3:  A Tale of Three Cities: Introduction to Contemporary Art in China<p>Course Description</p><p>An introduction to Chinese contemporary art focusing on three cities: Beijing, Shanghai, and Guangzhou. Increasing globalization and China's persistent self-renovation has brought radical changes to cities, a subject of fascination for contemporary artists. The art works will be analyzed in relation to critical issues such as globalization and urban change.<br> Same as <a href="/course/GASC74H3">GASC74H3</a></p><p>Prerequisite</p>Prerequisite: 2.0 credits at the B-level in Art History, Asian History, and/or Global Asia Studies courses, including at least 0.5 credit from the following: <a href="/course/VPHB39H3">VPHB39H3</a>, <a href="/course/VPHB73H3">VPHB73H3</a>, <a href="/course/HISB58H3">HISB58H3</a>, (GASB31H3), <a href="/course/GASB33H3">GASB33H3</a>, or (GASB35H3).<p>Exclusion</p>Exclusion: <a href="/course/GASC74H3">GASC74H3</a><p>Breadth Requirements</p>Breadth Requirements: Arts, Literature and Language</span></span><div class="views-field views-field-field-timetable-link"><div><a href=" https://ttb.utoronto.ca/cp/VPHC74H3">
